_(thread depth: 4)_

- **Date:** 2006-04-01T08:12:28+00:00

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message 
> news:t4LWf.42385$bn3.21269@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Found by googling for "cics csd file", on an ibm web site:

*** begin quote ***

CSD file management
The CICSï¿½ system definition (CSD) file is a VSAM data set containing a
resource definition record for every resource defined to CICS by means 
of CEDA or DFHCSDUP. It can be defined as recoverable, so that changes 
made  by CEDA or CEDB that were incomplete when an abend occurred, are 
backed out.

*** end quote ***

DFHCSDUP is a batch program that can process CEDA commands.  It is 
often used to generate reports from the CSD file.  But it can also be 
used in a production batch job to apply CSD updates.  That can be 
useful for audit purposes.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS DOCUMENT CREATE storage?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T05:37:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wUmZf.30306$Y53.21624@fe07.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1143564941.574464.155400@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
From my "usually reliable sources" (at IBM),

"Hi Bill,

The developer for this area has passed on some information that might be useful 
for you. A customer raised a service request recently about this. As a result of 
this there was an APAR PK17576 CICS GOES SOS IN ECDSA WHEN CREATING LARGE WEB 
DOCUMENTS.

The APAR explains what's happening - multiple copies of the document are being 
held:

[For sending a CICS document using CICS Web support] there will ... be 5 large 
storage users.
2 copies in document handler using DHDDB storage, 1 copy in TS using TSMAIN, 1 
copy in a temporary buffer in WBGENRAL, and 1 buffer to hold the completed HTTP 
response.
.
The HTTP response is in user key storage because the server program runs in user 
key. This will be the storage increase seen in EUDSA. The other 4 areas will all 
be in ECDSA.

The APAR reduces the copies of the document that are held. Unfortunately I see 
the only doc update requested was for the Data Areas manuals! I've made a note 
to improve the documentation about this.

Hope that helps,"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS DOCUMENT CREATE storage?

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1143894640.570569.316950@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1143851645.245065.123970@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1143666113.411117.288840@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1143766380.907869.188340@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1143848086.138829.199390@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1143851645.245065.123970@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com>`

```
Thanks, Richard.

Now to all of those who interested how to compile OpenCOBOL on Windows
platforms.

Here what I've done.

1) Extracted OC to c:\opencobol directory
2) Copied latest version of GMP to c:\opencobol\gmp
3) Copied Libtools to c:\opencobol\libtool dir
4) I use MSYS with MINGW, so I execute the msys in order to get the
'Linux' shell, and cd /c/opencobol/gmp
5) Writing (a) ./configuire (b) make (c) make install
6) Doing the same for Libtool
7) Entering cd /c/opencobol. There are parameters which need to be
specified for ./configure like this:
./configure CPPFLAGS=-I/c/opencobol/include LDFLAGS=-L/c/opencobol/lib

But only here I get the problem:

after successfully configuring the other things I come to this:

checking how to hardcode library paths into programs... immediate
checking whether stripping libraries is possible... yes
cat: write error: Invalid request code
sed: read error on {Unknown file pointer}: Invalid request code
configure: error: unable to update list of available tagged
configurations.

So if anyone has any idea, please post.
Meanwhile I'll try other things :)

Thank you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IF statement, I don't understand this

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-04-03T15:43:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0rfqk0skh@news1.newsguy.com>`

```

In article <1143920554.267190.133090@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>, "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> writes:
> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> so they are not a very reliable medium for data transfer.

Fortunately, TCP doesn't require a reliable transport; indeed that was
one of its primary design goals.

The real problem with TCP/CP is that because latency is so high you
have to set the retransmit timer very high to avoid unnecessary (and
expensive) resends, so it's not suitable for interactive use.

A similar problem in applying pigeon technology to remote data sensing
is documented at

   http://observe.arc.nasa.gov/nasa/exhibits/history/history_4.html

(The pigeon in the photography should clearly be wearing a Hawaiian-
print shirt - an incomprehensible omission on the part of the designer.)
```

```
The only language I can think of is SQL, although that's a different animal 
(declarative language vs. procedural like Cobol).

In SQL you can do:

select * from table_name
         where ship_code = 5 and i_dir in ("L","R","S")

This would evaluate as the original poster (incorrectly) expected Cobol to 
do it.

#### ↳ Re: IF statement, I don't understand this

- **From:** "RR" <RR@AOL.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T09:55:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NpWdnUOgsrVoFa_ZRVn-jg@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<2akn2295mqoqg2hok7b2vs96okhdug438a@4ax.com>`

```
Hello, Jack!
You wrote  on Thu, 30 Mar 2006 06:40:48 -0600:

 JB> This is Unisys A-series C74,
 JB> I have
 JB> 000600 01  SHH-SHIP-CODE               PIC 9 VALUE 1.
 JB> 000700 01  ASM010-I-DIR                PIC X VALUE "R".
 JB> and
 JB> 001000  IF  SHH-SHIP-CODE = 5  AND  ASM010-I-DIR = "L" OR "R" OR "S"
 JB> 001100         DISPLAY "TRUE"
 JB> 001200     ELSE
 JB> 001300         DISPLAY "FALSE".
 JB> 001400     ENDIF.

 JB> It comes up "TRUE" and I don't understand why.  It doesn't seem right.

"And" and "Or" are interpreted equally left to right, so anytime 
ASM010-I-DIR = "R" or "S" you will display "TRUE".  I believe you want it 
coded like this, with parenthesis:

 IF SHH-SHIP-CODE = 5  AND  (ASM010-I-DIR = "L" OR "R" OR "S")

With best regards, RR.
```

#### ↳ Re: IF statement, I don't understand this

- **From:** Last Boy Scout <eggbtr@ezl.com>

```
Jack Benny wrote:
> This is Unisys A-series C74,
> I have 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
What are the orders of operations for your compiler?

You have IF AND OR OR OR  How can any of us tell what you are trying to 
test for?  Your compiler is using some logic to determine it.  Try Using 
some parentheses.  Compound statements can be confusing when using if 
and or or or.  Do you even know what your compiler will evaluate first?
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-03T08:34:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET...

> Omnipotent doesn't equal rude.  In Revelation 3:20, God says "Behold, I 
> stand at the door, and knock: if any man hear my voice, and open the door, 
> I will come in to him, and will sup with him, and he with me."

Interesting passage, that.  I don't see very many qualifications -- words 
like "unless" or "until", for example, in it.  What does one have to do 
besides open the door in response?

And that brings up a point.  Whenever I see a passage directed to a 
particular group, I have to wonder why it is directed specifically to that 
group and not to the world at large.   Should that which is divinely 
directed explicitly to the Laodiceans be taken to heart with no less, or 
even more, fervor by the Philadelphians, or the Ephesians, or for that 
matter by the Anaheimians or the Montgomerians?    On what scriptural basis?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-03T21:35:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> matter by the Anaheimians or the Montgomerians?    On what scriptural basis?

While I agree in principle, there are a couple of reasons I think this 
particular one applies to all...

  - This is in the New Testament.  Generally, most precepts given in the 
NT are applicable to everyone.  Are we to ignore "Love thy neighbor as 
thyself" because Jesus said it to a crowd in Israel, and that's 
obviously not us?  :)

  - The letters to the seven churches (from which that verse was taken) 
dealt with a particular problem in each church.  Rather than only being 
good for that geographical region, I rather believe that they are good 
principles for dealing with the problems they were meant to address.

Also, the "world at large" is the target audience of the Bible.  Some 
things in it are recorded simply to pass along history - I don't think 
God needs any of us to stand between two pillars and push the way Samson 
did.  Others give us general precepts for living - I'm thinking here 
about Proverbs (among other passages).

When Jesus forgave prostitution and adultery, this gives us an example 
to follow (which is also applicable to WBC).  When He condemned those 
who were preying on worshippers, we can get an insight into how God 
feels about those who are supposed to lead His people, but lead them 
astray.  When Paul told the Thessalonians to "rejoice evermore" and 
"recompense no man evil for evil", I don't think that those things are 
limited to the Thessalonica city limits.

As far as a Scriptural basis, as Paul wrote to Timothy, "All scripture 
is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for 
reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness..."  (2 
Timothy 3:16)  Why would God have preserved these instructions if they 
were only directed at a few thousand people?  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T10:21:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
>> news:636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET...
>>

>  - This is in the New Testament.  Generally, most precepts given in the NT 
> are applicable to everyone.  Are we to ignore "Love thy neighbor as 
> thyself" because Jesus said it to a crowd in Israel, and that's obviously 
> not us?  :)

What Jesus said to a crowd in Israel is a different context from the eight 
letters (one to Asia in general and seven to individual groups in Asia) that 
*explicitly* specify and address their intented *and limited* audiences.  It 
is one thing to say "hey, folks, anybody who's listening", and another to 
say "Hey, Laodiceans! Listen up! This is for you!".

>  - The letters to the seven churches (from which that verse was taken) 
> dealt with a particular problem in each church.  Rather than only being 
> good for that geographical region, I rather believe that they are good 
> principles for dealing with the problems they were meant to address.

Well, that's kinda my point.  How do *you* know that *my* failings are those 
addressed by this passage to the Laodiceans?   I am fully capable of beating 
myself all the way to suicide if I spend all my time concentrating on how 
every single misstep on my part is driving yet another thorn into the head 
of the poor bleeding Jesus, represents yet another hammer-blow on the nail 
piercing His wrist, or is analogous to yet another taunt from the onlookers 
at the crucifixion, as I was brought up to believe.  I regard that tendency 
now as a *failing* on my part, however well-meaning the folks were who 
taught me to do that, and I just don't go there anymore.

> Also, the "world at large" is the target audience of the Bible.  Some 
> things in it are recorded simply to pass along history - I don't think God 
> needs any of us to stand between two pillars and push the way Samson did. 
> Others give us general precepts for living - I'm thinking here about 
> Proverbs (among other passages).

Well, you and I disagree about the monolithic nature of the Bible.

I believe the New Testament is a collection of books assembled over a period 
of four or five centuries.  Some of these books were included because 
Everybody Knew they were of apostolic authorship -- which underlying 
principle was the primary reason they were included.  Even Martin Luther 
regarded some of them as spurious.  Eusebius' History discusses the 
development of the New Testament Canon at some length.

More obviously controversial today is the Old Testament, in which the 
Orthodox and Catholic canon has more books (evolving from the Septuagint in 
the Alexandrian tradition) than the Protestant (evolving after Luther's 
rejection of the Apocrypha from the Jewish canon of the Masoretes out of the 
Palestinian tradition).

The premise that the Apostles decided the contents of the Bible, Old and New 
Testament, as we now have it, is quite simply not supported by the 
historical record.

> As far as a Scriptural basis, as Paul wrote to Timothy,

... direct authorship is in significant scholarly dispute ...

>  "All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for 
> doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in 
> righteousness..."  (2 Timothy 3:16)  Why would God have preserved these 
> instructions if they were only directed at a few thousand people?  :)

Presuming Paul wrote this, what does "pasa graphe theopneustos" actually 
*mean*?  Is he referring to "all inspired-of-God scripture is ... " or "all 
scripture is inspired of God, is ... "?  There's no explicit verb in the 
sentence and there are two equally-reasonable places to presume an elided 
"estin".

And did Paul really mean "God-breathed", that every single letter of every 
single word of the passages being referred to was dictated directly by God? 
He seems to have invented the word "theopneustos", so it's tough to figure 
out exactly what he meant by it (particularly since he wasn't exactly fluent 
in Greek, based on evidence in the indisputably-Pauline epistles).

And as to "graphe"  (="writing"), I think the big question is what's he 
referring to?   If Paul himself wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been written 
before his execution under Nero, whose reign ended in 67AD.  From what I 
read,  the earliest plausible dates for the Gospels are:  Matthew, after 
70AD and probably about 85AD; Mark, around 70AD; Luke/Acts, about 75AD, and 
John, 90-100AD.   If Paul wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been before his 
martyrdom.  He thus would have had to foresee the acceptance of four, only 
four, and exactly four Gospels as deserving of being considered "scripture" 
*that had not yet even been written*!  He would also be placing himself in 
the position of saying "Hey!  Everything *I've* written deserves to be 
treated as DIVINELY-INSPIRED SCRIPTURE!!!! FOREVER!!!" were his intent to 
include what we now know as the New Testament as part of this reference.

I don't think that these are supportable suppositions.

More likely, since he was someone with a strong Jewish background, I believe 
that to which he was referring was that which was accepted as "scripture" 
*at the time*.  The distribution and collection of Paul's letters occurred 
in the century after his death, and the solidification of the New Testament 
canon occurred three centuries and more after that.  That to which "pasa 
graphe" refers thus has to be, taken in its broadest possible 
interpretation, the Old Testament.   Not the New.  It didn't exist as a 
unit, and much of it didn't exist at all, when this part of 2 Timothy was 
written, if indeed Paul wrote it.   And Jesus' own words support this in 
Luke 24:44-45:  "the law of Moses, and in the prophets, and in the psalms 
... the scriptures ..." (the last referring to the first three).

That I find a passage in the New Testament useful does not have anything to 
do with whether I think "pasa graphe theopneustos" in 2 Timothy refers to 
anything written after, say, 49AD.  I don't, and that includes the letters 
to the Churches in Asia in Revelation.

By the way, I also believe that Revelation 22:18-19 can only be reasonably 
taken to refer to the Book of Revelation.  The word used in Greek for "book" 
here is "biblion" -- a small book, a scroll; it's a diminutive of "biblos". 
I know many Christians who believe the verse is an explicit and specific 
reference to everything from Genesis 1:1 through Revelation 22:21, but I 
think both the historical record and the direct meaning of the Greek text 
(KJV "and if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this 
prophecy ...") argues strongly against the plausibility of such a view.

And is Martin Luther's "part ... of the book of life" taken out because he 
didn't much care forHebrews, James, Jude and Revelation?

I will note that 2 Peter 3:15ff elevates the writings of Paul to the level 
of Scripture (="graphas"); the question I have is direct Petrine authorship 
of this epistle, and the consequential dating of this epistle (as well as 
the assembly of Paul's complete collected letters) as having been completed 
before 67AD.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> say "Hey, Laodiceans! Listen up! This is for you!".

Were not most epistles written to one church or person, but distributed 
around to many?  The Laodiceans had a specific problem which was given a 
specific solution.  It would be rather presumptuous of us to assume that 
if we encountered a similar problem, the solution would be different.  :)

>>  - The letters to the seven churches (from which that verse was taken) 
>> dealt with a particular problem in each church.  Rather than only being 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> addressed by this passage to the Laodiceans?

I don't, and I don't believe I've ever insinuated that you had failings 
at all.  None of this discussion has, at least on my part, been intended 
to identify failings.  This particular part of the discussion originated 
when Howard said it was mean-spirited of God to allow anyone to die if 
He doesn't wish them to.

> I am fully capable of beating 
> myself all the way to suicide if I spend all my time concentrating on how 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> taught me to do that, and I just don't go there anymore.

Well, the folks who taught you to do that missed it a bit, too.  Jesus 
would have gone through everything He did even if only one of us needed 
it.  This is the "freedom" that Paul (yes, I've read further) was 
talking about "in Christ".  What's been done to Jesus is done - no 
sinning or lack of sinning on anyone's part will change the severity of 
what has already taken place.

>> Also, the "world at large" is the target audience of the Bible.  Some 
>> things in it are recorded simply to pass along history - I don't think God 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> historical record.

I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible, and have obviously 
done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick 
away at one piece of it, its claim of infallibility then is gone.  Once 
that's gone, on what do you base your belief?

I have yet to have someone show me a discrepancy, where the Bible 
contradicts itself, that wasn't manufactured and taken out of context. 
Scientists have even found a missing day - coincidentally, around the 
time the "sun stood still" during an Old Testament battle.  Research on 
Mount Ararat, where remains of Noah's Ark have been found, has done 
nothing but prove the Biblical record.

Obviously, the Bible was written by men, and compiled by men.  Believing 
that these men allowed God to inspire them and guide their choices 
doesn't mean that it's all the work of man, and full of flaws.  God has 
preserved what He wants preserved, and I truly believe that stuff like 
"The Da Vinci Code" and "Misquoting Jesus" are serving to distract a 
large number of people, and make them question not only the pieces they 
challenge, but the Scriptures as a whole.

(Full disclosure - I have read neither book mentioned above in their 
entirety.  I have read more than one review of both books.)

>> As far as a Scriptural basis, as Paul wrote to Timothy,
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> "estin".

Either way, the effect is the same.  If it's Scripture, it's inspired of 
God.  If you have something claiming to be Scripture but not inspired of 
God, it's false.  In both cases, the profitability only applies to 
inspired Scripture.

> And did Paul really mean "God-breathed", that every single letter of every 
> single word of the passages being referred to was dictated directly by God? 
> He seems to have invented the word "theopneustos", so it's tough to figure 
> out exactly what he meant by it (particularly since he wasn't exactly fluent 
> in Greek, based on evidence in the indisputably-Pauline epistles).

Creating a word - heh!  :)  I guess that's been going on for millenia...

> And as to "graphe"  (="writing"), I think the big question is what's he 
> referring to?   If Paul himself wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been written 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't think that these are supportable suppositions.

How about the supposition that 2 Timothy 3:16 is God-breathed Scripture? 
  God said to write it, and "Paul" wrote it.  It didn't matter what was 
accepted into the canon at the time.  (In fact, "at the time", I imagine 
the church was a bit less organized than it was a few centuries later.) 
  God knew how it would be put together, and that's what He wanted written.

Is that supportable?

[snip]
> By the way, I also believe that Revelation 22:18-19 can only be reasonably 
> taken to refer to the Book of Revelation.  The word used in Greek for "book" 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> before 67AD.

With all these questions, what do you believe?  Are these deep-rooted 
questions, or are they "hey, I'm kinda curious about this - doesn't 
change my view of it, I just wonder" type of questions?

I'm just trying to figure out how one could believe the Bible without 
believing it to be the inspired, inerrant, infallible Word of God. 
Without that solid foundation, how can anything else be built?

(These questions aren't meant to imply that you don't - I'm just having 
trouble understanding...)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
> I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible,

Well, you should find out.  Most of it was argued over at Nicea in 325
when most groups agreed to disagree about what it should be (except the
jews who weren't invited).

> and have obviously
> done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick
> away at one piece of it, its claim of infallibility then is gone.
> Once that's gone, on what do you base your belief?

Hopefully you learn about the real world instead of the myths and
legends of the middle east.

If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
have seemed a reasonable belief. They didn't know any better and could
relate to what happened in the blacksmith's shop. If anyone said it
today they would be dismissed even if they claimed that meteorology was
'just a theory' and 'weathermen always got it wrong' so they should
teach 'Intelligent Weather' in meteorology classes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T03:02:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> > I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible,
>
> Well, you should find out.  Most of it was argued over at Nicea in 325
> when most groups agreed to disagree about what it should be (except the
> jews who weren't invited).

I do agree that finding out the facts before speaking is a good idea.
The story that the canon was decided at Nicaea is a myth, however.  I
researched it a while back and put or linked to all the material I
could find in the ancient sources online:

http://www.tertullian.org/rpearse/nicaea.html

The canon of the NT was more or less closed by general agreement during
the 4th century, and everything with any real claim to apostolic origin
was included.  But there is no one point at which this happened.

> > and have obviously
> > done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> legends of the middle east.

Do the irrational period values of one tiny society in one period of
history deserve such uncritical praise, or the religion of 2000 years
of humanity such stale dismissal?

In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
they happen to live?  Can we even state what we believe in terms that
make sense, if we are not Christians (or some other
ideologically-defined group)?  In my experience, we cannot.  We do not
ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

> If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
> his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> teach 'Intelligent Weather' in meteorology classes.

Are there not several strawmen in this argument?  Believing that the
theory of evolution explains how life got here is good if it is based
on rational examination.  It is bad when it is advanced merely because
those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
like Thor and his hammer so we must believe it.  It's even worse if we
find ourselves repeating such, surely?

All the best,
```

 <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote:

One does not rule out the other, last I looked.

>
>In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
>conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
>they happen to live?

I'm not certain what is being labelled 'our societies' here... and I 
barely know what *I* base my beliefs on, let alone anyone else.

>Can we even state what we believe in terms that
>make sense, if we are not Christians (or some other
>ideologically-defined group)?

Plural majestatus est.  Regarding the use of 'we' above it might be 
fruitful to consider the question posed by The Firesign Theatre of 'Who 
*am* 'us', anyhow?'.

>In my experience, we cannot.  We do not
>ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

Oh, is *that* why 'we' don't do this?  Thanks for clearing that up.

DD

Roger Pearse

<roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...
> Richard wrote:
>> > I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I found that interesting. Thanks.

> The canon of the NT was more or less closed by general agreement during
> the 4th century, and everything with any real claim to apostolic origin
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Things don't always get what they deserve.

> In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
> conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
> they happen to live?

I can't speak for others, but my world is far from unexamined.  I conform to 
the values I do because they make sense to me. (After considerable gathering 
of data and evaluation). I don't expect anyone else to do the same (although 
I'd sure be glad if they did :-)). For the most part, I'm happy if people 
grant me the same courtesy I grant them. Live and let live, I reckon...


>  Can we even state what we believe in terms that
> make sense, if we are not Christians (or some other
> ideologically-defined group)?

I certainly believe I can.

> In my experience, we cannot.  We do not
> ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.
>

I don't ask fish about water for a number of reasons. None of them is 
because fish can't see it. Besides, short of becoming a fish, how can one 
know what they see?

>> If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
>> his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> on rational examination.

All Science SHOULD be based on such a premise.


>It is bad when it is advanced merely because
> those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
> like Thor and his hammer so we must believe it.  It's even worse if we
> find ourselves repeating such, surely?
>

I don't think Evolution is advanced as a theory because the alternatives are 
just too fanciful. It may be mischievous to repeat something you know is 
spurious, unless you disclaim agreement with it and offer it purely for 
examination and debate. Putting something forward that you are not sure 
about or haven't made your mind up about, seems perfectly legitimate to me.

> All the best,
>
Likewise. Interesting points. Thanks.

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **In-Reply-To:** `<49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> I found that interesting. Thanks.

You're welcome.  There's too much assertion going around on these
things (often in good faith, of course, but not right).

> >> > and have obviously
> >> > done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Things don't always get what they deserve.

After which it seems almost a moral duty to swindle someone.

> > In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
> > conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
> > they happen to live?
>
> I can't speak for others, but my world is far from unexamined.  ...

I'm sure that we all think this, and it isn't my intention to pillory
individuals.  If you had asked any collection of Victorians, for
instance, they would have claimed that their views were the 'assured
product of scientific investigation' rather than merely a reflection of
the period in which they happened to live.

But would we now agree?  Is it not the case that everyone pretty much
lives by some subset of societal values?  I rather doubt that anyone
reading this is living by (e.g.) Victorian values, for instance.  It is
the menu of ideas that is characteristic of a period; and it changes.
That being the case, our beliefs, if derived from this purely temporary
set, must be mistaken.

> > In my experience, we cannot.  We do not
> > ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> know what they see?

I'm not sure that we're communicating here, you know.  It isn't about
fish... <grin>

> >> If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
> >> his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> All Science SHOULD be based on such a premise.

Indeed.  Believing that something is objective even more so.

> >It is bad when it is advanced merely because
> > those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> just too fanciful.

I agree.  But I'm not sure that everyone who advocates it does.  After
all, when you strip it right down, there are only 3 explanations of why
the world exists; (1) that someone made it, (2) that it made itself (3)
that it always existed.  These views are ancient, whatever new terms
may be invented for them, and are really philosophical rather than
scientific, and I can't help feeling that they will remain so until our
science has reached a final state of knowing everything about
cosmology.

> It may be mischievous to repeat something you know is
> spurious, unless you disclaim agreement with it and offer it purely for
> examination and debate. Putting something forward that you are not sure
> about or haven't made your mind up about, seems perfectly legitimate to me.

I agree entirely with this.

> Likewise. Interesting points. Thanks.

And yours.  Thank you.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

- **References:** `<636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net> <1144253470.127631.172560@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <yaSYf.27985$Ph4.6820@edtnps90> <i8s7329mmbo0gkbt407omu8u2t86indvi8@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:i8s7329mmbo0gkbt407omu8u2t86indvi8@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 05 Apr 2006 16:23:58 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3. It always existed?

    Probably (1), another bird (or pair of birds) made your bird.

    How about a star? They're formed when a group of particles happen to 
gather together in one spot in space, creating a gravitational field that 
attracts other particles, creating a stronger gravitational field, and so on 
in a snowball effect. I think that's an example of (4), a bunch of events 
occuring to cause the creation of the star with no one particular 
instigator.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T14:08:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uh9Zf.29238$Ph4.1333@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net> <1144253470.127631.172560@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <yaSYf.27985$Ph4.6820@edtnps90> <1144278416.902218.93150@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144314765.907343.250150@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com>`

```

<roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144314765.907343.250150@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com...
> Alistair wrote:
>> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Same thing, at this level.

    Arguable, but I'll let it go for now.

>
>> > and (4) the events were such that it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Same as #1.

    This I disagree with. I'll actually split (4) into two like I did with 
(1). (4a) is "Many things contributed in the creation, and there is no clear 
'primary creator'" and (4b) "It is not clear that any outside force created 
it".

    If I answer (1) or (1b), you could say "Well, whatever that someone, or 
something is, let's call it God." But if I answer (4a), then you'd have to 
say "each person or thing that contribute is one particular God". There are 
some religions like this (I believe some Native American religions believed 
that there was a holy spirit to be respected in every flower, rock, river, 
tree, animal, etc.) but I don't think this belief is compatible with 
Christianity.

    If I answer (4b), then there there isn't anything you can point to and 
say "Let's call that thing God". (4b) might occur in the case of a particle 
and its anti-particle pair coming into existence out of a vacuum. It's not 
clear that anything created the particle. It's not clear that anything 
created the anti-particle. It's not like the particle made itself, because 
if they could make themselves, you'd probably see an abundance of particles 
being created spontaneously all the time without its anti-particle pair. But 
in reality, when a particle is created, it's always with its anti-particle 
pair, so clearly the particle can't just create itself. By a symmetric 
argument, the anti-particle can't just create itself either. It just sort of 
happens.

>
>> >     (Also, by "world", do you mean planet Earth, or the known universe, 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I imagine in ancient times all these terms are more or less equivalent.

    Yes, but I'm asking what do YOU meant when you wrote it. I'm guessing 
you mean known universe.

>
>> And what about my favourite: I'm not sure that it exists?
>
> Sincerity is a pre-requisite for intelligent discussion.

    I'm not sure that Alistair's responce can be considered unreasonable (or 
"unintelligent"): At any one point in time, you cannot know for sure if the 
world you're experiencing around you is real. Whatever you're experiencing 
right now might be a hallucination, and your memories of the past which seem 
to lead to your current experiences of the present might be false memories 
created by your brain to support your belief in your current reality.

    E.g. Notice how often (but not always), when you're dreaming, you don't 
realize that you're dreaming? It's only *afterwards*, when you wake up, do 
you realize how ridiculous the "reality" of your dream was, and you can't 
understand how you could have possibly believed in it. But you did.

    So how can you know that's not what's happening right now? Hence 
Alistair's argument, how can you be sure the world around you right now 
really exists?

    - Oliver
```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1144268026.903416.198230@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>> Besides, short of becoming a fish, how can one
>> know what they see?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't think so, Richard.

The argument was: "We do not  ask a fish about water, because it can't see 
it."

It is a valid refutation of this argument to respond that nobody knows what 
a fish can see. If it were true, for example that a fish CAN see water then 
the argument is lost. (Or won, if you look at it from my point of view :-))

As the fact is that there is no definitive knowledge of exactly what a fish 
DOES see, this leaves the argument, at the very least, in doubt.

As it happens, I don't talk to fish, NOT because they can't see water. I 
wouldn't talk to them if they COULD see water :-)

I don't talk to fish because they can't understand  or respond to what I'm 
saying. That is the real point.

Pete.
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net>`

```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> >  Can we even state what we believe in terms that
> > make sense, if we are not Christians (or some other
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Perhaps we should ask a marine biologist to answer that one. Wait a
minute, I have a degree in marine biology!

To understand what a fish may see it is first necessary to understand
the structure and composition of the fish eye. Then you have to
understand the nature of the medium. Finally, you must consider the
structure and function of the fish brain. When all is said and done,
most fish can see as clearly as we can (some species have superior
visual accuity) in transparent media and can detect colours. However,
seeing is not the same as comprehension. I keep fish which seem quite
happy to swim within range of a catfish which could easily eat them;
they can see it but do not comprehend the danger it poses.

Ask a silly question, get a silly answer.    :-)
```

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144278260.782423.309640@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Here in Tauranga the local PolyTech runs one of the best Marine Biology 
courses in NZ. (We have easy access to the Pacific Ocean in both exposed and 
sheltered states.) The emphasis on this course has always been on the 
practical side, with most of the course taking place on the ocean or beaches 
and much Scuba diving involved. (One of my friends is a lecturer on this 
course and I have met a number of the students at social functiuons. They 
unanimously extol the course.) Now the PolyTech is seeking to upgrade to 
University status and so next year the syllabus will change to conform to 
the University of NZ (as practised specifically in Auckland) syllabus. This 
is much more theoretical than practical and I think it is a pity.

Where a Tauranga student would go and test fish with various colour 
detection and visual acuity practical tests, an Auckland student would 
simply dissect a fish eye and attempt to deduce the function from the form. 
:-)

Obviously, both approaches have their merits and as they are not mutually 
exclusive, you would hope that both would be applied. But the emphasis would 
depend on where you were studying...

I don't think your answer is silly, Alastair.

I think the whole analogy was off the beam (or should that be 'bream'?:-)) 
to start with...:-)

Pete.
```

On 5 Apr 2006 03:02:04 -0700, roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk wrote:

>In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
>conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

People accept those beliefs from the society they were born in.   If
they lucky enough to be born in the right society, the vast majority
who accept those beliefs will be rewarded with eternal bliss.    If
they were unlucky enough to be born in any of the other societies, the
vast majority who accept those beliefs will be punished with eternal
torture.

It's only fair and just (according to those who plan on looking smugly
down upon those being tortured).
```

Oliver Wong wrote:
> <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Two things:

1.  I have to tell people.
2.  When I do, a lot of them deny it.

But actually I think it's obvious that this is the source of our
beliefs, as soon as you think about it.  But a lot of people really do
not get that far, because it isn't explicily stated every night on TV.

>     (2) Depending on what those beliefs are, why should we do something
> about it?

Wouldn't it better to have beliefs that have some rational basis,
rather than whatever the Great and Good want us to believe?

> E.g. if the beliefs of one particular society can be summarized as
> "Everyone should be free to believe in whatever they want", should we really
> "do something" to change that?

This comment involves various beliefs on your part, which you presume
that I share.  From where do these beliefs of yours come <grin>?  You
see?

All the best,

Roger Pearse

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T11:40:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e12ums$41b$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1144253633.762187.148740@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <XcSYf.27986$Ph4.10749@edtnps90> <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
 <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote:
>Oliver Wong wrote:
>> <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>1.  I have to tell people.

Gah... with all due respect, Mr Pearse, note the use of '*we*' (emphasis 
original) above... one of the reasons why I, personally, try to avoid use 
of a first person plurals.  That you have to tell 'people' does not 
require a conclusion that you have to tell 'a certain subset of people' 
(the first-person plural group to which Mr Wong referred).

(the matter of necessity - 'I have to' - may be addressed at another time)

>2.  When I do, a lot of them deny it.

Gah again... this reminds me of an exchange I heard between two members of 
a (substance) Anonymous group regarding what is sometimes called 'casual 
use':

Person A: 'No, that's not how it is at all... you're just in denial, man.'

Person B: 'Ummmmmm... is there *any* way that I can disagree with you 
about this and *not* be 'in denial'?'

Person A: (no pause) 'No, no way, anything you say against it, it's 
denial.'

[snip]

>>     (2) Depending on what those beliefs are, why should we do something
>> about it?
>
>Wouldn't it better to have beliefs that have some rational basis,
>rather than whatever the Great and Good want us to believe?

Answering a question with a question, Mr Pearse, is no answer at all... 
but leaving that aside for a moment 'credo' is, by definition, not 
'ratio'; it may not spring from the same source and should not be subject 
to the same criteria of examination.

(hmmmmm... to take it a step further, at least according to the rules of 
English as She is Spoke... a permissible prefix for 'not' is 'ir-'; 
allowing for that substitution my assertion might be mangled into ''credo' 
is, by definition, 'irrational'')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T08:32:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e13ca6$1l5s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1144253633.762187.148740@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <XcSYf.27986$Ph4.10749@edtnps90> <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <e12ums$41b$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e12ums$41b$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,

> Gah again... this reminds me of an exchange I heard between two members of
> a (substance) Anonymous group regarding what is sometimes called 'casual
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> denial.'

Ummmmm .... when was the last time person A talked to his sponsor?  ;-)

And are you sure this is a *substance* abuse Anonymous group?  This sounds a 
lot like something I might expect to hear among relative newcomers to the 
likes of Adult Children of Alcoholics or even Al-Anon, no insult to those 
groups intended!

To be fair, I know some members of a (liquid substance) Anonymous group who 
continue to engage in "casual use" of a certain leafy substance whose use is 
decidedly frowned upon by the Federal Government, but I would have to say 
most of the people with significant time in that particular program look 
askance at the "casual" use of *any* mind-altering substances under *any* 
circumstance.

And while I know some members of a certain (decidedly illegal substance) 
Anonymous group who think they don't have a problem with liquid substances, 
the basic text (or is it Basic Text?) of the group clearly states that 
consumption of alcoholic beverages is in the same category as slamming their 
Drug of Choice.   It's my observation that those who choose to ignore that 
particular passage don't seem to be all that successful at avoiding the 
behavior they came into the program to overcome.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

>And are you sure this is a *substance* abuse Anonymous group?

No, it was not a '*substance*' (emphasis original), it was a 
'(substance)'.

>This sounds a 
>lot like something I might expect to hear among relative newcomers to the 
>likes of Adult Children of Alcoholics or even Al-Anon, no insult to those 
>groups intended!

No, it was (substance) Anonymous.

DD
```

Do you know which "Great and Good teachers" Howard is referring to 
specifically? This ties in to my concern with your other post "We have to 
change the beliefs that society is encouraging into ones that have a 
rational basis". How do you know that the specified society isn't already 
promoting beliefs that have a rational basis?

    Maybe you mean you have you change the specific society *YOU* live in; 
in which case, if you expect our help, you should at least specify which 
society that is. The societies I happen to interact with tend to promote 
rational beliefs already. The teachers I've learned from, those that I would 
consider "Great and Good" anyway, encouraged me to question their 
assertions.

    - Oliver

*anyone* should do something to force the society to change? What beliefs 
should they promote instead?

that, granted.

roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk wrote:
> Richard wrote:

> > Well, you should find out.  Most of it was argued over at Nicea in 325
> > when most groups agreed to disagree about what it should be (except the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The story that the canon was decided at Nicaea is a myth,

That is not what I stated.  I said it was argued about and disagreed.
In fact they also disagreed about dogma, what is the nature of Jesus:
was he a god in his own right, or a demigod like Hercules, or part of
the god of the jews, or just a man.

> > Hopefully you learn about the real world instead of the myths and
> > legends of the middle east.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of humanity such stale dismissal?

Actually I find that there are thousands of religions which have
different histories, different gods, different values mainly because
they originated in different areas. Even within the major grouping of
some of the more popular of these there are vast differences.

The reason that christianity spread in europeans was that jews, then
and now, are semi-nomadic and carried it to europe where it was imposed
by the remnants of the Roman Empire as a buffer against the pagan
and/or heathen barbarians.

I am sure that most christians dismiss the religions of the norse, the
classical greeks, and the romans. They also may dismiss the
Rastafarians (did I mention ...), the scientologists and many others.
In fact studying how and why recent religions have been formed shows
the process of creating religions around charismatics for the purpose
of social control by providing the promise of something better.

I just don't see any particular difference in any of them that would
make one 'right' and the others 'wrong'.  Rastafarians are just as
convinced and sincere in their beliefs as the most rampant
fundementalist.

>  We do not ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

I don't ask fish about anything, nor chicken entrails, though I am sure
that some may do so, and use the advice given, or pass it on to their
followers.

However, as on goldfish said to another: "If there is no god then who
changes the water ?".

> Are there not several strawmen in this argument?

Is Thor a strawman or an analogy ?

> Believing that the
> theory of evolution explains how life got here

Actually Darwin explicity stated that he was not explaining 'how life
got here' but his theory was not 'evolution' but was 'natural
selection' and 'common decent' which are explanations for the
observable occurance of evolution.  His is one of several theories
explaining various and different aspects of how evolution works.

You should note that in spite of the FUD produced by religionists
evolution is not a theory, it is natural selection, sexual selection,
and common decent that are the explanations of how it works.

> is good if it is based
> on rational examination.  It is bad when it is advanced merely because
> those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
> like Thor and his hammer so we must believe it.  It's even worse if we
> find ourselves repeating such, surely?

The 'anything else' consists of hundreds of diverse and incompatible
creation myths many of which you would most likely label as 'like Thor
and his hammer'.

The point is that man trys to create explanations for the evidence they
see. On mountains one sees the fossils of sea creatures and sea shells,
one also finds remains of unknown animals. One hypotheis for this is
that the sea was once so high that sea creatures lived here and in
doing so drowned other creatures.  Hence the story of a flood as an
explanation for what is observed.

Today we understand (or at least those who are educated do) about
tectonic plates and moutian forming so, for example, the southern alps
here (NZ) are the scapings from the sea bed piled up as the pacific
plate collides with the Autralasian (this is measurable). So there is
no need for the old mythologised explanations of why sea shells are up
mountains.
```

> the god of the jews, or just a man.

This also is mainly fiction, I am afraid, and again just stuff that is
repeated around the web.  The council was concerned with whether the
second person of the Trinity was of the same substance (homoousios) as
the first person, or of like substance.

But don't take my word for it.  Why don't you take half an hour and
look over all the ancient sources which mention the council, and see
what they say?  You won't find this stuff in them: it's all a modern
invention.  I linked to or uploaded them all at:

http://www.tertullian.org/rpearse/nicaea.html

(I know some brain-dead people then suggest that I am quoting my own
website as an authority.  But of course such people can do the legwork
for themselves, then -- I merely created the shortcuts as a help to
everyone).

> > > Hopefully you learn about the real world instead of the myths and
> > > legends of the middle east.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Actually I find that there are thousands of religions ... (snip)

I'm sorry, but these comments don't deal with my point, do they?  And
the game of calling Christianity a religion, then saying there are
loads of different religions therefore all of them (and especially
Christianity) must be untrue is a fraud.  Those who practise it always
have a set of beliefs and values that they propose to exclude from this
process, because they would be equally debunked by it.

> The reason that christianity spread in europeans was that jews, then
> and now, are semi-nomadic and carried it to europe where it was imposed
> by the remnants of the Roman Empire as a buffer against the pagan
> and/or heathen barbarians.

This is not a good representation of the history of Western Europe from
30AD- 410: sorry.  Where did you find this one?

> >  We do not ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.
>
> I don't ask fish about anything...

Well, I'd stop listening to donkeys if I were you... (Gotcha...!)

> > Are there not several strawmen in this argument?
>
> Is Thor a strawman or an analogy ?

Not an answer to my point.

> > Believing that the theory of evolution explains how life got here
> > is good if it is based on rational examination.  It is bad when it is
> > advanced merely because those who set the media agenda of our
> > day tell us that anything else is like Thor and his hammer so we must
> > believe it.  It's even worse if we find ourselves repeating such, surely?

(I snipped your comment on this, as it seemed to me just a change of
subject.)

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

- **Message-ID:** `<HByZf.33174$K11.32085@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <1144267591.491087.261230@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <1144430839.566147.92310@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>`

```

<roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144430839.566147.92310@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com...
>
> the game of calling Christianity a religion, then saying there are
> loads of different religions therefore all of them (and especially
> Christianity) must be untrue is a fraud.

    In my experience, what actually happens is an advocate for a given 
religion might say "All other religions are wrong, but mine is right." 
Someone might respond "What evidence do you have to support this claim?" to 
which the advocate responds "How dare you question my religion! You cannot 
prove the inexistance of my God! your logic has failed you! My religion 
wins!".

    Note that this hypothetical advocate has jumped to conclusions. The 
person did not actually claim that the advocate's religion was untrue; 
rather, he was just asking for evidence of its truthfulness. There's a big 
difference. It's not uncommon to ask for evidence for a claim which one 
suspects is true. E.g. "I believe it should be possible to open files in 
COBOL, but I just haven't been able to figure out a way to do so. Can 
someone demonstrate a program which does this?" In this example, I'm asking 
for evidence that COBOL can open files. I'm not claiming that COBOL cannot 
open files.

    So the "game" usually isn't saying that (especially) Christianity is 
untrue. Rather, it's asking "where's the evidence?", or more specifically, 
"That other advocate from another religion over there says HIS religion is 
right and yours is wrong. Who should I believe, and why?"

    - Oliver
```

That one has always intriqued me... :-)

If I'm dead and a spirit, why would a burning lake of sulphur or anything

pattern, why would I be concerned about heat? How do you 'burn' the 
unburnable...?

As for some psychotic believing God told him I need to believe that his 
religion is the true one, tell him that God has since appeared to me and 
confirmed that there was a misunderstanding. I am not welcome or eligible 
and should not bother the Prophets or waste their time... "For all have 
sinned and come short of the Glory..." except that, in my case, it is SO far 
short I must be declared 'Stony Ground' and anyone seeking to persuade me 
further is guilty of the sin of timewasting on God's ministry... :-)

Pete.

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T12:10:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e114mt$b0j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144243113.134237.323110@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144243113.134237.323110@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...

> Perhaps it was authored by Paul but subsequently edited by some other
> divinely-inspired member of the church?

Authorship meant something different in the first century than it does 
today, and that's part of the issue with New Testament authorship (books 
have been written on the subject).  In summary, someone being considered the 
"author" of something included the premise that the basic teachings they 
espoused were the *authority* behind the text.

Note that if 2 *Peter* was actually *written* by *Paul*, it would go counter 
to that premise!

Both Peter and Paul died before about 67AD, and the historical record seems 
to indicate that the copying, collection and distribution of Paul's letters 
didn't occur for a few decades after that.   The tradition reflected in this 
passage that Paul's letters *had been* collected and deserved to be treated 
with significant respect *cannot* have originated during Peter's lifetime.

And there's *nothing* in the passage that indicates *which* of Paul's 
authentic letters were to be included, to say nothing of which letters once 
attributed to Paul but whose authorship (in either sense) is now unknown, 
like the Epistle to the Hebrews, and whose direct Pauline authorship (like 
the Pastorals) is in significant question on linguistic grounds.   Even if I 
accepted the passage as having applicability to certain letters indisputably 
written by Paul, for me to accept it as having applicability as well to 
those that Paul *didn't* actually write is a bit of a stretch.

And if I followed Luther's advice, or I were of the Syriac Orthodox 
persuasion, I'd be dismissing the passage (along with the one in Revelation) 
as non-Scripture, so what body of literature the passage should be 
interpreted as referring to would be a meaningless question to start with!

    -Chuck Stevens
```
