```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>  
> Thanks for the response, Daniel. I accept it in the spirit in which you 
> intended it.

Excellent...  :)

> Some further comments below...
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> required.

But that's just it - parting company with God leaves one wide open to 
Satan, with nothing holding him back.  The sacrifice (annual in the 
Hebrew tradition, once-for-all with Jesus) was meant to be a picture of 
someone or something else taking the punishment for the wrongs one had 
done.  In fact, this separation seems to be the greatest punishment of 
all - Jesus' most impassioned cry from the cross was "My God, My God, 
why hast thou forsake me?"

(I'm sure that Jesus spoke the King's English - didn't everyone in Bible 
times?  ;>  )

> And why is an omniscient, omnipotent being so insecure that He requires 
> worship anyway? What does the worship prove? That you terrified people into 
> doing whatever you want?  I really have some major problems with all of 
> this...

I don't think it's insecurity at all - it's just that, because He is Who 
He is, He demands it.  Not to compare CEOs to God, but if one walks into 
a meeting room, don't the other folks in the room stand *because* he 
deserves it?  And would this CEO be showing insecurity if they corrected 
someone who did not do this?

>>> 2. And who was this terrible sacrifice made to?
>>> Well.... God.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> like mine....

(Keep in mind, these are all answers from the Bible - I know that using 
a document to validate itself is frowned upon in some circles, but when 
it comes to Christianity - well, it's pretty much what we've got.)

According to the Bible, sin passed beginning with Adam, through the 
male, to all humankind.  So, yes, we all were born with a nature that 
has, as a part of it, a propensity to sin.  (And, to expand on that - 
this is why some things that people say just "come naturally" may not be 
viewed as "right" according to the Bible's teachings.)

Back to Jesus - He was conceived by the Holy Spirit inside a virgin - 
so, although He was human in form, He did *not* have the sin nature that 
all other men (as in "mankind") are born with.  So, yes - He didn't 
sleep with Mary Magdalene, or lie to his parents, or play hooky from 
school, or steal apples.

(Having raised two babies to full elementary-school-hood, and with a 
13-month-old, I often wonder what it was Baby Jesus did as He grew up. 
I'd love to compare His behavior as a child to my children - to see what 
was developmental, and what is actually the result of sin.  To find that 
interesting, though, I guess you have to believe that sin exists.)

>>  For 33 1/3 years, He did it.  His death was an acceptable sacrifice.
> 
> But WHY was ANY sacrifice required? Because Adam and Eve disobeyed God?

Yes.

> Wasn't getting evicted form paradise enough punishment for that?

Right - they did, and they made the wrong one

> Besides they were supposed to have a choice. It isn't a choice if the only real 
> option is to do as you are told. If God didn't want robots then the choice 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> This bit really does escape me.

Make one choice, stay protected under God's care.  Make another, and 
you're on your own.  Both Adam and Eve thought they could do better on 
their own, and mankind has been working harder ever since.

[some snipped]

> To be fair, Daniel, I have given this whole subject a great deal of thought. 
> (And study.) Over 20 years. And not just Christianity...All of it. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> for each other's viewpoints.

I definitely agree on your last statement above.  Let me ask you this - 
how is evangelical Christianity treated in New Zealand?  Are cities 
allowed to put up Christmas trees without the threat of lawsuit?  Are 
Bible clubs allowed in public high schools, and afforded the same 
privileges as the chess club or the debate club?  Are people who object 
to lewd behavior labeled as "prudes" and "Bible-thumpers"?

I suspect that some other countries may actually have greater freedom of 
religion that the good ol' US of A does these days...

(I heard about an Easter Bunny decoration that had to be taken down so 
it wouldn't offend non-Christians.  Maybe Chuck, with his extensive 
Scriptural knowledge, could help us - just what book, chapter, and verse 
deals with the Easter Bunny?  :>  )

>> He is infinitely kind and forgiving, but also infinitely just.  Justice 
>> requires punishment for wrongdoing.
> 
[snipped parting of company]
> I contend that a history of punishing people has failed to change their 
> behaviour. It's time we tried an alternative approach,. The aim should be to 
> treat commission of crime as indicating aberration on the part of the 
> perpetrator. The object should be to rehabilitate them back into society. 
> Only when that fails should they be removed.

That sounds great - now define rehabilitation.  Is taking them off the 
streets for a time rehabilitation?  And how does this square with 
recidivism, especially high among sex offenders?  "Three strikes and 
you're out" seem too much leniency for rapists and molesters (as well as 
murderers and near-murderers).

> Punishment and 'an eye for an 
> eye' is simply a very flawed, primitive, concept that leads inevitably to a 
> blind and toothless society.

True - but isn't it the role of government to protect its citizens, even 
if it's from other of its citizens?  I'm not saying that if you shoot 
someone, your dominant hand should be cut off.  And, the justice that 
lead to this point in the discussion, God's justice, is quite plain.  "I 
know you can't do this by yourself - that's why I've provided a safety 
net for you.  However, I'm not going to make you use it - you've got to 
accept it for yourself."  It's not eye-for-eye, it's all-or-nothing. 
(That may not seem much better to you, but there is a subtle difference.)

> If people thought right in the first place, 
> they wouldn't commit crime. We should therefore encourage them to think 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> can be ruthless and cruel. A decent society would favour the former.

Not that you need it to support your view (nor are you required to 
defend it to me)...  But, in your opinion, how would a "human and 
civilized" society deal with the crimes I mentioned above?

>>  Mercy means that He accepts someone else's payment for our wrongdoings, 
>> so we don't have to suffer punishment ourselves.  All we have to do is 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> growth.

And, as it deals with other men, this is as it should be, IMO. 
According to the Bible, though, this doesn't square things up with God. 
  (If, though, as you believe, He doesn't exist - then, you're good to go.)

>>> The above is not intended to be offensive to devout Christians and I 
>>> apologise unreservedly if you find it so. I'm trying to honestly convey 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> same issues, but, as you might expect, I am not persuaded.

And these replies were in the same vein.  (This is kinda fun...)

>>> Gotta go now... there are some guys in hoods setting fire to a cross on 
>>> my front lawn... must be Christians, I guess... It's a very violent 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't deserve it, should lose it.

True.  But, let me assure you, my friend - when it comes to their 
religion, you're just as much an infidel as I am!  :(
```

- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
>  Let me ask you this - how is evangelical Christianity treated in New Zealand?

That depends on what they do.

> Are cities allowed to put up Christmas trees without the threat of lawsuit?

Christamas trees have nothing to do with religion. In fact the
christmas feast and presents have nothing to do with religion either,
they are entirely based on the northern european mid-winter solstice.
3 days after the solstice it could be established from the exact
direction of the sunrise that the sun was 'rising' and thus
calculations could be made as to how long the food would last before
spring. The female animals were pregnant for spring birth and the
excess males could be killed and eaten in celebration and to save the
grain they would have eaten.

These pagan rituals were taken over by the church and renamed because
it was easier than stopping the people continuing with their existing
celebrations. They have little meaning to the middle east.

However, there has been complaints about a cross put on top of the
clock tower in Palmerston North. It was originally put up for Christmas
and Easter which was OK. But then the mayor had it left there permantly
which raised protests.  Last week it fell down and this was dubbed "an
act of god".

> Are Bible clubs allowed in public high schools, and afforded the same
> privileges as the chess club or the debate club?

> Are people who object to lewd behavior labeled as "prudes" and "Bible-thumpers"?

Only if they are bible thumpers and prudes.

> I suspect that some other countries may actually have greater freedom of
> religion that the good ol' US of A does these days...

We have laws to protect that sort of thing. But then we don't have many
in fundementalist groups trying to run the country and the school
systems.

> (I heard about an Easter Bunny decoration that had to be taken down so
> it wouldn't offend non-Christians.  Maybe Chuck, with his extensive
> Scriptural knowledge, could help us - just what book, chapter, and verse
> deals with the Easter Bunny?  :>  )

Easter, eggs, and bunnies have nothing to do with christianity either.
Easter is Eostre, the celtic goddess of spring and fertility
represented and celebrated by the eggs and the rabbits (or march hares
in some places) as the return of life to ground at the equinox.

Shrove tuesday and lent are similarly pagan and related to the needs of
the european rural communities. They have no meaning in the middle
east.

Again taken over by the church and not even renamed because the people
were going to celebrate as they had done for centuries.
```

```
> But they were events with religious associations in Europe so (to pick
> nits) your assertion that they were not religious is wrong.

The mid-winter 'eat a pig to save feeding it' feast did not have a
religious basis, it was an entirely practical resource distribution.
Similarly, making pancakes and what turned into lent were just what
happened with food resourses before spring.

>> But then we don't have many
>> in fundementalist groups trying to run the country and the school
>> systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T02:28:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
 

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>  Thanks for the response, Daniel. I accept it in the spirit in which you 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snippage pf previous points and responses>
>>>  Rather, He wanted beings who would *choose* to worship Him for Who He 
>>> is, and trust Him for their needs.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Satan, with nothing holding him back.

Ah, here we diverge again.See, I don't believe in God, so I can't possibly 
believe in Satan.  My position on this is that the Universe has events. 
(Shit happens :-)) They are neither good nor bad; they just ARE. From our 
point of view as humans we decide that something helps us or it doesn't, so 
we have developed concepts of good and evil. As these concepts were invented 
by us (the Universe goes about its business quite happily as it has done for 
the last 16 billion years or so, and as it would do, whether we were here to 
observe it or not, completely oblivious to our moral or ethical dilemmas), 
then the ultimate personification of them into God and Satan has no more 
relevance to everyday life than turning widdershins before going to bed 
would.

As you may have gathered by now, there isn't much to be afraid of in my 
philosophy (neither God nor Devil... :-)). So, if I behave myself (and by 
and large, I do...) it is not through fear, but rather because it makes 
sense to do so.

>The sacrifice (annual in the Hebrew tradition, once-for-all with Jesus) was 
>meant to be a picture of someone or something else taking the punishment 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I wondered about that. When I first read it, I thought he might have been 
expecting God to take him off the cross as a demonstration of His power. (If 
he was human, this would be a reasonable hope; it was almost as if he was 
hoping that something would happen to stop the torment. But it didn't. On 
the other hand, if he was, in fact, God, then how could he forsake 
Himself...?)  I understand what you are saying here... that the separation 
from God was the worst agony of all and a necessary part of the punishment. 
It simply makes me wonder again about this God who would do that to anyone 
(let alone Himself or His Son), because humans made a 'wrong' choice, aeons 
before. I'm afraid it doesn't encourage me to join the followers of such a 
Being...
> (I'm sure that Jesus spoke the King's English - didn't everyone in Bible 
> times?  ;>  )

I really like the KJV. I like it even better in German. There is a power 
about the German version that makes God more terrifying and real. (Though 
still not enpugh to persuade me, I hasten to add... :-))
>
>> And why is an omniscient, omnipotent being so insecure that He requires 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> He is, He demands it.

That is my point, Daniel. Demanding it is not the action of one who 
understands real power. You don't demand respect, you earn it.


> Not to compare CEOs to God, but if one walks into a meeting room, don't 
> the other folks in the room stand *because* he deserves it?

They certainly do in the Forces. It may not necessarily be because that 
particular individual deserves it, but rather, in the same way you salute 
the uniform as  a matter of respect for what it represents, you would stand 
for a senior officer as a mark of respect for the office he holds and 
because it is a custom or tradition.

> And would this CEO be showing insecurity if they corrected someone who did 
> not do this?
>

Definitely, in my opinion. In the military, it simply wouldn't happen (if it 
did, it could be considered a mark of disrespect and the perpetrator would 
deserve whatever they got. In normal life, it's a bit different. If the guy 
has the right stuff he'll be afforded respect automatically. From time to 
time I have meetings with the CEO of the place where I work. I like him and 
respect him (as does everybody in the organization), but I wouldn't stand up 
when he entered the room and he wouldn't expect it. (Oddly enough, I 
probably would if he was a woman... :-)).  Neither would I treat him 
differently than anyone else. BECAUSE I respect and like him, I converse 
with him exactly as I would anyone else.

I believe your analogy in this case, is not a good one.


>>>> 2. And who was this terrible sacrifice made to?
>>>> Well.... God.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I appreciate the upfront disclaimer... :-) I'm happy to have this case made 
with Biblical quotations if that is what it takes...

> According to the Bible, sin passed beginning with Adam, through the male, 
> to all humankind.  So, yes, we all were born with a nature that has, as a 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I understand what you (or The Bible) is saying here. But I simply can't 
accept it. How can I personally be held accountable for what an acestor did 
at the dawn of Time? How is that fair? I'm not even responsible for what my 
Father or Grandfather did (a perceptive observer might note that I wasn't 
born at the time...) before I came along, and even afterwards. How do I know 
what I would have done if I were faced with the same choice as Adam? 
Besides, it implies that new born babies are sinful and you'd have a very 
hard time making that case.

> Back to Jesus - He was conceived by the Holy Spirit inside a virgin - so, 
> although He was human in form, He did *not* have the sin nature that all 
> other men (as in "mankind") are born with.  So, yes - He didn't sleep with 
> Mary Magdalene, or lie to his parents, or play hooky from school, or steal 
> apples.

You must understand how hard it is for me to accept the Virgin Birth 
argument (given that science requires a cloned offspring from a woman to be 
female), but, going along with it for the moment, even if he didn't have the 
propensity to sin that the rest of us have, that would not preclude him from 
taking the opportunity. If MM offered herself on plate as it were and he 
wasn't gay, wouldn't he be simply following human nature like he was 
supposed to? My point here is not whether he did or didn't (the Bible 
certainly doesn't report the details of his life) but the fact that there is 
no evidence to conclude that he didn't. And how would it make any difference 
to the ultimate sacrifice whether he was sinless or not? My point is that 
the whole sacrifice was pointless and perverse and God should not have 
required it. I really can't warm to this Lord God of Israel... He behaves 
very badly, for someone who should be setting an example.

>
> (Having raised two babies to full elementary-school-hood, and with a 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> interesting, though, I guess you have to believe that sin exists.)
I don't, as explained above. (I certainly believe that bad behaviour 
exists... :-)) I think it is interesting that you can view it the way you do 
and I commend your fairness here. I would be very interested to see which 
events and behaviours were ascribed to 'growing up' and which to 'sin'... 
:-)
>
>>>  For 33 1/3 years, He did it.  His death was an acceptable sacrifice.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes.

Well, wouldn't you think that a Supreme Being might have gotten over it? He 
knew they were going to disobey Him but he let it happen then held it over 
them for thousands of years until His Son could atone for it. It is so 
illogical and unjust that it is really hard to believe a God could do this.
>
>> Wasn't getting evicted form paradise enough punishment for that?
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> their own, and mankind has been working harder ever since.

Yes, and THAT would have been fair. But this whole atonement and blood 
sacrifice thing just doesn't stack up.

>
> [some snipped]
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> how is evangelical Christianity treated in New Zealand?

People are free to practise their religion. We have a diverse society and 
there is a Kiwi attitude that says people should be given a "fair go". If 
you start sacrificing goats on your front lawn in the name of your religion, 
you can expect complaints from your neighbours. If you don't desist, there 
will be a visit form the cops and you will probably be charged with cruelty 
to animals, running an unlicensed abbatoir, failure to comply with Public 
Health and Safety regulations or some other Civil statute that protects your 
neighbours from having to put up with it. You can scream "Freedom of 
Religion" all you like, the State has precedence.



>  Are cities allowed to put up Christmas trees without the threat of 
> lawsuit?

As far as I know. They have one in Tauranga.


>Are Bible clubs allowed in public high schools, and afforded the same 
>privileges as the chess club or the debate club?

Yes. At least at the schools I attended. (None of which were 'religious' 
schools.)


> Are people who object to lewd behavior labeled as "prudes" and 
> "Bible-thumpers"?

If they ARE prudes and Bible thumpers :-)  We are, on the whole, a very 
tolerant lot. Every kind of weird way out faih, religion, organisation, or 
cult, flourishes here and gets a 'fair go' unless they overstep the bounds 
as in the scenario above.

(If ritual goat sacrifice WAS actually part of your religion, it would 
certainly be possible to have certain premises (well out of sight of the 
public) licensed for that purpose and registered as a church, provided you 
could persuade the appropriate authorities that hygiene and health were up 
to the required standard and that the despatch of the animals was painless 
and humane. I don't know if it would be approved, but you would get a fair 
go... :-))

>
> I suspect that some other countries may actually have greater freedom of 
> religion that the good ol' US of A does these days...
>

Maybe other countries don't work so hard at it...

> (I heard about an Easter Bunny decoration that had to be taken down so it 
> wouldn't offend non-Christians.  Maybe Chuck, with his extensive 
> Scriptural knowledge, could help us - just what book, chapter, and verse 
> deals with the Easter Bunny?  :>  )
>

I think this is just silly. I know people here who are deeply offended by 
Hallowe'en. (I dressed up as the Devil for the last one, and they were just 
mortified and couldn't even look at the photos...)  Despite the fact that 
there area fair proportion of people who probably agree with them, noone has 
made a move to ban Hallowe'en.  Like I said, it is a diverse society...



>>> He is infinitely kind and forgiving, but also infinitely just.  Justice 
>>> requires punishment for wrongdoing.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> streets for a time rehabilitation?

No, rehabilitation is getting them to realise that if they are to live in a 
society, they have to respect their fellow citizens and treat them right. 
Rehabilitation means they lose the urge to be anti-social.

> And how does this square with recidivism, especially high among sex 
> offenders?  "Three strikes and you're out" seem too much leniency for 
> rapists and molesters (as well as murderers and near-murderers).

Yes, agreed. Recidivism is irrefutablke proof that rehabilitation (at least 
on this occasion) has failed. At this point we are probably better off 
without them...How you actually remove them from society and for how long is 
something to be considered. But removal should not be considered punishment; 
rather, dealing with bad behaviour. The goal of imprisonment should be to 
rehabilitate, and reparation by the perpetrator should be an essential part 
of the consequences for bad actions.
>
>> Punishment and 'an eye for an eye' is simply a very flawed, primitive, 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> if it's from other of its citizens?

Citizens have a right to live in safety and the weak must be protected. 
Anti-socials could lose their citizenship and be sent to Australia. (The 
Brits had it right...) (Sorry, I had to throw that in for a laugh...) 
Seriously, I think criminals should lose their rights as citizens and maybe 
even some of their Human rights for the termof their incarceration, with 
these things being earned back as part of the rehabilitation process.


> I'm not saying that if you shoot someone, your dominant hand should be cut 
> off.  And, the justice that lead to this point in the discussion, God's 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, getting back to what you refer to as God's justice, I see it as 
seriously flawed. You have a choice, but it really isn't a choice, you have 
a safety net but if you elect  not to use it you will be severely punished, 
in fact, anything other than complete obediance and unquestioning commitment 
to the Master's will results in some prety awful consequences.

I know noone said God has ot be democratic, but He really shouldn't abuse 
supreme power like that either...

>> If people thought right in the first place, they wouldn't commit crime. 
>> We should therefore encourage them to think right. (No, I am not 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> society deal with the crimes I mentioned above?

Believe it or not, but in a 'humane and civilized' society, these crimes 
simply would not occur. Yes, we have got a way to go but it is important to 
have aspirations if you intend to grow. When I was a quite young child we 
never had murders and rapes in our society on a daily basis and as a matter 
of course. There would be maybe 3 or 4 murders a year (usually as the result 
of passion) and rape was unheard of. Kids could go to school by hemselves 
using public transport without fear. Parents didn't need to worry about 
their kids, policemen and adults were your friend, and we left our houses 
unlocked. Somehwere along the way, we lost it, and it will probably never 
come back.  I blame Television and the USA... :-)

OK, I dodged your question... here's what I think:

Anyone who commits sexual crimes against children and women... or murders 
innocent people by acts of terrorism:  Cut their arms and legs off and leave 
them on street corners for people to kick as they walk past... No? OK 
then... removal permanently from civilized society by means of a painless 
and humane injection.(Checks and balances in place to ensure there is no 
doubt of guilt whatsoever. If there is doubt, then they get a long prison 
sentence that can be shortened depending on their progress towards 
rehabilitation.) In all cases, including execution, full reparation to be 
made to the victims (if alive) or their families. Executed criminals have 
all of their worldly wealth distributed back to the victim's family.
>
>>>  Mercy means that He accepts someone else's payment for our wrongdoings, 
>>> so we don't have to suffer punishment ourselves.  All we have to do is 
>>> claim that payment.

As discussed, I see no place for punishment in an enlightened society.

>>
>> Nope. I can't do that. I don't want anybody (not even God made flesh) 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> right!" "Long live Humanity!" "God is unfair!"

>
>>>> The above is not intended to be offensive to devout Christians and I 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
:-)

>>>> Gotta go now... there are some guys in hoods setting fire to a cross on 
>>>> my front lawn... must be Christians, I guess... It's a very violent 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Probably. But I'm less of a threat... I don't have a faith to die for... :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:bem732hb910e92kc4sg11djcnmisf874ce@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 6 Apr 2006 02:28:44 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> You deserve punishment for what your ancestor did -

Why? I wasn't there and had nothing to do with it. And what's more, I don't 
condone it. (If I did, you might have a case...) That's like saying that a 
generation of Japanese, born after the war, are responsible for the 
atrocities their fathers did in POW camps. It is a total non-sequitur. The 
perpetrators of crime and bad action are the ones responsible for it, not 
their offspring.


> but fortunately
> your boss hired a whipping boy to take that punishment.
>
I find the whole concept of a whipping boy offensive and repugnant. Like I'm 
not man enough to take my own punishment (when it is deserved...)? As I 
indicated in previous post, I don't personally support the idea of 
punishment anyway, but if you do, and I have knowingly offended you, then 
I'd cop it. (I'd make as strong a case against it as I could and I would 
ensure that whatever reparations that could be made, were made, but if I 
lost the case, I'd accept the punishment.)

Pete.
```

```
On Sat, 8 Apr 2006 01:30:20 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>> Certainly everlasting torture does nothing to help - even if the crime
>> was torturing other people.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sorry Howard, you lost me here...

Most Christian theology says we are all condemned (either to die
(which is OK by me), or to be tortured) to for our inherited sins.
Only those who accept Jesus Christ as our savior will be saved.

Statistically, those who accept Jesus Christ do so because that's what
their parents and society taught them.   Those who accept Islam do so
because that's what their parents taught and society them.   Those who
accept Hinduism do so because that's what their parents and society
taught them.

Accepting what your parents and society teaches you will either result
in you being in heaven or in hell - depending on where you were born.

So some people are being rewarded, others punished for being born the
right or wrong place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T17:03:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OLSYf.27992$Ph4.12733@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net>`

```
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET...

>> Not to compare CEOs to God, but if one walks into a meeting room, don't 
>> the other folks in the room stand *because* he deserves it?

    If I work in some big company, and someone walks in (who may or may not 
be the CEO, as far as I know), and everyone stands, I'd stand too. Not 
because I particularly "respect" this person, or because I think this person 
"deserves" it, but because I don't really know what's going on, but I figure 
if I do what everyone else is doing, I'm less likely to get in trouble.

    If I knew the CEO, and didn't respect very much at all (e.g. because 
I've observed that (s)he is generally dishonest) but (s)he expected me to 
stand, I'd probably stand anyway. The "pain" of standing up is less than the 
potential pain of punishment, should the CEO wish to extract it from me.

    If the CEO and I got along well, and (s)he asked me to stand, I'd do it 
as a favor to them.

    If the CEO and I got along well, and (s)he told me I didn't have to 
stand, I probably wouldn't stand.

[...]
>> And would this CEO be showing insecurity if they corrected someone who 
>> did not do this?

    Yes. It says to me "I want you to SHOW me that you respect me, either 
because I'm not sure that you do, and it's important to me that you do; or 
because I want other people to see that I'm respected, and it's important to 
me that other people see this." As opposed to "The people I care about 
respect me, and I don't really care what the people I don't care about think 
of me."

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net...
>
> I believe your analogy in this case, is not a good one.

    A situation where it might make sense to correct someone for not 
standing up (or showing some other form of respect) would be an adult to a 
child, in which case the adult assumes the child genuinely did not know of 
this custom. The adult does so not because (s)he demands respect from the 
child, but just to inform the child of this custom in this particular 
society, so that as the child grows up, they can conform (if they choose to 
do so), and if they don't conform, they do so knowingly rather than out of 
ignorance.

    Not sure if this can be compared to what God may have been doing.

> Recidivism is irrefutablke proof that rehabilitation (at least on this 
> occasion) has failed. At this point we are probably better off without 
> them...How you actually remove them from society and for how long is 
> something to be considered. But removal should not be considered 
> punishment; rather, dealing with bad behaviour.

    I feel the same way, but I find it difficult to get people to understand 
this concept. I compare it to throwing away a broken appliance (say a 
toaster which doesn't actually toast the bread). I do it not because I want 
to punish the toaster, but because the toaster simply doesn't behave the way 
a toaster should behave, and I was unable to repair it.

> Anyone who commits sexual crimes against children and women...

    Why specify children and women (e.g. as opposed to men)?

    - Oliver
```

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

```
On Wed, 05 Apr 2006 17:03:42 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>>> Not to compare CEOs to God, but if one walks into a meeting room, don't 
>>> the other folks in the room stand *because* he deserves it?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>if I do what everyone else is doing, I'm less likely to get in trouble.

When a low-life miscreant is in the court room being tried - he stands
when the judge enters the room - not because he trusts the judge, nor
his office - but because he doesn't want any more trouble than
necessary.
```

- **References:** `<f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <OLSYf.27992$Ph4.12733@edtnps90>`

```
On Wed, 05 Apr 2006 17:03:42 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>    I feel the same way, but I find it difficult to get people to understand 
>this concept. I compare it to throwing away a broken appliance (say a 
>toaster which doesn't actually toast the bread). I do it not because I want 
>to punish the toaster, but because the toaster simply doesn't behave the way 
>a toaster should behave, and I was unable to repair it.

And if you gave up on your hound that you couldn't train, you wouldn't
allow the sadist down the street to torture it.

>> Anyone who commits sexual crimes against children and women...
>
>    Why specify children and women (e.g. as opposed to men)?

For that matter, why do we want to punish someone who raped someone
more than one who beat that person up, knocking his/her eyes out?

My guess it's because we are upset because something good (sex) gets
defiled.    But sex isn't as important as people.    Baseball fans are
more upset by someone "hurting baseball" than by "setting an example
which destroys children".    Are such ideals more important than
people?
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:OLSYf.27992$Ph4.12733@edtnps90...
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
>> news:52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET...
>
<snipped some good stuff. I always enjoy your posts, Oliver. :-))
>
>    I feel the same way, but I find it difficult to get people to 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> it.

That's it in a nutshell. A very good example.
>
>> Anyone who commits sexual crimes against children and women...
>
>    Why specify children and women (e.g. as opposed to men)?

Because they are weaker physically, and should be protected and nurtured. 
(Obviously, if I saw a weaker man being attacked, I would (and have done) go 
to his assistance. For me, it has always been that way. At school, I stopped 
some kids bullying a weaker boy, and we were friends until his death a 
couple of years ago. I just had it drilled into me that the strong and the 
able have a responsibility to those less strong and able, that behaving like 
this is good for my growth as a man and as a Human, and that nobility, 
decency, and honour, come through courage and standing up for what is right. 
Old fashioned? Maybe. (I'm not a young man :-)) Corny? When it's written 
down, I guess it looks that way. Nevertheless, it's how I feel.)

My comment was not intended to be sexist.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T13:40:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e18egm$lh4$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <OLSYf.27992$Ph4.12733@edtnps90> <49ovogFplfm9U2@individual.net>`

```
In article <49ovogFplfm9U2@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>I just had it drilled into me that the strong and the 
>able have a responsibility to those less strong and able, that behaving like 
>this is good for my growth as a man and as a Human, and that nobility, 
>decency, and honour, come through courage and standing up for what is right.

To subject to drilling is one thing, Mr Dashwood... how something responds 
to that drilling speaks more of the substance of the drilled.

(This is a variant of 'Out of mud you cannot make a diamond... at best, a 
good brick'... to which I have learned to append '... but how often do you 
hear someone expressing a desire to live in a house made of 'good, solid 
diamonds'?')

That left aside... the thought that Strength and Ability bring 
Responsibility, instead of Privelege, has been said to be anti-Darwinist, 
anti-Capitalist and the sign of a Weak, Dependent Mind... wasn't it Maxim 
who said his gun would end war because no Gentleman would send his troops 
into Certain Death?

(Interesting that some folks who speak of the Rule of the Strong and use 
Nietzsche as a justification - the classic was the Leopold and Loeb 
murders, dripping with 'Overman' stuff - seem, somehow, to be able to skip 
over statements like (convention 'vowel-e for umlaut used) 'Was waere dein 
Glueck, wenn du nicht Die haettest, welchen du leuchtest!' ('What would be 
your fortune, if you did not have, those for whom you shine!' (your/you 
being rendered in the familiar case, not the formal))

DD
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:e119si$dqn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are you *sure* you're not referring to the Luther translation?

I don't know, Chuck. I'm referring to a Gideon Bible I found in a hotel room 
in Dusseldorf.

I started reading Genesis and it had much more effect on me than the same 
passage in English... There was something SO powerful about the actual 
language that you could hear God speaking it...(To get what I'm talking 
about read the following aloud (remembering to roll the 'r's); it might help 
if you pretend to be Hitler, but that's probably not entirely necessary :-))

1    Am Anfang schuf Gott Himmel und Erde. [In the beginning, God created 
the Heaven and the Earth]
2    Und die Erde war w�st und leer, [ And the Earth was desert and empty] 
und es war finster auf der Tiefe [And there was darkness on the face of the 
Deep]; und der Geist Gottes schwebte auf dem Wasser. [And the Spirit of God 
moved on the Waters]
3    Und Gott sprach: Es werde Licht! Und es ward Licht. [And God spoke: Let 
there be Light! And there was Light]
4    Und Gott sah, da� das Licht gut war. [and God saw that the Light was 
good...]

To me, it just doesn't have the same ring to it in English. Lines 3 and 4 
are superb...:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T09:28:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1e13b$1sh6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <e119si$dqn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <49ovojFplfm9U5@individual.net>`

```
Yes, that matches verbatim the Luther translation.

Haydn's setting of "The Creation" uses this text, and "...Und es ward Licht" 
is one of the most moving monents in all of choral music, as far as I an 
concerned.  Verse 3 is sung by the chorus in unison pianissimo (IIRC a 
capella), until you reach the last word, which is fortissimo with full 
orchestra on a tonic chord.  Verse 4, attaca, introduces as I recall a tenor 
aria.  Great music, great poetry.

But I don't think any nonsemitic language can capture the many layers and 
subtleties in meaning of this text.  I dabble in Hebrew, and the semantic 
implications of just the various verb forms in these few verses are worthy 
of much study.  It's amazing stuff.

Problem with *any* translation is that it's impossible to capture all the 
levels of meaning in another language.  As I recall, there's an Italian 
saying that runs something like "traduttore � traditore".

Think of the task of trying to supply the script for a dub of a half-hour 
"Rocky & Bullwinkle" show, ensuring that all of the puns and in-jokes are 
instantly recognizable by the audience, into Tagalog, Swahili, Lao or 
Tlingkit.   Getting it all across is virtually impossible, and an explained 
pun loses its impact.

 Such is the problem with the Bible, and it's a bigger problem for the Old 
Testament than for the New (particularly when the original texts themselves 
are obscure, garbled or inconsistent with each other -- e.g. a whole bunch 
of passages in Job).

    -Chuck Stevens

<top post, no more below>

news:49ovojFplfm9U5@individual.net...

…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T10:07:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1e3bv$1tur$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <e119si$dqn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <49ovojFplfm9U5@individual.net> <e1e13b$1sh6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <hp2l325jsf8np5lq2jd9rev0o02jo454ao@4ax.com>`

```
Well, hmm.  While it captures the fact that there *is* a pun in the passage 
really well, and the sense of the pun in Italian is in the same general 
"area" of the original , it still doesn't capture both the fact that it's a 
pun *and* the sense of the English very well.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qn4l32lpns1vfok0vd9uni8cojlaiq297c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <e119si$dqn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <49ovojFplfm9U5@individual.net> <e1e13b$1sh6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <hp2l325jsf8np5lq2jd9rev0o02jo454ao@4ax.com> <e1e3bv$1tur$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Mon, 10 Apr 2006 10:07:11 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Well, hmm.  While it captures the fact that there *is* a pun in the passage 
>really well, and the sense of the pun in Italian is in the same general 
>"area" of the original , it still doesn't capture both the fact that it's a 
>pun *and* the sense of the English very well.

It's probably the best one can do.   When you have the job of
translating puns - especially where there are visuals indicating that
something funny is going on, you sometimes need to make up your own
puns as being the best way to get in the spirit of the original.

Poetry must be similarly hard to translate.   

Also - some languages are easy to write even novels that rhyme - and
other languages are very difficult to do so.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

[much snippage - one > is Pete, two is me, etc.]

>> I don't think it's insecurity at all - it's just that, because He is Who 
>> He is, He demands it.
> 
> That is my point, Daniel. Demanding it is not the action of one who 
> understands real power. You don't demand respect, you earn it.

Sure, we as people feel that way - but, then again, we aren't God.  If 
you are the absolute, one and only Creator of the Universe, aren't you 
entitled to demand respect if your uppity creations don't give it on 
their own?

>> Back to Jesus - He was conceived by the Holy Spirit inside a virgin - so, 
>> although He was human in form, He did *not* have the sin nature that all 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> supposed to? 

Yes - and that's the point.  Human nature is inherently sinful.  If she 
did, and He did *not* partake, that squares with Hebrews 4:15b - "but 
[He] was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin."

>> I definitely agree on your last statement above.  Let me ask you this - 
>> how is evangelical Christianity treated in New Zealand?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Religion" all you like, the State has precedence.

What's the COBOL market like over there?  ;)

>>  Are cities allowed to put up Christmas trees without the threat of 
>> lawsuit?
> 
> As far as I know. They have one in Tauranga.

And they're called "Christmas Trees"?  Cool.  (Of course, it would be a 
*real* switch to celebrate Christmas on December 25th - in the middle of 
summer!)

>> Are Bible clubs allowed in public high schools, and afforded the same 
>> privileges as the chess club or the debate club?
> 
> Yes. At least at the schools I attended. (None of which were 'religious' 
> schools.)

Excellent.

>> Are people who object to lewd behavior labeled as "prudes" and 
>> "Bible-thumpers"?
> 
> If they ARE prudes and Bible thumpers :-)

Heh - that was Richard's reply as well.  At least Kiwis are consistent!  :)

>> I suspect that some other countries may actually have greater freedom of 
>> religion that the good ol' US of A does these days...
> 
> Maybe other countries don't work so hard at it...

Hard work at taking it away requires hard work at keeping it.  I'd love 
the forces at work in this country to stop trying to sanitize our 
heritage and our culture.  But, when you've got folks suing to eliminate 
things that have been in our country for over 200 years, not "working 
hard at it" isn't an option.

>> That sounds great - now define rehabilitation.  Is taking them off the 
>> streets for a time rehabilitation?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rehabilitation means they lose the urge to be anti-social.

[snip]

As I expected, very reasoned.  :)

> Believe it or not, but in a 'humane and civilized' society, these crimes 
> simply would not occur.

I wholeheartedly agree.  But why are things getting worse instead of 
getting better - are that many people rebelling against human nature? 
Or is human nature "naturally" depraved?

> OK, I dodged your question... here's what I think:
> 
> Anyone who commits sexual crimes against children and women... or murders 
> innocent people by acts of terrorism:  Cut their arms and legs off and leave 
> them on street corners for people to kick as they walk past...

Sounds good to me....

> No?

I'm sure someone would object...

> OK 
> then... removal permanently from civilized society by means of a painless 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> all of their worldly wealth distributed back to the victim's family.

That last part should be part of every nation's code.
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> Then he was not a man, or human in any way. This is the silliest damned
> disscussion I have ever read on usenet. Says a lot for how obsolete
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Perhaps you could expand on that statement - "Says a lot for how
obsolete
Cobol has become too".  I fail completely to see how the silliness of
the discussion has any bearing on the obsolescence of the language.

Exactly why is Cobol considered obsolete, anyway?  Still works, doesn't
it?  Is it because certain functions can't be done with verbs intrinsic
to the language?  Or just because it's old?

I'm asking to provoke discussion.  Seems to me that it's only "obsolete"
because a growing number of people say it is.  Most of them have never
used it so are obviously the most trustworthy authorities!

PL
```

```
Peter Lacey wrote:
> Donald Tees wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> the discussion has any bearing on the obsolescence of the language.

The forum was created to talk about a computer language.  Now it has
degenerated to gossip on who got laid, and who they got layed by 2000
years ago. That the main practitioners or the language all consider such
a disscussion more germaine to the NG than Cobol says lots to me, even
if you completely fail to see the problem.

> 
> Exactly why is Cobol considered obsolete, anyway?  Still works, doesn't
> it?  Is it because certain functions can't be done with verbs intrinsic
> to the language?  Or just because it's old?

Because nobody writes code in it any More.  Sure it works.  All I have
to do is four times as much work, and five times as much maintanance.

> 
> I'm asking to provoke discussion.  Seems to me that it's only "obsolete"
> because a growing number of people say it is.  Most of them have never
> used it so are obviously the most trustworthy authorities!

I used it for 30 years.  I even like the language. But what was good
enough when I graduated 35 years ago is not quite as good enough any more.

> 
> PL
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> The forum was created to talk about a computer language.  Now it has
> degenerated to gossip on who got laid, and who they got layed by 2000
> years ago. That the main practitioners or the language all consider such
> a disscussion more germaine to the NG than Cobol says lots to me, even

One of COBOL's strengths is that it continues to run, no matter how the 
world may try to move on, or J4 try to keep up.  :)

Besides, we already debated upper/lower case, full-stops on every 
zero-nesting-level statement vs. one per paragraph, sections vs. nested 
subprograms vs. only paragraphs, etc.  Got a new topic?
```

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T12:05:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4437ED65.9C8861C3@mts.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <4435D38E.AB243ACB@mts.net> <b9tZf.3069$sh3.212117@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 

> >
> > Exactly why is Cobol considered obsolete, anyway?  Still works, doesn't
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Five times as compared to what?  With all due respect - if you have to
do five times as much maintenance it's because the programs were
designed shoddily in the first place.  PD is stating that zero
maintenance is possible if the design is done properly.  I can't speak
to the comparative amount of work in writing a Cobol program as compared
to any other language, but I suspect it's a conceptual artifact because
a lot of work is done these days in event-oriented applications.  I've
worked in PROGRESS - an up-to-date language designed for GUI; I can
testify that while event-oriented is a snap, writing a report or a BOM
explosion or any other primarily procedural program is no quicker or
easier than in COBOL.

The answers that have been published on why COBOL is considered to be
obsolete seem to be about the same as Donald has stated above. 
Basically: it's because people don't use it because it's obsolete.  A
nice positive-feedback situation.  Mind you, to be fair, evaluating the
comparative merits of languages is a lost cause: when FORTRAN first
became THE language some commentator or other said it was because of its
comparative strengths, not its absolute strengths.  Nobody can make a
conclusive case here: even machine language is essential although only
compiler-writers have to be aware of it.

PL
```

```
On Mon, 10 Apr 2006 12:10:58 -0400, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>> How do you define perfect code?   Code that does the job as per specs?
>
>Code that does it's job forever, in otherwords, no maintenance.

Nothing is forever.   But there is code that works until its purpose
is no longer applicable.     Sometimes it is "maintained" to match new
conditions, sometimes it is discarded.    Sometimes it is in side of a
device that is discarded.

If you write a program to solve a puzzle to win a contest and then
throw out that program after you win - then that program did its job
perfectly.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<4a1jcaFqcvppU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <4435D38E.AB243ACB@mts.net> <b9tZf.3069$sh3.212117@news20.bellglobal.com> <4437ED65.9C8861C3@mts.net> <SKTZf.4166$sh3.309512@news20.bellglobal.com> <oatk32tojqbfbc99673dsmnbgonhjs24bg@4ax.com> <msv_f.1825$fo1.84877@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:msv_f.1825$fo1.84877@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>> On Sat, 08 Apr 2006 15:00:01 -0400, Donald Tees
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Donald,

I have had my posts on this misunderstood, spun, and pushed to extremes I 
never intended or supported.

Consider the following:

If you write a COBOL statement: "Multiply B by A giving B on overflow 
perform b-is-not-valid"

If you documented this as a subroutine and said:

"This subroutine takes three parameters, A, B, and a return code It 
multiplies B  by A and leaves the result in B. It  checks that this 
operation succeeded. If it did, it returns zero. If it didn't it returns 
non-zero."

I file this subroutine in my box of subroutines and call it "MultAB"

Some years later, I am looking for a subroutine to multiply two numbers.  I 
re-read the documentation on MultAB and it looks like it will do the job. I 
use it.

Another year goes by.

I need a subroutine to multiply two numbers, BUT the result has to be in 
short floating point format .

*** Do I dig out MultAB and change the picture of B to be comp-1?***

Well, I could.  If I did, I would run the risk that anything that used 
MultAB would no longer function correctly.

So, instead, I decide to 'extend' the functionality of MultAB WITHOUT 
maintaining it.

I code: Call "MultAB" using A B return-code
                on exception perform handle-call-failure
            end-call
            if return-code = zero
               move B to BFP *> a field defined as comp-1 in the CALLING 
program...
            else
               perform MultAB-failed
            end-if

I can easily package this as another subroutine and put it in my subroutine 
box... maybe call it "MultABFP"

Now I have two building blocks.

If  I only need to multiply two numbers I use MultAB; if I need to multiply 
two numbers and get the result as short floating point, I call MultABFP

I will NEVER amend either of them.

They will work doing what they do, no more, no less,.... forever.

Perfect code? I dunno... but I sure ain't maintaining it.

If I need SOMETHING LIKE what they do, I'll write a new subroutine.

That is what I mean by maintenance free code. I never claimed the code is so 
perfect it doesn't need maintenance. I claimed it does what it does and I 
don't change that.

Encapsulation in components is an ideal example of this. Some component 
based shops DO maintain components.

I don't.

I believe that maintenance introduces more errors than any other activity. 
(Including development in the first place...)

It is best avoided.

(However, I've only been programming 41 years, so maybe in the next 4 years 
I'll change my mind... :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Cobol obsolete?

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-04-11T18:56:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RZS_f.3620$WI1.2946@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4a1jcaFqcvppU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <4435D38E.AB243ACB@mts.net> <b9tZf.3069$sh3.212117@news20.bellglobal.com> <4437ED65.9C8861C3@mts.net> <SKTZf.4166$sh3.309512@news20.bellglobal.com> <oatk32tojqbfbc99673dsmnbgonhjs24bg@4ax.com> <msv_f.1825$fo1.84877@news20.bellglobal.com> <4a1jcaFqcvppU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

> Donald,
> 
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

What you have written above is crystal clear.

"I have had my posts on this misunderstood, spun, etc......"

So you are bewildered why your posts have been misunderstood - it's down 
to terminology where you use the word COMPONENT. I'm hoping you didn't 
take 40 minutes to figure out your 'components' above.

Try this - what you have illustrated is NOT Components. The 'sacred 
book' issued by J4 has a another feature called UDF (User Defined 
Functions), which are not restricted to OO. A quick check of both the 
NetCOBOL and Net Express LRMs, doesn't reveal that this feature appears 
in either compiler - I wonder if it ever will.

So no doubt you have an extensive library of UDFs and where necessary 
you 'glue' them together to produce a Component (Dialog ?). Your 
individual UDFs do not require maintenance.

Consider following - I pass parameters per control to an instance of 
Dialog Template - I want to create a text-entry field :-

        *>--------------------------------------------------------------
        Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield".
        *>-------------------------------------------------------------
         invoke self "getObjectFromId"
                using Dialog-ID returning Dialog-object
         invoke Dialog-object "accessSystemEvents"
         ....lots more stuff to create entryfield........+++

        End Method "zCreateEntryfield".
        *>-------------------------------------------------------------

Ahhh, but now I want to 'draw' or create dynamic entryfields as well :-

        *>------------------------------------------------------------
        Method-id. "zDynamicEntryfield".
        *>-------------------------------------------------------------
        Linkage section.
        01 lnk-record                       pic x(RL).
        01 lnk-CoOrdinates.
           05 x                             pic x(4) comp-5.
           05 y                             pic x(4) comp-5.
           05 w                             pic x(4) comp-5.
           05 h                             pic x(4) comp-5.

        Procedure Division using lnk-record, lnk-CoOrdinates.

         move lnk-Record      to Control-Data
         move Dialog-Sequence to Dialog-Field

         Evaluate true

           when   Dialog-EntryType = 0  *>  SingleLineEntry
                  invoke TextEntry "new"
                       using self returning Dialog-object

           when   Dialog-EntryType = 1  *>  SingleLineWordWrap
                  invoke TextEntry "new"
                       using self returning Dialog-object
           when other
                  invoke MultipleLineEntryField "new"
                       using self returning Dialog-object

         End-Evaluate

         invoke Dialog-object "SetRectangle"
                using x, y, w, h
         invoke Dialog-object "accessSystemEvents"
         ....lots more stuff to create entryfield........+++
	... I'm repeating the same stuff above for normal entryfield

        End Method "zDynamicEntryfield".
        *>--------------------------------------------------------------

I'm looking at the above two methods as I'm planning - given the 
separate methods, note the repetition, so let's revamp :-

        *>--------------------------------------------------------------
        Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield".
        *>-------------------------------------------------------------
         invoke self "getObjectFromId"
                using Dialog-ID returning Dialog-object
         invoke Dialog-object "accessSystemEvents"
         invoke self "zCreateEntryfield2"

        End Method "zCreateEntryfield".
        *>-------------------------------------------------------------
        Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield2".          *> PRIVATE
        *>-------------------------------------------------------------
        Local-storage section.
        01 ls-event                     pic x(4) comp-5.
        01 ls-Format.
           05 ls-Format1                pic 9.
           05 ls-Format2                pic 9.
        01 ls-length                    pic x(4) comp-5.

        *> The method above "zcreateEntryField" creates the Dialog-object

        initialize ls-length

        *>----------------------
        *> Alphanumeric
        *>  0 = Numerick
        *>  1 = LowerCase
        *>  2 = UpperCase
        *>  3 = Default (0-2)
        *>  4 = Date4 (yymm)

        Evaluate true
            when Numerick   invoke Dialog-Object "numeric"
            when LowerCase  invoke Dialog-Object "lowercase"
            when UpperCase  invoke Dialog-Object "uppercase"
            when Date4      invoke Dialog-Object "numeric"

	.... further detail of course..........

        Exit Method.
        End Method "zCreateEntryfield2".
        *>--------------------------------------------------------------
           Method-id. "zDynamicEntryfield".
        *>-------------------------------------------------------------
        Linkage section.
        01 lnk-record                       pic x(RL).
        01 lnk-CoOrdinates.
           05 x                             pic x(4) comp-5.
           05 y                             pic x(4) comp-5.
           05 w                             pic x(4) comp-5.
           05 h                             pic x(4) comp-5.

        Procedure Division using lnk-record, lnk-CoOrdinates.

         move lnk-Record      to Control-Data
         move Dialog-Sequence to Dialog-Field

         Evaluate true

           when   Dialog-EntryType = 0  *>  SingleLineEntry
                  invoke TextEntry "new"
                       using self returning Dialog-object

           when   Dialog-EntryType = 1  *>  SingleLineWordWrap
                  invoke TextEntry "new"
                       using self returning Dialog-object
           when other
                  invoke MultipleLineEntryField "new"
                       using self returning Dialog-object

         End-Evaluate

         invoke Dialog-object "SetRectangle"
                using x, y, w, h
         invoke Dialog-object "accessSystemEvents"
         invoke self "zCreateEntryField2"
         invoke Dialog-object "show"

        EXIT METHOD.
        End Method "zDynamicEntryfield".
        *>--------------------------------------------------------------

The above work WITHOUT maintenance, having ensured, in the design stage, 
I've covered all bases for the COMMON properties. I'm quite happy with 
the properties listed.

Then I get into an accounting application and decide I'll use black on 
white for Debits and Red on White for Credits. Oops ! Change those 
methods ! I DON'T - I have separate methods for colouring (using RGB), 
which are controlled from the Business class, which are only applied 
against the objects to which they are relevant.

My Dialog Template is a series of methods/UDFs.

Bye, bye COBOL - I'll see if I can do the above using Smalltalk, Java or 
   C#. Haven't decided yet but Smalltalk and C# have freebie IDEs; 
haven't checked but with Java could use Eclipse ?

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:RZS_f.3620$WI1.2946@pd7tw2no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
> take 40 minutes to figure out your 'components' above.

No, I'm not bewildered about it; I understand the resistance.
>
> Try this - what you have illustrated is NOT Components.

Of course it isn't! I never claimed it was...  It is as simple as I can make 
the concept of non-maintenance, using non-joined-up writing, with a 
crayon... :-)

I'm no longer bugged by the lack of understanding. For a while I was bugged 
because it seemed people didn't even WANT to understand; they's rather have 
an argument  based on their misunderstanding, or decide that it was just a 
re-invention of what they've always done, or that I must be crazy because 
"everybody knows" you can 't write computer systems without changing the 
code on a regular basis... Now I just smile at all of it. (Frequenting CLC 
is good for growth :-))

 The 'sacred
> book' issued by J4 has a another feature called UDF (User Defined 
> Functions), which are not restricted to OO. A quick check of both the 
> NetCOBOL and Net Express LRMs, doesn't reveal that this feature appears in 
> either compiler - I wonder if it ever will.

User Defined Functions are available in other languages, and  I have a 
feeling they are in COBOL 2018... :-)
>
> So no doubt you have an extensive library of UDFs and where necessary you 
> 'glue' them together to produce a Component (Dialog ?). Your individual 
> UDFs do not require maintenance.

No, I have a library of components. Most of them are ACtiveX or COM+. I say 
'most' because i also have a couple fo Java beans that I'm very proud of and 
they are also components. Oddly enough (in light of your post) I DON'T glue 
things together to produce what you suggest could be a DIALOG Component 
above. For that I use Controls. These are components already, in the 
broadest definition. But to build GUI interfaces I use controls. The things 
I do with the data are usually caried out by component methods, but that has 
nothing to do with the interface. (Multier tier... components for the 
Business level, controls for the presentation level, and ODBC for the Data 
level. Of course, PowerCOBOL simply shortcuts the whole process and allows 
you to drag n drop whatever you want (including my own componets which 
appear on the PowerCOBOL control toolbar once they are registered. It is the 
same process as registering libraries in Visual Basic.

Speaking of VB, I never thought I'd see the day, but I have been forced to 
learn and use it so I could extend the Rational tool set to manage my RAD 
activites. I have Rational Clear Quest eating out of my hand now and have 
added so much functionality to it that the IBM rep asked for a copy of the 
User Manual for my RAD control tool which is based around Clear Quest. (I 
call it RADISH...it stands for RAD implementation system housekeeping. :-) 
There is a 23 page manual (including screenshots) and it is growing.) RADISH 
lets me track system requirements from the time they were identified (and 
stored on Rational Requiste Pro), through Gap analysis and into JAD review 
sessions. I can see the state of each Use Case, the flows under it and its 
deliverables. (Requirements are tied to Use Cases and vice versa). It 
calculates immediately the time spent reviewing the Use Case and identifying 
problems (we call them 'Flaws' because they are anything that stops the 
process working and this can be configuration, environment, functional 
inadequacy, defects in software, bugs in the web interface, whatever...), 
and how long is being spent fixing the identified problems. RADISH 
automatically 'debits' the time from the timebox for the Use Case, and 
updates statuses. The state of each flaw and complete history of all work 
done on it, is also maintained by RADISH.) The team are happy with it 
because they only have to click at the start of a JAD session and then raise 
flaws on it. At the end fo the session they click again on the session 
record and it is closed and the time elapsed is calculated (no entering 
dates and times... all done by RADISH, and great fun in VB... :-))

I have to confess, after years of despising VB without ever actually using 
it, I have changed my mind. It is useful and fun. I'm considering investing 
in Visual Studio and Fujitsu NETCobol for .NET. I looked (briefly) at the 
new release of MF Visual  Express which is supposed to replace Server 
Express, but I still feel MicroFocus is too low level in their approach. 
Visual Studio (it comes with Fujitsu)  could be a very good option because I 
would get C++, VB, C#, and COBOL for .NET in one fell swoop and all running 
under the same IDE. But, it isn't cheap and I don't plan on doing much 
further serious commercial development for myself, so it would just be for 
fun. I want a dual 64 bit notebook that can run Vista as well, so I probably 
can't have it all :-)

> Consider following - I pass parameters per control to an instance of 
> Dialog Template - I want to create a text-entry field :-
…[142 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I can see you have done a good job of that, Jimmy. But this is a 
demonstration of exactly why I wouldn't go MF. It is all mind-numbing 
detail. If I want an entry field I can drag and drop one on a form in 
seconds, have complete control of all the properties you mention above  (and 
then some... I can enable it, disable it, make it invisible, the control has 
a property sheet as long as your arm, and I don't have to code any of it. If 
I want one dynamically I simply create an instance of the control and set 
the co-ordinates of where I want it. I like the simple life :-))

Nevertheless, given that is the tool you are using, I think it is fine.

And you have certainly tumbled to the idea of avoiding maintenance with 
careful design. Using controls gives the same advantage of course.

> Then I get into an accounting application and decide I'll use black on 
> white for Debits and Red on White for Credits. Oops ! Change those methods 
> ! I DON'T - I have separate methods for colouring (using RGB), which are 
> controlled from the Business class, which are only applied against the 
> objects to which they are relevant.

Hmmmm.... That is one way.

I would encapsulate the fields as controls. (You have virtually done that, 
but I think they may need a few more properties before you could be sure of 
not needing to maintain them).  I wouldn't use a method to change colours; 
I'd simply expose the background and foreground colour attributes of the 
control (component). They can be flicked on and off from anywhere and at any 
time, so it gives somplete flexibility and it is less overhead than using a 
Method.

It really doesn't matter. The details are not so important; it is the 
concepts that matter.

>
> My Dialog Template is a series of methods/UDFs.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> checked but with Java could use Eclipse ?
Yes, Eclipse is free also (I think... not sure on that). If you extend it to 
WSAD you start paying for it. I wouldn't go with SmallTalk; it is deader 
than COBOL and really is now a 'teaching' language, just like Pascal. Great 
for Acadaemia, but not really much use in the real world.

C# looks like fun... :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:4a4amlFr16s6U1@individual.net...
>
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message

>> Bye, bye COBOL - I'll see if I can do the above using Smalltalk, Java or 
>> C#. Haven't decided yet but Smalltalk and C# have freebie IDEs; haven't 
>> checked but with Java could use Eclipse ?
> Yes, Eclipse is free also (I think... not sure on that). If you extend it 
> to WSAD you start paying for it.

    Eclipse is free, and it's what I use at work and at home. The download 
is available at http://www.eclipse.org/downloads/

    Eclipse is promoted as "not only" an IDE, but a "platform". You can 
download all sorts of plugins for Eclipse, which could add support for C++, 
COBOL, PHP for example, but also UML drawing tools, "webbing" stuff, and 
even games. A list of "official" plugins are available at 
http://www.eclipse.org/downloads/index_project.php and if you google for 
"Eclipse plugin", you'll find a bunch of sites listing "unofficial" plugins 
(e.g. http://eclipse-plugins.2y.net/eclipse/index.jsp, 
http://www.eclipseplugincentral.com/, etc.)

    - Oliver
```

```
In article <657%f.22373$4S.4981@edtnps82>, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> writes:
> 
>     Eclipse is promoted as "not only" an IDE, but a "platform". You can 
> download all sorts of plugins for Eclipse, which could add support for C++, 
> COBOL, PHP for example, but also UML drawing tools, "webbing" stuff, and 
> even games.

It's for people who find Emacs too fast, lightweight, and spartan.

Personally, if I were an Eclipse user, I'd make a Xen plugin so I
could run multiple copies of my OS under Eclipse.  Why settle for a
mere "platform" when you can put an entire virtual system in your
editor?  In fact, why not start a new virtual machine for every
external command, just in case it crashes?  Performance might be OK
on my 32-way SMP box, and anything smaller is just a toy.

"Eclipse - finally a basket big enough for *all* your eggs!"
```
