###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Cobol obsolete?

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T07:31:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0qc32h08g7hdb28hq0hog1va2d0iq769m@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <4435D38E.AB243ACB@mts.net>`

```
On Thu, 06 Apr 2006 21:50:54 -0500, Peter Lacey <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>Exactly why is Cobol considered obsolete, anyway?  Still works, doesn't
>it?  Is it because certain functions can't be done with verbs intrinsic
>to the language?  Or just because it's old?

It will survive - as a niche language.   Most computer languages are
niche languages.   I learned it in 1969 and possibly will be still
using it in 2009.    For most of that time, it has been the no-brainer
choice for business computer applications.

Nowadays, businesses don't buy a language - they buy business
packages.    They tweak their databases and their interfaces - which
interfaces are turning out to be Web based more and more.   So their
language for their applications is whatever their vendor used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** anderschwan@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T21:21:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
AMEN!  Dang it I was interested in the first article then this became a
total bs session on religion - how does that happen?  Can people let
religion aside for a minute and help a sister out ?

I like to kick it old school with COBOL, what's wrong with that?  I
miss the good ole days, especially now.  I work for a startup sponsored
by a big insurance company, and they transferred 5 of us mainframe
programmers (basically we came in as insurance company employees one
day, then the next we were told we were now working for a subsidiary -
congratulations! ) to help them design a web based system accessing a
database stored on a new offsite server.  Well, that sucked because
none of us know jack about Windows networking, web development or
Oracle.  The real jewel was that the managment in their wisdom let us
think we were migrating to a Unix server for a couple of months, which
would have been great because we've got Unix skills, and would be able
to convert our COBOL with microfocus and run some shell scripts and
such.  But then a month ago we meet with this offsite hosting company
they hired, and we find out it is a Windows server that our director
decided on (it's cheaper - yay!).  Great.  So now we are trying to
figure out what kind of software we need to convert our COBOL programs
to work on a Windows Server.  Our deadline is this summer, and they
still haven't ponied up the money for any of the software we suggested.
 But they want the five of us to draw up system plans, timelines, meet
with users, negotiate with partners and do all that as well as learn
client server technology in the next 2 weeks.  We have one dude who
actually has worked with Oracle & Java- and he knows what ASP is!
Fabulous.  The other dude worked with VB and is a windows guy with
valuable experience - from the outside world!  Personally I have
touched ten VB 6 programs so I guess they think I'm a guru.  We are so
qualified to do this!

Sorry to go on and on but my team lead, manager, etc are still trying
to figure out what software we are going to have to learn on the fly.
The server hosting company is getting tens of thousands of dollars to
basically house 2 windows servers and occasional dusting - no support
or software (except they did buy a tape drive to read our old 3290
tapes, probably off ebay, which I am sure we will get billed 10,000 for
- I am in the wrong business in more ways than one here).

Do you guys think that we should just convert to VB and run some
vbscript (there is an Oracle database we also have to create, but thank
goodness I have a Oracle DBA buddy to help me since I am now the DBA as
well as a developer, and also project manager and Network Services
support.)
What generally is easier, from COBOL, would it be fujitsu cobol, cobol
.net or java or vb .net?  I know I'm being a bit of a tool here but
when you are desperate for info it's hard to be eloquent.

If anybody has any ideas then I will personally get down on my knees
and pray for them to go to heaven.  I am an old school gal being forced
to learn this new junk in 2-4 months on my own.  I can't even get these
morons to buy me a book.  At least they haven't restricted google yet!

Donald Tees wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
> > Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald
```

```
<anderschwan@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
> AMEN!  Dang it I was interested in the first article then this became a
> total bs session on religion - how does that happen?  Can people let
> religion aside for a minute and help a sister out ?

Sure. What's the problem?

>
> I like to kick it old school with COBOL, what's wrong with that?  I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> database stored on a new offsite server.

Pretty straightforward stuff that wouldn't require great research.


Well, that sucked because
> none of us know jack about Windows networking, web development or
> Oracle.

Then you didn't keep up with your education, did you?

Relational database you can get the basics of in a day; Oracle specifically, 
say 3 months.

Web Development - You need HTML (That's an afternoon of teaching yourself), 
ASP (another cople of days and access to a web server. If you have Win XP 
you can run IIS. It's great for trying out your ASP code. When it works, you 
just FTP it to the real server. ) Might be useful to learn XML too.

You're going to need OO concepts because all the tools you are likely to use 
assume it. This will be harder of you already know COBOL because you'll 
think you have nothing to learn and that will set you back about three 
months. Try to clear your mind and suspend judgement until you have fiddled 
with the VB/HTML/ASP.

> The real jewel was that the managment in their wisdom let us
> think we were migrating to a Unix server for a couple of months, which
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> to work on a Windows Server.

It's actually more complex than that. You are dealing now with a Web UI. 
Depending on your existing COBOL it may be very difficult ot convert the 
existing User Interface. I would recommend refactoring the Business logic 
and maintaining a separation layer (interface... buffer in memory... 
whatever) between the Business logic and the presentation layer. Where the 
COBOL currently writes to stdout or a display, trap that datastream and look 
at making it ASP session variables or (better still) Form variables driven 
with ASP.

>Our deadline is this summer, and they
> still haven't ponied up the money for any of the software we suggested.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> qualified to do this!

You are more qualified to do it than your manager... or the Janitor... or 
the tea-lady. Be positive.
>
> Sorry to go on and on but my team lead, manager, etc are still trying
> to figure out what software we are going to have to learn on the fly.

See above.

> The server hosting company is getting tens of thousands of dollars to
> basically house 2 windows servers and occasional dusting - no support
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> support.)

Yes, VBScript is definitely an option for the client side. Use ASP for the 
server side and processing of the forms with input data.

One option might be to convert your existing COBOL into Server side routines 
and run them as ASAPI on the server. Without seeing the code it is hard to 
assess what is most viable

> What generally is easier, from COBOL, would it be fujitsu cobol, cobol
> .net or java or vb .net?  I know I'm being a bit of a tool here but
> when you are desperate for info it's hard to be eloquent.
>
This is simply a matter of opinion. I have done all of the things you are 
trying to do and I used Fujitsu, but that doesn't mean the others are no 
good.

> If anybody has any ideas then I will personally get down on my knees
> and pray for them to go to heaven.

Don't do that... a simple 'Thank you" will suffice, when you get it working 
:-)


I am an old school gal being forced
> to learn this new junk in 2-4 months on my own.  I can't even get these
> morons to buy me a book.  At least they haven't restricted google yet!
>

OK. Now listen up. What you need to learn CAN be learned in the time you 
have. You can do it yourself, from the web. (I did...)

Here is the best kept secret and probably the most important web site you 
will ever visit:

http://www.w3schools.com/

Everything you need to know... HTML, XML, VBScript, ASP (and have a look at 
CSS, after you've got the hang of HTML). Do the tutorials, work regularly 
and methodically. Experiment and play as your knowledge grows. You'll find 
it's actually huge fun building web sites. Especially ones that access 
databases...remotely.

Just a final point - get them to invest in Dreamweaver or better still 
MacroMedia Studio 8 which has Dreamweaver, content management, Flash and few 
other goodies thrown in.  Even Visual Studio is pretty useful. These tools 
can save hundreds of hours.

Good Luck!

Pete.

PS If you get stuck, mail me privately. I may not be a Christian, but I 
don't mind helping people... :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T13:52:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net>`

```
In article <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><anderschwan@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>Then you didn't keep up with your education, did you?

Eh?  A manager says to a secretary 'You use pencil and paper, an 
accountant uses pencil and paper... so, today, without any further ado, 
you are to perform as an accountant'... and it is the secretary's fault 
for 'not keeping up with education'?

Rather... managerial of you, Mr Dashwood.

[snip]

>> We are so
>> qualified to do this!
>
>You are more qualified to do it than your manager... or the Janitor... or 
>the tea-lady. Be positive.

She seems positive, Mr Dashwood, that Management has inappropriately 
allocated personnel and resources towards the accomplishment of a stated 
Executive goal... and by her description she seems correct.  That does not 
mean it cannot be done, of course - I do my best work after I rear back 
and snarl '*Don't* tell me I 'can't do that'!' - but if a manager assigns 
a task which requires learning and does not allocate the resources 
necessary to learn (those being, in part, time and money) then it is not, 
I would say, the responsibility of anyone *but* said manager if the task 
is not completed.

[snip]

>> I am an old school gal being forced
>> to learn this new junk in 2-4 months on my own.  I can't even get these
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>have. You can do it yourself, from the web. (I did...)

Mr Dashwood, as my Sainted Paternal Grandfather - may he sleep with the 
angels! - used to say, 'Never use yourself as a comparative, you'll only 
be disappointed'.  To apply this to the present situation: it seems as 
though the managers here are expecting the employees to respond to a 
situation as consultants do - you've been a consultant for a few years 
now, haven't you? - without paying them as consultants are paid.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T08:49:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
Thank you so much for that - you seem to understand my ramblings and
see the big mess for what it is.  I can't help being worried because
the problem to me isn't so much learning new technology, I do think it
could be a great opportunity all of us.  At first we were thrilled that
we would be working on this project.  But most of us feel that we
aren't being given the resources, training or even the time that we
need to do this correctly.

I may not have a lot of experience, I don't have a computer science
degree and I've learned what I do know from some great mentors and
learning these skills on my own.  But I didn't apply for this - I came
in one day before Christmas with one job, knowing my roles and
responsibilities, and found out that I was now working for a new
company in a vague role with a lot more work and no more pay.  The way
my manager, who I do like by the way, he's a great guy also being asked
to do things outside his scope, explained it to us was that this was a
big challenge and that it wasn't a secure job.  Some of us asked if
we'd get a new title, more money or overtime maybe or a bonus if it
went well - but we were told that since this is a new company, we would
be expected to keep the same benefits title and pay that we have now,
but there would be "opportunities".  So maybe that will happen - or
what is more likely is that they will get amnesia when it comes time
for review and I will get the standard 4% raise to keep up with
inflation and they will talk about how the budget is tight and this is
generous, blah blah blah.

I want to be positive but I just don't have the confidence that it will
work.  There are so many things that people haven't thought of that
they should have.  I don't know why this always happens - but they
seemed to pick us all at random expecting us to punch magic buttons on
a keyboard and produce this out of thin air.  They were selling the
product way before we came on board, and when we did, we had no
software, training or direction.  We are having to scramble to meet
these unrealistic deadlines and do job functions I never imagined.
Then when we do get some data (which we are having to get from outside
sources and it is never clean) we have to do the project management and
coordinate with random companies to get some kind of testing and data
validation done.  Nobody here ever validates it - they let the
customers point out inconsistencies or data errors.  We do our best but
we have limited knowledge of healthcare claims and and we don't have
time to do it since we are already working 12+ hours a day and weekends
for no pay.

A couple of us have been sent to meet with state officials and business
partners and we are forced to beg for their data to be provided to us
for free.  When they ask us - why should we send our sensitive data?
or Will we be compensated?  - we have to then become salespeople trying
to sell our product's merit to them and we don't have the authority to
promise anything.  They've sent us as representatives to meet with top
level mgmt at other companies, and they wonder why they've been sent a
programmer as a representative and not presented with a slick marketing
dude or important manager with an expensive suit who can become their
golfing buddy.  And we've been asked to draw up - LEGAL contracts. They
never thought to hire a legal specialist and we are handling very
sensitive healthcare information!  Then they ask us to do it!

Yeah, in all my extensive training that I should have been keeping up
with my nonexistent free time and paying for with my own gigantic
salary I don't think that Business Law and Contract Negotiation would
have been covered.  Our data analysts and business design team asked us
to display sensitive procedure codes that - uh, no lawyer here but I
think could get us sued.  I don't want to get too into this - but the
purpose of our project is to function as a kind of data clearinghouse
of information that if it isn't handled properly, and isn't properly
secure will get us in deep doo doo!

I could b*ch about this for days, but I'm trying my best to remain
positive.  I appreciate your supportive comments because I know it
seems unreal to people who haven't had to work in this type of
environment.  I know you probably have a lot of experience with it.  I
have to go in every day and bluff my way into this when I don't have
experience or confidence it will work, even as a business idea.  As a
consultant who is only asked to do the technical end of things, get it
implemented, then leave with a nice paycheck I wouldn't be so worried.
But as a regular company peon, I know first hand that when the
consultants put something in without proper planning or forethought,
we'll be the ones here at midnight in our cubes fixing what didn't go
right.  We'll be the ones called in to management's offices being
grilled as to why we didn't deliver the product on time.  We'll be the
ones having to answer questions from the users as to - why didn't you
do this - or why didn't anybody validate the results?

All in all this would have been a great experience if we nerds weren't
having to do everything.  I'd like to go to meetings business lunches
and trips but unfortunately I don't have time because 4 of us are
having to figure the entire thing out.  Then we get to hear management
talk about how stressed out they are because they've had to work
overtime to deal with a problem.   When there's a question, a real
question, guess who has to answer it.  When there are lights on in the
building at 2 am, chances are it's not going to be management or the
business unit on the 1st floor, but us nerds who are seated in the
basement tucked behind another department.  When we do have questions
they float around as wisps of a general atmosphere of whatever until
somebody determines there is a crisis and we have to fix it - right
away!  The experience and knowledge that I am gaining is how to deal
with a dysfunctional company and keep my paycheck coming while not
getting any positive feedback or direction.  If it goes well, then I am
sure our management will be compensated very well and we will get a
nice positive sentence on our annual review on how we managed our time
and maybe if we are lucky a $50 honeybaked ham certificate (that's what
we got for doing an Amisys conversion from HP3000, we were the first
company to do it in 9 months under budget and we got a turkey for our
700+ hours of overtime).  If it doesn't, then you probably can all
guess who is going to be the turkey then.

Think happy thoughts!  Be positive!  after all, it is all my fault for
not being a magician because I forgot to get the necessary skill set
for working with the impossible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T02:43:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e19sd5$3k$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
Holly <anderschwan@hotmail.com> wrote:
>Thank you so much for that - you seem to understand my ramblings and
>see the big mess for what it is.

I can't say that I do that... what I can say is that I read what you post 
about your situation and I relate it to situations I have seen, myself.

[snip]

>But most of us feel that we
>aren't being given the resources, training or even the time that we
>need to do this correctly.

All of you are wrong, of course, because none of you has The Big 
Picture... or so someone in a corner office says, right?

[snip]

>The way
>my manager, who I do like by the way, he's a great guy also being asked
>to do things outside his scope, explained it to us was that this was a
>big challenge and that it wasn't a secure job.

Nothing in life is certain, including this statement... that having been 
said, it is common, in the World of Investments, for rate of return 
(reward) to be proportional to risk.  What rewards have you been offered 
besides 'something really good'?

>Some of us asked if
>we'd get a new title, more money or overtime maybe or a bonus if it
>went well - but we were told that since this is a new company, we would
>be expected to keep the same benefits title and pay that we have now,
>but there would be "opportunities".

Employees at startups are frequently offered stock options... or so it 
used to be.

>So maybe that will happen - or
>what is more likely is that they will get amnesia when it comes time
>for review and I will get the standard 4% raise to keep up with
>inflation and they will talk about how the budget is tight and this is
>generous, blah blah blah.

When working for a company that is listed on a stockmarket it is an... 
interesting thing to come into a review and respond to it with readings 
from the Annual Report.

'Budget is tight?  How can that be, here it says that things are *so* much 
better than they were last year.'

[snip]

>We do our best but
>we have limited knowledge of healthcare claims and and we don't have
>time to do it since we are already working 12+ hours a day and weekends
>for no pay.

That's the first thing that has to stop, period.  To expect you to give 
work for which you are not compensated and for which there is no 
clearly-agreed-upon standard of compensation is as ethical as expecting to 
be compensated for time you have not worked.

Falsifying a timesheet is a firing offense in most companies I know of; 
they get rid of you if you steal from them.  For what reason(s) should 
they expect you to allow them to steal from you?

[snip]

>They've sent us as representatives to meet with top
>level mgmt at other companies, and they wonder why they've been sent a
>programmer as a representative and not presented with a slick marketing
>dude or important manager with an expensive suit who can become their
>golfing buddy.

My bet is that your bosses know this; they get feedback from the people 
with whom you meet.

>And we've been asked to draw up - LEGAL contracts. They
>never thought to hire a legal specialist and we are handling very
>sensitive healthcare information!  Then they ask us to do it!

Make sure the contracts you draw up include provisions for paying you and 
the team large amounts of money no matter what happens.

[snip]

>I could b*ch about this for days, but I'm trying my best to remain
>positive.

You are being remarkably positive, considering what you say you are 
subjected to.  It is situations like this which caused me, decades on 
back, to live the life of a consultant/contractor/hired gun.

[snip]

>But as a regular company peon, I know first hand that when the
>consultants put something in without proper planning or forethought,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>do this - or why didn't anybody validate the results?

No matter what goes wrong you are allowed to blame Stuff Going Wrong on 
two things:

1) 'It's the consultant's fault.'

2) 'We wanted to but we didn't have the budget.'

[snip]

>The experience and knowledge that I am gaining is how to deal
>with a dysfunctional company and keep my paycheck coming while not
>getting any positive feedback or direction.

Bingo.

>If it goes well, then I am
>sure our management will be compensated very well and we will get a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>700+ hours of overtime).

Wait a minute... you've had them do this to you before and you're allowing 
it to happen again?

I take back everything I said.  You deserve what you allow them to give 
you; remember the adage which addresses fooling someone twice.

The next time something comes up you might consider what your response 
would be to the following interchange between You and A Boss:

Boss: 'We need this done right away, now Now NOW!'

You: 'Sorry, I have other plans for this evening... but you know, they 
might be adjusted; what benefit would there be in my doing so?'

Boss: 'Uhhhhh... you'd get to keep your job.'

You: 'I asked 'what benefit?''

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T15:20:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49rcrmFq49aiU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
> Thank you so much for that - you seem to understand my ramblings and
> see the big mess for what it is.  I can't help being worried because
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> need to do this correctly.

And you are right. But who will suffer if you do nothing?
>
> I may not have a lot of experience, I don't have a computer science
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> generous, blah blah blah.

You have every right to be sceptical. But you really should not let these 
people decide YOUR future. You have demonstrated adaptability, tenacity and 
capability. These are very valuable skills. To you and the company. Invest 
in yourself. Do the tutorials. Wheter you go or stay, whether they deliver 
on theior promises or they don't, you will be better off.  YOU are 
responsible for the condition YOU are in!
>
> I want to be positive but I just don't have the confidence that it will
> work.

Why not? Sure, what they are trying to do may not work, but that really 
isn't your problem. There is no need for you to lack confidence. Look what 
you already achieved...


> There are so many things that people haven't thought of that
> they should have.  I don't know why this always happens - but they
> seemed to pick us all at random expecting us to punch magic buttons on
> a keyboard and produce this out of thin air.

It's because they really don't understand what is involved and have never 
done it. Unfortunately it is an all-too-prevelant (not just in the USA) 
attitude, where people who know they don't understand the detail simply 
write it off as "Oh the techies can do that... I don't trouble myself with 
the details..." and treat people who write code as if they were a lower life 
form.  Don't react to it. Just do your job. Use their facilities to test 
stuff as you learn it, and improve your skill base until you have a better 
chance of marketing it to a more perceptive company.



> They were selling the
> product way before we came on board, and when we did, we had no
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Stop doing it!  You are allowing bad management to enforce insane practices 
on you. Would you really be any worse off if they fired you? Why work for 
nothing when you could be learning for nothing?

> A couple of us have been sent to meet with state officials and business
> partners and we are forced to beg for their data to be provided to us
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Be very careful here.  You would do well not to get involved in aything that 
may have a shady legal basis. Try and get advice.

> Yeah, in all my extensive training that I should have been keeping up
> with my nonexistent free time and paying for with my own gigantic
> salary I don't think that Business Law and Contract Negotiation would
> have been covered.

OK, I take your point :-)  However the web site I gave you is free.


> Our data analysts and business design team asked us
> to display sensitive procedure codes that - uh, no lawyer here but I
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Or, why didn't anybody blow a whistle when they saw the whole thing was 
shaky?

> All in all this would have been a great experience if we nerds weren't
> having to do everything.  I'd like to go to meetings business lunches
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> basement tucked behind another department.

So why are you (collectively) allowing this?


>When we do have questions
> they float around as wisps of a general atmosphere of whatever until
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Feeling sorry for yourself will not solve the problem. It isn't sympathy you 
need it is action.

There are things you can do, for yourself, and together with your 
colleagues. If you all decide you WON'T work at 2 am then who will make you? 
If you don't deliver on time and are called to account, produce a log of 
what was required, the work done on it and why it isn't on time. Show the 
fact that management decisions were changed or not forthcoming.  Show the 
fact that you were expected to do things you had no training for. Will they 
fire all of you? That won't get them their system.

Would you be any unhappier if you were looking for another job? Think about 
it.

Management need you a lot more than you need them. That is why they 
endeavour to make the perception that they have power over you. They don't. 
(And good managers know this; they encourage and persuade rather than coerce 
and bully, and good managers don't set their teams up for failure by 
expecting them to do things way outside their brief.)

This company sounds like a place that is "best avoided".

I hope things get better for you.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T22:34:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49uqmpFqluc2U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <49rcrmFq49aiU1@individual.net> <1144563679.617291.152860@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <e1aq81$7r1$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
 

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e1aq81$7r1$1@reader1.panix.com...
>
> In article <1144563679.617291.152860@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It depends... I have actually worked for no recompense whatsoever when I 
felt the cause was worthwhile. :-)

However, you are right that I have never done that for a profit making 
concern.

I've also found (and it is quite bizarre) that the more you charge, the more 
credibility you seem to get!

I can think of several occasions where I have been asked to investigate and 
report (and charged an upper market rate for doing so, but still 
considerably less than Arthur Andersen or IBM...), and I found that what 
they had already been told by their existing staff was right on the button. 
They wouldn't accept it from someone earning $x a week, but they would from 
someone earning the same $x per day... I guess they get what they deserve...

Of course, it doesn't always work that way.  Sometimes (especially when I 
come up with answers that are unpalatable or not what they want to hear), 
what I tell them is ignored or filed and forgotten. I can think of a classic 
where I was asked to cost a project and the answer was not what was wanted 
(believe it or not, I was coming in too cheap!). I did it again, using a 
different method and it was within 5% of my original ballpark. Still 
unacceptable, so I did a detailed cost and got the same result. They 
continued to say the project would cost 2.5 times what I had shown it would 
cost, because it was politically expedient to make it look expensive.

Many, many, many years ago, one of the first contract jobs I ever did was to 
assess some proposals for new hardware that had been given in answer to a 
RFP circulated to the major vendors. I took this job very seriously and went 
the 'extra mile'. I even wrote benchmark programs to test the various 
systems being proposed. I presented my report, received the agreed payment 
and was pretty happy. Three months later I was having a beer with someone 
from that shop and asked how the new X machine was going. He smiled and told 
me they hadn't bought that. I was stunned because it was a quantum leap 
above the other contenders. It turns out they purchased the one that came in 
fourth or fifth because the managing director's wife had a brother who 
worked for that company selling computers.

I was so incensed I could've spat tacks. I phoned up an ex-Boss of mine whom 
I had great respect for and who was like a mentor to me, and told him the 
story. He agreed to meet for a drink and discuss it. When we met I angrily 
recounted the whole story again. He listened patiently then said: "Did you 
get paid?"  I agreed I had been. "In full?" Yes. "Then why are you upset?" I 
began to rant again about the extra effort, the benchmarks, the attention to 
detail, and all for nothing. They chose the wrong option.  "No, it wasn't 
for nothing. You got paid. You were asked to advise them and you did. To the 
very best of your ability. They decided not to take your advice. That's 
their right. You're not God. They don't HAVE to do what you say, even if it 
is the best course of action. Now stop acting like a Prima Donna and buy 
another round..."

I have never become unwrapped when my advice is ignored since that day, and 
I have never forgotten that conversation. (It took place in 1970 so you can 
see what an impression it made on me :-))

Finally, all rates I charge are subject to negotiation. I give two 
guarantees:

1. It will be less than if you got someone from one of the major 
consultancies to do it. (But it still won't be cheap; Doc was right... :-))

2. I guarantee due diligence and satisfaction. If you don't get what I 
promised you, by when I promised it, I'll give you your money back. (So far, 
I have never had to do this but I came very close on one occasion, and it 
would have meant financial ruin for me.  I made it with just hours to spare, 
after 9 months. These days I am VERY careful about what I promise,  and I 
carry insurance I never used to... :-).

Pete.




>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ New project without training or tools was Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2kji32plr83jnaga0bm7jb0sid10hkn9nn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 8 Apr 2006 08:49:12 -0700, "Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com> wrote:

>> snip
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>> more snipped

After reading this, if I were in your position I would be nervous
enough to talk to a lawyer.  The lawyer should know the privacy
protection laws such as the United States HIPPA and the Canadian
PIPEDA acts and practice in the area of employee responsibility and
legal accountability.  It sounds like you could be in a mess if there
are security breaches.  

Security is a zoo under the best of circumstances but in this case,
abandoning ship might be the smartest thing.  While Windows can be
made fairly secure, this requires good configuration knowledge and
good application design including prevention of buffer overruns.  The
same is true to a greater or lesser extent on the other platforms
including z/OS.  The problem with Windows is that even XP Pro defaults
to being fairly open while the other systems have more paranoia in
their defaults.   

A security expert must be on your team as must an expert in HIPPA law
and related laws to your project.  Both might be part time depending
on the scope of the project.  Code on any platform should be paranoid
and validating information and given the nature of the information you
are talking about this is doubly true.  You may be on the line since
you represented your organization to other organizations.  I second
Pete's posting about cutting back your hours and other points that he
made in that posting but feel that the problems he is addressing pale
in comparison to the fact that this application seems like one that is
headed for a major security and privacy breach.       

ls
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2006-04-13T08:20:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144941623.941638.225190@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144511352.605460.84890@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

Holly wrote:
> Thank you so much for that - you seem to understand my ramblings and
> see the big mess for what it is.  I can't help being worried because

Your problem is not confined to just your organization but I am sure
that most of us have at some time experienced similar problems.

Some advice/comments from an aged cynic:

1. Other people employed by your new employers may visit
comp.lang.cobol and may be able to identify you from your postings. You
could get into deeper water (dismissal for unprofessional conduct) than
you are already in. Please desist or be guarded about what you say.

2. Your problem is insoluble ( See the game "Why don't you, yes but..."
in "The Games That People Play" by Eric Berne) so all you can do is do
your best. There is no magic-bullet solution that any of us can come up
with to help you but we can make sympathetic noises and maybe,
occasionnally, profer advice.

3. I don't need to read between the lines to know that your project has
broken all of the rules and is doomed to failure. You've already
high-lighted managerial, platform, language, training and
lack-of-experience problems. IIRC, stage one of any project is to
identify the scapegoat. Make sure that it is not YOU. Cover your back.

4. 12 hour days, 7 days a week will kill you. You will not be any more
productive and, ultimately, any design/code produced under those
circumstances will be buggy. I know, I watched a high-pressure project
be put in by a development team and then had to spend the next six
months getting it working whilst the developers went off sick or on to
new contracts.

5. Working every hour that the Flying-Spaghetti Monster sends will not
result in a successful implementation and your efforts are unlikely to
be noticed or rewarded. As a general rule of thumb (I expect DD to
counter this) any project requiring unlimited unpaid overtime will
fail. I once had a conversation with a man who said, having just
refused to work on the fourth weekend, that he would not be remembered
for having worked 26 days straight but for the fact that he needed time
to himself on the fourth weekend.

6. Look for another job. When the project fails you should be ready to
jump ship (rats are both wise and beautiful animals). Try to avoid
being tarred with the brush of failure.

You and Pete Dashwood might find the following interesting or maybe
amusing:

Beating a Dead Horse
Dakota tribal wisdom says that when you discover you are riding a dead
horse, the best strategy is to dismount. However, in business we often
try other strategies with dead horses, including the following:

1. Buying a stronger whip.
2. Changing riders.
3. Say things like, "This is the way we have always ridden this horse."
4. Appointing a committee to study the horse.
5. Arranging to visit other sites to see how they ride dead horses.
6. Increasing the standards to ride dead horses.
7. Appointing a tiger team to revive the dead horse.
8. Creating a training session to increase our riding ability.
9. Comparing the state of dead horses in todays environment.
10. Change the requirements declaring that "This horse is not dead."
11. Hire contractors to ride the dead horse.
12. Harnessing several dead horses together for increased speed.
13. Declaring that "No horse is too dead to beat."
14. Providing additional funding to increase the horse's performance.
15. Do a Cost Analysis study to see if contractors can ride it cheaper.
16. Purchase a product to make dead horses run faster.
17. Declare the horse is "better, faster and cheaper" dead.
18. Form a quality circle to find uses for dead horses.
19. Revisit the performance requirements for horses.
20. Say this horse was procured with cost as an independent variable.
21. Promote the dead horse to a supervisory position.


A big corporation recently hired several cannibals.
"You are all part of our team now", said the HR rep during the
welcoming briefing.
"You get all the usual benefits and you can go to the cafeteria for
something
to eat, but please don't eat any of the other
employees".
The cannibals promised they would not.
Four weeks later their boss remarked, "You're all working very hard,
and I'm quite
satisfied with you. However, one of our secretaries has disappeared. Do
any of you
know what happened to her?"
The cannibals all shook their heads no.
After the boss had left, the leader of the cannibals said to the
others, "Which one of
you idiots ate the secretary?"



21 Rules of Executive Combat
1. If the enemy is in range, so are you.

2. Incoming fire has the right of way.

3. Don't look conspicuous, it draws fire.

4. There is always a way.

5. The easy way is always mined.

6. Try to look unimportant, they may be low on ammo.

7. Professionals are predictable, it's the amateurs that are dangerous.


8. The enemy invariably attacks on two occasions:
	a. when you're ready for them.
	b. when you're not ready for them.

9. Teamwork is essential, it gives them someone else to shoot at.

10. If you can't remember, then the claymore is pointed at you.

11. The enemy diversion you have been ignoring will be the main attack.


12. A "sucking chest wound" is nature's way of telling you to slow
down.

13. If your attack is going well, you have walked into an ambush.

14. Never draw fire, it irritates everyone around you.

15. Anything you do can get you shot, including nothing.

16. Make it tough enough for the enemy to get in and you won't be able
to get out.

17. Never share a foxhole with anyone braver than yourself.

18. If you're short of everything but the enemy, you're in a combat
zone.

19. When you have secured an area, don't forget to tell the enemy.

20. Never forget that your weapon is made by the lowest bidder.

21. If it's stupid but it works, it isn't stupid.



The 10 Rules of Management


You're always right, even when you're stupid

The physical laws of time and space were meant to be broken.

The problem is not a lack of resources, it's a lack of meetings.

When in doubt, ask for status reports.

If you're talking, you're communicating.

Low morale is caused by character flaws in your staff.

If people can complete a project in 10 days, then one person can
complete the project in one day.

Teamwork is when other people do your job for you.

Employee illness is a manifestation of laziness.

Abuse is a form of recognition; and recognition is what every employee
wants.



Office morals

Lesson number one ... A crow was sitting on a tree, doing nothing all
day. A small rabbit saw the crow, and asked him, "Can I also sit like
you and do nothing all day long?" The crow answered: "Sure, why not."
So, the rabbit sat on the ground below the crow, and rested.

All of a sudden, a fox appeared, jumped on the rabbit and ate it. Moral
of the story is: To be sitting and doing nothing, you must be sitting
very high...

Lesson number two ... A turkey was chatting with a bull. "I would love
to be able to get to the top of that tree," sighed the turkey, "but I
haven't got the energy." "Well, why don't you nibble on some of my
droppings?" replied the bull. "They're packed with nutrients." The
turkey pecked at a lump of dung and found that it actually gave him
enough strength to reach the first branch of the tree. The next day,
after eating some more dung, he reached the second branch. Finally
after a fornight, there he was proudly perched at the top of the tree.
Soon he was promptly spotted by a farmer, who shot the turkey out of
the tree.

Moral of the story: Bullshit might get you to the top, but it won't
keep you there.

Lesson number three ... When the body was first made, all the parts
wanted to be Boss. The brain said, " I should be Boss because I control
the whole body's responses and functions." The feet said, " We should
be Boss as we carry the brain about and get him to where he wants to
go." The hands said, " We should be the Boss because we do all the work
and earn all the money."

And so it went on and on with the heart, the lungs and the eyes until
finally the asshole spoke up. All the parts laughed at the idea of the
asshole being the Boss. So the asshole went on strike, blocked itself
up and refused to work. Within a short time the eyes became crossed,
the hands clenched, the feet twitched, the heart and lungs began to
panic and the brain fevered.

Eventually they all decided that the asshole should be the Boss, so the
motion was passed. All the other parts did all the work while the Boss
just sat and passed out the shit!

Moral of the story: You don't need brains to be a Boss - any asshole
will do.



Unfortunately, too much of the following is true:
The Rules of Work

The first 90% of a project takes 90% of the time, the last 10% takes
the other 90% of the time.

If you can't get your work done in the first 24 hours, work nights.

A pat on the back is only a few centimeters from a kick in the butt.

Don't be irreplaceable, if you can't be replaced, you can't be
promoted.

It doesn't matter what you do, it only matters what you say you've done
and what you're going to do.

After any salary raise, you will have less money at the end of the
month than you did before.

The more crap you put up with, the more crap you are going to get.

You can go anywhere you want if you look serious and carry a clipboard.


Eat one live toad the first thing in the morning and nothing worse will
happen to you the rest of the day.

When the bosses talk about improving productivity, they are never
talking about themselves.

If at first you don't succeed, try again. Then quit. No use being a
damn fool about it.

There will always be beer cans rolling on the floor of your car when
the boss asks for a ride home from the office.

Keep your boss's boss off your boss's back.

Everything can be filed under "miscellaneous."

Never delay the ending of a meeting or the beginning of a cocktail
hour.

To err is human, to forgive is not our policy.

Anyone can do any amount of work provided it isn't the work he/she is
supposed to be doing.

Important letters that contain no errors will develop errors in the
mail.

If you are good, you will be assigned all the work. If you are really
good, you will get out of it.

You are always doing something marginal when the boss drops by your
desk.

People who go to conferences are the ones who shouldn't.

If it wasn't for the last minute, nothing would get done.

At work, the authority of a person is inversely proportional to the
number of pens that person is carrying.

When you don't know what to do, walk fast and look worried.

Following the rules will not get the job done.

Getting the job done is no excuse for not following the rules.

When confronted by a difficult problem you can solve it more easily by
reducing it to the question, "How would the Lone Ranger handle this?"

No matter how much you do, you never do enough.

The last person that quit or was fired will be held responsible for
everything that goes wrong.

A hand raised hesitantly, to which the leader of the cannibals
continued, "You fool!!!
For four weeks we've been eating Managers and no one noticed anything,
but 
noooooo, you had to go and eat someone important!"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T14:49:54+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49rb2nFpo1ooU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not at all. And it wasn't meant to wound, either. The windows Operating 
System has been around now for nearly 20 years; I built my first web pages 
in 1995  after teaching myself HTML in an afternoon and using Notepad as an 
editor. And I was a late starter... there were already 16 million people 
accessing the web every day when I first decided it might be a good idea to 
learn something about it.

Education is NOT the responsibility of your management. (Sure, it's great if 
they assist you to get it, but in the end it is down to you.)   Education is 
part of your personal growth path. It never stops, and when you decide you 
won't or can't learn something unless someone pays you to, you might as well 
die.


> [snip]
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Executive goal... and by her description she seems correct.

I'm not supporting her management here; but, having heard only one side of 
the story, I'm not closing my mind either. It certainly would appear that 
this is far from good management, but, for me at least, the jury is still 
out.

As I have mentioned here many times, you don't have to suffer bad 
management, and if you let bad management stunt your growth then you are 
foolish. And I am not advocating a worker's revolution, either. Everything I 
want to say about this is covered in the thread "Management Wars" easily 
extractable from GOOGLE Groups.

YOU are responsible for the condition YOU are in. Not your wife, your 
family, the governement, or your employer. If you don't have skills to meet 
the challenges required of you, then either change your job or hit the books 
(or the Web). I did give Holly a web link that will enable her to get the 
knowledge she needs, in the time she needs it. If she simply sits back and 
says: "Well, the company aren't paying me to do this, so I won't..." who 
will suffer in the long run?



> That does not
> mean it cannot be done, of course - I do my best work after I rear back
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is not completed.

As a manager, I could exercise the option to simply employ someone who DOES 
have the necessary skills. (assuming I had the budget to do that, of course. 
However, if I had the budget to do that, I probably also have the budget to 
get people trained...)  I actually agree with your last sentence; it IS the 
responsibility of the manager if the task is not completed, but HOW and WHAT 
he does to get it completed is entirely down to him. If he wants to keep 
existing staff, but recognises they may not have a the necesary skills, then 
he has only two options:

1. Assist them or encourage them to get those skills.
2. Employ someone else who CAN do it.

Both of these options are financially unattractive to the manager, but he 
has to do SOMETHING and option 1 is certailny the lesser of the two evils. 
(There... even without considering the moral advantages, I have made a case 
as to WHY corporations SHOULD invest in their people... :-))

Actually, getting back to Holly's education for a minute, I would urge her 
to educate herself for all the reasons outlined above. It is down to her to 
make herself more valuable, and this then gives her the option to move to 
another job where her work in educating herself will be recognised.
>
> [snip]
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>
Bypass them. Get your own book, spend your own time on the web, and, when 
you have the skills, move to a more perceptive company.
>>
>>OK. Now listen up. What you need to learn CAN be learned in the time you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> be disappointed'.

With the utmost resect for your Paternal Grandfather, Doc, his homily may be 
flawed. I don't use myself as a comparative as a matter of course (on this 
occasion it is the only first hand experience I can draw on to show that 
what I am suggesting can be done, and it was offered not in a spirit of 
"look at me", but rather, "if I can do this, you certainly can...". It was 
meant to encourage, not intimidate), and, on the rare occasions when I do 
use myself as a comparative, I have NEVER been disappointed. :-)

> To apply this to the present situation: it seems as
> though the managers here are expecting the employees to respond to a
> situation as consultants do - you've been a consultant for a few years
> now, haven't you? - without paying them as consultants are paid.

Yes it does, and I agree that that is very wrong. However, if Holly DOESN'T 
do the skill acquisition the only one who will suffer will be her.

Actually, from what she has posted, It looks like the management problems 
here go MUCH deeper than just not recognising he need to train... I'd like 
to hear what the current management view in this place is :-)

From posts you have made here in the past, and from this post, plus the 
general murmuring of assent from people in the NG, it seems to me that US 
management is generally of a very poor quality. (After seeing George Bush on 
TV, I can't believe I actually wrote that... :-)).

I'm not suggesting that management exploitation of workers doesn't exist 
outside the US, but it has certainly been my experience that companies are 
HAVING to adopt much more enlightened attitudes in order to survive in an 
increasingly competitive marketplace,. Successful companies are realising 
that the most important single asset they enjoy is their people, and valuing 
and treating them accordingly. This attitude has to filter down from the 
top. I mentioned already that whereI am working currently the CEO is one of 
the best 'people persons' I have ever seen.  Aside from his legal and 
commercial background, his people skills alone would be enough to ideally 
suit him for the top job. Watching someone like this who genuinely cares 
about the welfare of the staff can restore your faith in management. Does he 
do it because he realises it can add value to his bottom line to have a 
happy work force, or does he do it because he is a decent human being and 
that is the way he generally treats people? I don't know (although I 
strongly suspect it is the latter) and it doesn't matter. The effect is the 
same.

Hopefully, people who are coming up through the ranks of US corporations 
will start to realise that the macho management image of the bully Boss is a 
totally unacceptable one in the 21st century and it will decline.

Meantime I would urge everyone (as I have consistently done here for years) 
to expand your skill set and don't let your education depend on your 
employer. Your growth is mch too important for that.

Pete.
```

```
In article <49rb2nFpo1ooU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e18f7j$qtv$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>news:1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...

[snip]

>>>> Well, that sucked because
>>>> none of us know jack about Windows networking, web development or
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

[snip]

>Education is NOT the responsibility of your management.

Once again, from the textbooks: 'The allocation, co-ordination and 
motivation of personnel and resources towards the accomplishment of a 
stated Executive goal is the responsibility of Management.'

Given that definition... if a manager wishes to allocate personnel with no 
training in a particular discipline towards the accomplishment of a stated 
Executive goal requiring training in that discipline then the 
responsibility for being trained lies on... the personnel?

>(Sure, it's great if 
>they assist you to get it, but in the end it is down to you.)   Education is 
>part of your personal growth path.

Not if the company is making money off of it.  If the company says 'From 
today on your job requires you to speak Swahili' then the circumstances of 
my employment changes and their previous contracts with me, both explicit 
and implicit, are null and void... or so the employment law in the USA has 
it, in a few states.

>It never stops, and when you decide you 
>won't or can't learn something unless someone pays you to, you might as well 
>die.

What I learn is one thing, what I am expected to learn in order for my 
employer to make money may be quite another.

[snip]

>> That does not
>> mean it cannot be done, of course - I do my best work after I rear back
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>he does to get it completed is entirely down to him.

Results-oriented, it used to be called... and I agree, if standing on 
one's head will get the job done then do it.

On the other hand... if standing on one's head will get the job done then 
a competent manager might hire someone who knows how to do this or send 
qualified personnel - a wheelchair-bound employee might not be the best 
choice - to head-standing school.

Simply bellowing 'from today onward you'll be expected to stand on your 
head!' might not be the best way to get the task done.

[snip]

>> Mr Dashwood, as my Sainted Paternal Grandfather - may he sleep with the
>> angels! - used to say, 'Never use yourself as a comparative, you'll only
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>flawed.

Nothing is absolutely true in all places and at all times, Mr Dashwood... 
including this statement.

>I don't use myself as a comparative as a matter of course (on this 
>occasion it is the only first hand experience I can draw on to show that 
>what I am suggesting can be done, and it was offered not in a spirit of 
>"look at me", but rather, "if I can do this, you certainly can...".

Gah... I call this 'The Drill Sergeant's Contradiction' because I first 
encountered it in Basic Training.  My Sergeant was wont to bellow 'I 
*always* give 110% and I expect everyone around me to give 110%, too!'... 
and I pondered 'But if everyone is giving 110% then there's nothing to 
differentiate you from everyone around you... you're all the same.'

[snip]

>> To apply this to the present situation: it seems as
>> though the managers here are expecting the employees to respond to a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yes it does, and I agree that that is very wrong.

'Oh my... we agree?  One of us must be wrong!', he exclaimed, Wildely.

>However, if Holly DOESN'T 
>do the skill acquisition the only one who will suffer will be her.

I am not sure what you're seeing as 'suffering' here, Mr Dashwood.

>
>Actually, from what she has posted, It looks like the management problems 
>here go MUCH deeper than just not recognising he need to train... I'd like 
>to hear what the current management view in this place is :-)

Why, they've got the Big Picture... and are all too Busy to explain it to 
anyone.

DD
```

```
allotted to them. Holly is neither trained nor sufficiently experienced
for her allotted tasks so, if they are ISO 9000 certified, then they
could certainly lose the certification.

> training in a particular discipline towards the accomplishment of a stated
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-13T16:34:09+00:00

- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <49rb2nFpo1ooU1@individual.net> <e19u1j$12f$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144944076.679486.236190@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1144944076.679486.236190@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>could certainly lose the certification.

Hmmmmm... an interesting point but I'm not sure; there was mention made 
about a subordinate and 'separate' organisation that was being set up to 
deal with the project and the certification of the Parent Company might 
not be jeopardised.

[snip]

>> >(Sure, it's great if
>> >they assist you to get it, but in the end it is down to you.)   Education is
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Presumably you get paid more for speaking Swahili?

I wonder who is doing the presuming, Mr Brazee... a few decades back I was 
working as a Lowly Warehouseman for a small company that had purchased the 
rights to manufacture a particular machine.  The owner asked the employees 
assembled in the lunch-room 'Does anyone know how to solder?'

I replied that I had been trained in such by the United States Air Force 
and my instructor insured my worked matched his own standards of NASA 
certification; I was told 'Oh good... then you can teach Jimmy here how to 
solder so he can deal with the new machine.'

I responded with 'Oh good... then you can pay me (a sum thrice my 
then-current salary) while I do so, that's what a soldering-instructor 
earns'... and the owner laughed, weakly, and I never heard about it again.

DD
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

> Good Luck!
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't mind helping people... :-)

Even the good Samaritan was not a Christian.
```
