- **In-Reply-To:** `<49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net>`

```
Thanks Pete!  I'm pretty stressed out so that advice helps me out a
lot.  I don't have much experience outside of the mainframe because I'm
used to working 60 -70 hours a week for no extra pay so any free time
or money for education isn't there.  The company doesn't actually train
us - they like to give us a website address where we are supposed to
use our "free time" to take an online tutorial.  I'm not sure that
qualifies me to do this type of major conversion in such a limited
amount of time. The free time I get is usually the 30 minutes I can
microwave a cup a soup and slurp it at my desk before somebody comes in
and asks a question so I may not be getting the full enrichment of a
cheesy online tutorial.

If you don't mind I will take you up on your offer to do the help the
needy and email you sometime.

For our implementation phase we just have to get our database over to a
windows server offsite and be able to load and extract data from
various sources.  I am also figuring out Networking, VPN, Citrix and
all that too so that is an added thing on my list due by the end of the
month.  We have Oracle help from my buddy who is doing this
unofficially to help us out but with 4 developers this is a bit much.

It does help to know about the web development but right now we are not
doing that portion of it - it is now done by another "data partner".
But we will have to know that because our mgmt doesn't want to depend
upon them to do our website and web side apps in the future, which
makes sense, if they purchase the tools and training and maybe hire a
couple other people.

We are glorified data processors, dbas and networking admins right now
which is pretty much outside my experience level.  We also have to do
contract negotiation, marketing, user testing, define business
requirements, and manage projects.  If I could only concentrate on
developing code it would be so great, but now I am still waiting on
them to purchase licenses and equipment.  That for our company could
take forever.  And I have to follow up with purchasing, management,
network support, dba's, offsite vendors and state government to get
stuff done or it tends to fall by the way side.  Just getting people to
do what they are supposed to do takes oodles of patience and constant
attention.  We could do some of it ourselves, but we aren't allowed the
permission.  So on it goes...

I'm bugging out a bit because getting decisions made or some sort of
system plan is very difficult in the current bureaucratic nightmare so
it always requires an eform, approval from ten different areas and
coordination with peeps at other departments and outside the company.
It's a crash course in system design but even though I'm bellyachin'
about it - it is fun.
I just worry it won't be right, so I don't want our heads chopped off
because they stretched us in too many areas at once.

It really does help to have your advice.  Thank you very much!  I wish
we had more time to be able to learn some of these skills.  Since we
have such limited time, I don't want it to be slapdash but I also can't
take months to learn new technologies because they haven't given us the
time.  It will probably be - as it usually is - what is cheapest and
easiest to do.  Now if I can get the software licenses, the system set
up and the data partners connected, that will be another challenge.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T18:09:42+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49rmpbFpagf2U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <1144518777.941565.260320@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:1144518777.941565.260320@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com...
> Thanks Pete!  I'm pretty stressed out so that advice helps me out a
> lot.  I don't have much experience outside of the mainframe because I'm
> used to working 60 -70 hours a week for no extra pay so any free time
> or money for education isn't there.


I understand. That is the first thing you need to address. Nobody has any 
chance of success while they are stressed out.

I'm going to break a personal rule here and offer some free advice :-). My 
qualifications to do this are that I am a highly experienced Project Manager 
(with a successful track record, all over the world) and have been a 
programmer  all of my working life (40 years); I understand the position you 
are in. The following should be considered simply suggestions; obviously, 
without being on site I can't know the full details of what is happeneing 
and where corrections need to be made, although it is quite apparent that 
there sare some serious management shortcomings manifesting themselves here.

Step 1. Stop the stress.

Talk to your colleagues and see if you can get everyone to agree to 40 hours 
a week.  At least for the next few weeks until the direction is clear and 
you all know where you are going. Make sure that even if they don't agree, 
you personally aim at these hours.

The difference between amateurs and professionals is that professionals pace 
themselves. Amateurs rush in and work huge hours then collapse and are 
unable to finish. Professionals recognize that it is not possible or 
desirable to get everything done on day one. They plan what they will 
accomplish, and review that plan each day.  Every day when you come in set 
yourself goals for that day. Stay focused and you will achieve them. Stop 
stressing when your goals for the day are accomplished. Tomorrow is another 
day.

Stop worryng about it, and make sure you get time out and proper sleep. 
Enjoy your weekends.

Step 2 - WHAT

It seems that there is no proper plan for this exercise and that is he first 
thing you need. Instead of just trying to use some rudimentary skills to get 
something in place, it would be much better to decide the WHAT before 
deciding the HOW.

If it is a web accessible database that is required, this is not too hard. 
Don't try and do it all at once.

WHAT do you need? Allocate time on the plan for designing building and 
debugging the database. Your ORACLE guy can do this and let you look over 
his shoulder. You DON'T need to have web pages or ASP working to do this. 
Once the DB is designed and built, it is a relatively simple exercise to put 
it on the web server and activate it from web forms using ASP.

Maybe you can be looking at ASP while the DB is being built? Focus on one 
thing at a time. You will need a container for your ASP and this will be a 
web page. So you need to get some idea about how to build one. HTML will do 
for a start. It really is easy (kids are doing it after school) and I don't 
believe you'll struggle with that. ASP takes it to the next level. If you 
get stuck, mail me (send me your ASP code so I can see you had a go at it 
:-))

The important thing is for you and your colleagues to devise and agree a 
plan BEFORE you start thinking about WHICH technical option, or HOW you 
intend to implement it, or WHEN it will happen.

Plan what you are doing based on 6 hours a day (That is the amount of an 8 
hour day that you are actually productive, if you are really focussed and 
don't allow interruptions). Show the steps required to achieve what is 
wanted, and allocate time and people to each of these steps. You may find MS 
Project useful for this.

When you have identified the required tasks (including acquiring the 
necessary knowledge) see if you can put milestones on your plan. These allow 
you to show Management that you are proceeding to plan and that stuff is 
being achieved. Typically you might have a milestone at the end of each 
major task:

TASK: Investigate DB requirements, design and build DB.
MILESTONE: DB is running in test and seems to work.
TASK:  Learn enough ASP to process data from web forms against the DB.
MILESTONE: First ASP routines tested on web server and worked!

STEP 3 - HOW

Now that what is required has been properly considered, some thought can be 
given to the options for attaining it. There are often a number of technical 
options (I think you mentioned converting the mainframe COBOL routines or 
using VBScript etc. These will need time to investigate (make sure you plan 
it...:-)).

From a technical point of view it is very tempting to let a preferred 
technical strategy drive the whole project.

Don't do this. Just because X company has a red hot tool for refactoring 
your COBOL into C# is not a reason to go with that option... It might 
actually be quicker to write the functionality into ASP pages than to commit 
to an expensive tool that will only be used once...for example.  If this is 
going to be web based it will require major changes to the existing COBOL in 
terms of the User Interface, so you will need time to look at existing 
inputs  and outputs  and consider how these fields can be encompassed into 
web pages. The functionality needs to be provided either by back end DB 
stored procedures or ASP routines. As it is currently in COBOL, conversion 
and refactoring options need to be looked at. For example, If the business 
logic was encapsulated into ActiveX/COM components (Fujitsu can do this 
fairly easily), you could have the session variables from the forms simply 
set properties in the components and this would be very similar to COBOL 
reading data.  (However, much and all as I would personally favour a 
component based solution, I think it might be a bit too ambitious for  you 
folks just at the moment. Stick to straightforward processing and either 
convert the COBOL to VBScript or JavaScript or scrap it completely and 
redevelop the functionality using ORACLE procedures that can be triggered by 
ASP.

STEP 4 - WHEN

Having mapped out the path, considered, discussed, and decided on the 
approach, it is then possible to say when it will be available. Note that 
this is NOT the same as saying: "It has to be ready by Thursday or we all 
lose our jobs." That kind of directive should be ignored. See above about 
amateurs and professionals. Just because your management behave like 
amateurs is no reason for you to emulate them. Point out that if they want 
this thing to happen, they must let you do it properly.  If they won't do 
this, then wash your hands of it, in writing.  Copy your Boss and his Boss 
and if necessary, his Boss too. You have nothing to lose because you are 
being set up for failure anyway. If someone asks you to account for why you 
are putting in writing that you won't be responsible for it, tell them. 
Encourage your colleahues to do likewise.  (Ideally, send a joint memo from 
the whole team to all concerned.) People don't have to suffer bad 
management; bad managers just make them think they do...

>  The company doesn't actually train
> us - they like to give us a website address where we are supposed to
> use our "free time" to take an online tutorial.

Well, you can't blame them for that. :-)

>  I'm not sure that
> qualifies me to do this type of major conversion in such a limited
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Maybe you need to be a bit more forceful about your time management. 
Politely decline the questioner with: "I'm really sorry, I can't take 
questions right now. I need to understand some stuff and I simply won't have 
time if I keep getting sidetracked.  Maybe you could ask my Boss or come 
back later? Perhaps we could schedule a half hour to discuss this, please 
check my calendar on Outlook.... or you could put in an email and I'll deal 
with it as soon as I can." (make sure your time is blocked out... )

You are responsible for how you manage your time. If someone demands that 
you give them time immediately, get them to sign a note saying they did this 
(have some ready prepared that simply require a signature and you can fill 
in how long it took). When you have to account for why things are late, 
produce these notes.

> If you don't mind I will take you up on your offer to do the help the
> needy and email you sometime.
>
Absolutely. I have code for accessing a DB on a remote web server using ASP. 
It isn't that hard. It is what you do with the information (retrieving it 
and presenting it on a web page is pretty trivial;  processing it and 
updating the DB with the results, is a bit more interesting...) that 
requires careful thought.

> For our implementation phase we just have to get our database over to a
> windows server offsite and be able to load and extract data from
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are getting too dispersed and spreading yourself too thinly. You don't 
need to be a network expert or understand Citrix or VPNs to access a remote 
database. There are specialists who will help you do that. Concentrate on 
getting your DB properly designed and processing (stored procedures, 
triggers, or SQL form ASP) that is more than enough to be going on with.

> It does help to know about the web development but right now we are not
> doing that portion of it - it is now done by another "data partner".
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK. Consider it a problem deferred... :-) Concentrate on your DB.

> We are glorified data processors, dbas and networking admins right now
> which is pretty much outside my experience level.  We also have to do
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> them to purchase licenses and equipment.

Your plan should show the purchase and availablity of the necessary licences 
as a critical milestone. If it doesn't happen by the milestone date, all 
bets are off. Tell them this. In fact, once you have the plan it, is good to 
identify the CSFs (critical Success Factors) and risks in a separate 
document, attached ot the plan. Circulate it to your management. If things 
go pear shaped, remind them they were warned of the necessity to obtain 
licences.

  That for our company could
> take forever.  And I have to follow up with purchasing, management,
> network support, dba's, offsite vendors and state government to get
> stuff done or it tends to fall by the way side.

You need a general manager. If you don't have one, get your Boss to take on 
that role. You are taking on far too much and almost guaranteeing failure. 
Just stop doing it. If you need to chase someone, send them an email and 
copy your Boss. When you are unable to proceed because you are waiting on 
some outside agency, remind your Boss that it needs attention then get on 
with your study... :-)

 Just getting people to
> do what they are supposed to do takes oodles of patience and constant
> attention.  We could do some of it ourselves, but we aren't allowed the
> permission.  So on it goes...
>
If you don't have permission, it is not your responsibility. Forget it. 
Holly, being responsible is admirable but you can have too much of a good 
thing. If you try to be responsible for the things that your managers and 
colleagues should properly be attending to, you create a number of 
undesirable effects:

1. They will never take responsibility for the things they should, because 
there is no necessity to.
2. You will fail and you will be blamed because you were responsible.
3.  You will stress out because you have more going on than you can 
reasonably handle.
4. Going to work will not be an eagerly anticipated event (and it should 
be... :-))

> I'm bugging out a bit because getting decisions made or some sort of
> system plan is very difficult in the current bureaucratic nightmare so
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> about it - it is fun.

Good. Keep that in mind. It is even more fun when you succeed at it...

> I just worry it won't be right, so I don't want our heads chopped off
> because they stretched us in too many areas at once.

They only stretched you because you allowed them to.

>
> It really does help to have your advice.  Thank you very much!  I wish
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> time.

Yes you can. You can learn as you go and you can manage your time better. 
Learn to say "No." nicely... :-)

 It will probably be - as it usually is - what is cheapest and
> easiest to do.  Now if I can get the software licenses, the system set
> up and the data partners connected, that will be another challenge.
>

Why did you accept this if you don't have time to do it? Manage your time 
better.

I realise you may find some of the above a bit hard. I honestly do 
sympathise with your situation but very often these things are self 
inflicted wounds. Remember that your managers need training too. Until they 
are capable, don't let them spill their incompetence onto you. Be 
professional, Work on developing your own skills and managing your own time. 
Be responsible only for the things you can manage and refuse to accept 
responsibility for the things you can't.

Good luck, and if you need practical help or clarification or explanation 
for some of the stuff you are learning,  post here or mail me privately.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T08:10:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k3pk32hvnnt82suevdu2c5u78379e1vran@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <49ovooFplfm9U8@individual.net> <1144518777.941565.260320@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <49rmpbFpagf2U1@individual.net>`

```
On Sun, 9 Apr 2006 18:09:42 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Step 1. Stop the stress.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>you personally aim at these hours.

I play golf.   I started only after acquiring a son-in-law who played
and I am still a poor player.

One of the top selling golf books is called _Golf is Not a Game of
Perfect_, and was written by a psychologist, not a golf pro.   

Golf is an interesting game to learn:

The harder you try, the worse you play.   So you have to try hard to
not try hard - but to still learn the right way to play.    One of the
most useful tools to have is a good forgetter.    After you hit a bad
shot, and you get ready to make a recovery shot - that first shot
needs to be all the way out of your mind (sort of like forgetting the
money you have put in a poker pot).      That shot is gone, you are
now playing a new game of "how to play this hole the very best you can
from this unusual starting point".

The time to remember your shot is when you evaluate your best strategy
for playing next time - which evaluation needs to be like a coach's
study of game films.

Also - you look at the lake when you decide what shot you're going to
make - then you forget that the lake is there when you actually make
your shot.   If you keep looking at the lake, it will draw the ball
in.    You've already made the decision and have a target, and know
your desired swing.   The lake doesn't matter any more.

These head trips are hard to do.

Tiger hits a bad shot, gets upset, and allows himself 10 steps to get
over his anger.   Of course he *is* self-employed, but like him, we
can make a bad situation worse by carrying it with us.   We've got to
leave it behind.

And everybody hits bad shot - whether they are our fault, or the rub
of the green.
```

- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

anderschwan@hotmail.com wrote:
> AMEN!  Dang it I was interested in the first article then this became a
> total bs session on religion - how does that happen?  Can people let
> religion aside for a minute and help a sister out ?
>

<big snip>

I use Fujitsu Cobol v3 on my pc and am more than happy with it.
However, I have not used it or any of the more recent versions
commercially so can not comment on how good it is for real work (others
on this group will comment).

If you opt for the convert to Java option then you will almost
certainly fail (more so than the big failure that your organization is
already heading for). At one place where I worked, the management hired
an all-new team with no CICS/Cobol experience and expected them to code
complex screens from the start. Eventually, they paid for a CICS
course. One week later some ashen-faced programmers arrived, junked
their previous three months work and re-wrote the entire system. Good
luck. Go for re-writing it all in Java. You will probably fail but (a)
you will have fun trying and (b) you will become instantly more
marketable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Go for re-writing it all in Java. You will probably fail but (a)
> you will have fun trying and (b) you will become instantly more
> marketable.

    If the (b) is your goal, then the .NET framework is getting pretty 
popular too. I believe Fujitsu makes a "COBOL for .NET" compiler. I have no 
experience with it, so I don't know how similar it is to "normal" COBOL. I 
know Microsoft has a "C++ for .NET" compiler (they call it "C++/CLI") and 
I'm told it's very much *not* like C++.

    - Oliver

Thanks Oliver - we have a .NET contingent amongst a few of us nerds,
but I don't think our management wants to pay for it, which is funny
because I am sure they spent plenty on gizmos to decorate their
offices.  We only have 5 developers and one is on maternity leave.
Some of our project analysts like the idea of .NET too, so we are
campaigning for it.
I've heard good things about fujitsu - I'll keep my fingers crossed
that they at least make a decision on what type of software to purchase
before they announce that the website is available.
What a joy!  At least it payz the billz..
```

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T17:17:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1e3v602vs@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <t9wZf.33152$K11.20760@clgrps12>`

```

In article <t9wZf.33152$K11.20760@clgrps12>, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> writes:
> 
>     If the (b) is your goal, then the .NET framework is getting pretty 
> popular too. I believe Fujitsu makes a "COBOL for .NET" compiler.

Micro Focus Net Express for .NET and the upcoming MF Studio 5 also
can target .NET.

> I have no 
> experience with it, so I don't know how similar it is to "normal" COBOL.

Depends on what you consider normal COBOL, I suppose, though I
haven't looked into the area in any great detail.

> I know Microsoft has a "C++ for .NET" compiler (they call it "C++/CLI") and 
> I'm told it's very much *not* like C++.

It's pretty much what you'd expect: C++ with restrictions to prevent
violating the .NET execution model and extensions to enable the .NET
features which aren't natively available.  It's more like C++ than
any other .NET language is.  If you write clean, modern C++, with
containers and iterators and the like rather than a bunch of low-
level mucking about with pointers, C++ .NET isn't terribly different.
It's the people who think C++ is "C plus some other stuff" who find
C++ .NET troubling.

Personally, my opinion is that any given piece of C++ code is rarely
much like any other piece of C++ code, so why quibble over the .NET
differences?

The *interesting* .NET language is F#, though. :-)
```

```
I wasn't sure anybody would reply since I wasn't presenting a religious
debate on the existence of whatever -  people here still think
creationism should be taught in schools.  Religious topics make my skin
crawl as I am surrounded by the thumping of multitudes of bibles.
There's a dude on the street corner shouting at people - I think he
called me a heathen.

I thought Java might be a bad idea, but you are right, at least it is
marketable experience!
What I'm really frightened of is that today I heard rumours of SAS.
Good Lawd Amighty!
They have no idea or direction of where this is going.  The only thing
they have is a big budget to spend, but so far they've only invested in
themselves (Treos, laptops, etc).  Did we nerds get anything - you can
probably guess not.  I didn't even get the cheesy vinyl day planner or
wooden logo emblazoned pen set.

Thanks so much!  I appreciate your time - it helped me vent a bit too!
- Holly
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** anderschwan@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T09:40:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144428035.393298.162910@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e163ij$pei$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <e163ij$pei$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
That was awesome, and yet, eerily similar!  We have 4
consultants/contractors (CC), 1 Senior Consultant or Team Lead (SC) who
has a TimeSheet Signer (TSS) with her own Corner-Cubicle Boss (CCB) and
COM Corner office Manager not to mention our COM2, who has a Director
and the CEO above that.  We are a "spin off" of a big bureaucratic
health care company, and we were told we would be "cutting edge" and
revolutionary.  Now when we are trying to actually get this shizzle
done, we are told to follow our old company's policies and procedures,
and use our old company's resources, but to still present the fact that
we are a separate and wholly owned subsidiary.
They've got me meeting with partners, networking, offsite server
admins, oracle dbas, software vendors and with our old company's users
not to mention state government.  I have yet to code one line, or even
to know what licenses they are willing to purchase.  They tell me on
the one hand to plan the architecture to fit the "new" vision, but then
I have my hands tied by all of these layers of bureaucratic bs.  And I
can't convey to the partners who have volunteered to send us their
company's data, that we have in a secure, offsite location that we are
still part of the old company, using the same resources at the old
company, because we are supposed to give the impression that we are
totally independent.

I have resolved Never Ever to join the dark side and go over to that
crazy world.  And now, they are dragging me in!

It helps to hear other people's stories, and yours was the bomb!
Thanks so much!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T10:19:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
>> anderschwan@hotmail.com wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> then it should win out with the kids every time.

1+1 = 2  is provable fact - evolution is not.  It's not the "theory of 
mathematics" - but, it is the "theory of evolution".

> If you want to lead
> your own children (or grand children[I had a new girl grandchild this
> week]) dpwn the garden path, then do it on your own.  And stop denying
> my children an education on what we do know unless your god is pandered to.

There are many reasons why my children aren't in government school - and 
this is one of them.  Seems to me, though, we taught creation 
*exclusively* in our government schools for longer than the theory of 
evolution has existed.  Why exclude the old in favor of the new?  Why 
not present both sides' viewpoints, and let the children understand?

This is one of the same problems I have with the pro-abortion argument. 
  They don't want both sides presented, and let people use their free 
will to make the decision they feel is right.  All they want to do is 
beat their chests and say "IT'S MY RIGHT!"

Notice that I didn't say evolution shouldn't be taught - I think that it 
should.  Kids (and, by extension, adults) should be knowledgeable in 
creation *and* the various forms of evolution.  Knowing both fosters 
greater communication among people, when they can understand the 
underlying views of those with whom they may disagree.

An uninformed choice is no choice at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

```
LX-i wrote:

> Donald Tees wrote:
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Nonsense.  Are you saying we only imagined all those fossils, and that
breeding of farm animals never happened? Survival of the fittest as the
main evolutionary force may be a theory, but it is not theory that
evolution actually happened. There is a huge body of factual data
available, and it is as provable as the fact that 1+1=2. Creationism has
no evidence whatsoever. It is based on the folklore of people with a
religion to sell, and a vested interest in selling it.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT Creation vs Evolution wasRe: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T21:37:27-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ro9j32pus174oei8qaps5bmr7n5h6ocm4v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET> <TIQZf.4367$pZ3.618887@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Sat, 08 Apr 2006 11:33:06 -0400, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>LX-i wrote:
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>religion to sell, and a vested interest in selling it.

Evolution may be part of the explanation but not all of it.  There
could be creation and then modification.  Some of the discontinuities
(splitting to sexual reproduction or external egg to gestation inside
a womb for example) are hard to explain if evolution as a gradual
change is the explanation.  Neither original creator nor the lack of
original creator can be proved.  Either explanation has major
philosophical problems.  Then belief in a creator says nothing about
the nature of the creator.  Thus I believe that a more critical look
at the various explanations is in order.   
>
>Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2006-04-13T07:42:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144939350.392018.252820@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<TIQZf.4367$pZ3.618887@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET> <TIQZf.4367$pZ3.618887@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Donald Tees wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Whereas 1+1=2 is a known and provable fact, there are mathematical
theories that are unprovable (see this month's edition for an article).

Before anyone reads the rant below: I am not getting at anyone in this
newsgroup (past or present) and the offensive language I use is to
express how deeply felt are my irreligious scientific beliefs.

<Angry scotsman mode> I have to admit that it really p***es me off to
have some jumped-up religious bigot announce that the theory of
evolution is unproven and, after all, is only a theory like creationism
or intelligent design. </Angry scotsman mode>

Creationism is not a theory; it is a story made up to explain the
inexplicable to people who lived at a time when the concept of an
almighty creator was as good as any other. If one wishes to consider
creationism and id as anything other than myths and stories, then it
must be as unproven unscientific theories. Creationism, and it's thinly
disguised bastard brother intelligent design, have no relevance in the
real world. A true religion would understand the need to modernise and
would junk the old rubbish when it became patently false, rather than
crucifying those who can see the light.

As an aside: Do creationists/intelligent designers refuse life-saving
medical treatments because they are only theories with no proof? I
believe that Mormons and Jehovah Witnesses do permit blood transfusions
in certain circumstances.

Finally, in the last week scientists have revealed fossils to the world
which go some way to bridge the gaps between sea-life and land-forms (a
fish capable of limited movement on land and with hand-like
articulating bones in their fins), and the one between
Australopithecines and the Homo genus.
```

> Whereas 1+1=2 is a known and provable fact,

It is only provable that it follows the definitions of its elements in
the particular set of mathematics.

Similarly 1+1=10 is a 'known and provable fact'.
```

our throats".

LX-i wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> > On Thu, 13 Apr 2006 18:45:52 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have taken part in a number of religious debates in CLC.
Unfortunately, demands upon my time mean that I have had to leave gaps
between sessions here. I can not remember what you wrote which wound me
up at the time (I was sober, BTW) but the string finally snapped.


> > Or that it's more admirable to be brave and have a position everybody
> > else disagrees with?
>
> His post just reminded me of some who walks up to a group of people and
> then says "me too!".

Yeah, me too. But you are out-numbered here. One original thing about
my post - I did express in no uncertain terms my feelings about the
creationist stance. I think no-one else has let rip over the years as
they are probably too polite and too considerate for your right to
believe as you feel. Creationists pasionately believe in their version
of the truth. I passionately believe in my version too. In former
times, people like me would have taken adherents to the old ways and
burned them at the stake (I reserve that option for the future).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-04-14T12:17:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1o3te02dvm@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144939350.392018.252820@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```

In article <1144939350.392018.252820@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>, "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> writes:
> 
> Whereas 1+1=2 is a known and provable fact, there are mathematical
> theories that are unprovable (see this month's edition for an article).

Unprovability is just the tip of the iceberg.

Gregory Chaitin has shown that the vast majority of mathematical
facts (*all* mathematical facts, to a first approximation :-) are
uncompressible: they can't be proven using less information than they
themselves contain, so they can't be proven in any useful sense.[1]

As Chaitin notes, mathematical incompleteness isn't strange - what's
unusual, statistically speaking, is that there are some elegant
theorems that can be proven from a given set of axioms.

(Kolmogorov Complexity demonstrates this from another angle: most
strings are "at least close to algorithmically random", as one source
puts it, due to the pigeonhole principle - there are more longer
strings than shorter strings.)


1. See
   http://www.cs.auckland.ac.nz/CDMTCS/chaitin/#UnderstandablePapers

I particularly like the UMass-Lowell version of "A century of
controversy":
   http://www.cs.auckland.ac.nz/CDMTCS/chaitin/lowell.html
```

LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>Donald Tees wrote:

[snip]

>> If you want to lead
>> your own children (or grand children[I had a new girl grandchild this
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>not present both sides' viewpoints, and let the children understand?

The Theory of Humours was taught for longer than the Germ Theory of 
Disease... break out the leeches!

>
>This is one of the same problems I have with the pro-abortion argument. 
>  They don't want both sides presented, and let people use their free 
>will to make the decision they feel is right.  All they want to do is 
>beat their chests and say "IT'S MY RIGHT!"

Not this again... Mr Summers, by this definition the Planned Parenthood 
movement does not present 'the pro-abortion argument'; as was pointed out 
to you in 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/569cc49e2dd8911e?dmode=source&hl=en>

--begin quoted text:

The onus probandi lies on the one making the assertion... but I'll give 
you a hint: if all that Planned Parenthood wanted to do was to, as you 
assert, 'whip up the emotions about their rights, and get them to kill 
that child' then they might wait until it was a child, not a fetus... and 
they might not give out information about contraception, which decreases 
the possibility that a fetus can develop... and they might not have, on 
their website, information about infertility, prenatal care and adoption.

That seems to be a pretty broad offering of choices.

--end quoted text

>
>Notice that I didn't say evolution shouldn't be taught - I think that it 
>should.  Kids (and, by extension, adults) should be knowledgeable in 
>creation *and* the various forms of evolution.

That kids should be knowledgable about creationism was never contested, Mr 
Summers; that *my* tax dollars should be spent teaching them what *your* 
religion teaches is 'The Way Things Were' was decided by the Supreme Court 
of the United States of America a few years back.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T14:59:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1drsk$n61$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <b7aj32leinptdnljoj0rkr03sknm3tpr5c@4ax.com> <e1ccfq$1p3$1@reader1.panix.com> <i8pk32dgk0ifcv299efe19hegds1mui6js@4ax.com>`

```
In article <i8pk32dgk0ifcv299efe19hegds1mui6js@4ax.com>,
Clark Morris  <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
>On Mon, 10 Apr 2006 01:30:34 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>done the google homework.

From http://www.bizbag.com/mencken/menkchiro.htm :

--begin quoted text:

For all I know (or any orthodox pathologist seems to know) it may be true 
that certain malaises are caused by the pressure of vagrom vertebrae upon 
the spinal nerves. And it may be true that a hearty ex-boilermaker, by a 
vigorous yanking and kneading, may be able to relieve that pressure. What 
is needed is a scientific inquiry into the matter, under rigid test 
conditions, by a committee of men learned in the architecture and plumbing 
of the body, and of a high and incorruptible sagacity. Let a thousand 
patients be selected, let a gang of selected chiropractors examine their 
backbones and deter mine what is the matter with them, and then let these 
diagnoses be checked up by the exact methods of scientific medicine.

Then let the same chiropractors essay to cure the patients whose maladies 
have been determined. My guess is that the chiropractors' errors in 
diagnosis will run to at least 95% and that their failures in treatment 
will push 99%. But I am willing to be convinced.

Where is such a committee to be found? I undertake to nominate it at ten 
minutes' notice. The land swarms with men competent in anatomy and 
pathology, and yet not engaged as doctors. There are thousands of 
hospitals, with endless clinical material. I offer to supply the committee 
with cigars and music during the test. I offer, further, to supply both 
the committee and the chiropractors with sound wet goods. I offer, 
finally, to give a bawdy banquet to the whole Medical Trust at the 
conclusion of the proceedings.

--end quoted text

DD
```

- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <b7aj32leinptdnljoj0rkr03sknm3tpr5c@4ax.com> <e1ccfq$1p3$1@reader1.panix.com> <i8pk32dgk0ifcv299efe19hegds1mui6js@4ax.com> <e1drsk$n61$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Mon, 10 Apr 2006 14:59:32 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>Let a thousand 
>patients be selected, let a gang of selected chiropractors examine their 
>backbones and deter mine what is the matter with them, and then let these 
>diagnoses be checked up by the exact methods of scientific medicine.

Some tests are hard to do ethically.   There was a test that involved
people opening up knees, making appropriate sound effects, but not
doing any real surgery once inside, and then telling the patients that
they cleaned out the stuff in there.

The result was that they healed just as well as the control group with
the conventional surgery.

But significantly better than those without any surgery at all.
```

healing to do, so you take it easy and follow his directions to
recover.

```
If you are going to bring abortion into this, then be prepared.  I'm
ready to burn my bra right now!

It's not that both sides aren't presented.  I don't like abortion and
think it is a terrible thing.  But I don't want government telling me
what I can and can't do to my own body.  Maybe you would understand
that it isn't just beating my chest but the fact that my best friend
was a victim of incest at 15 and it was a terrible choice she had to
make.  She tried to commit suicide and it wasn't until she was in the
hospital at the age of 32 that her parents finally understood what she
had been through.

have you ever felt the pain or the shame of something like that!  i
don't think you have. You shouldn't judge everyone the way you do!

If pro lifers really respect life - well then - they shouldn't vote in
favor of locking up people in camp Xray with no proof and caging them
like animals in the Bay - they shouldn't vote for government sponsored
torture - they shouldn't vote for wars that blow up innocent families
and rip apart civilizations - they shouldn't vote to allow the
government to electrocute or inject people on death row (especially
when some of them MAY be innocent) - the shouldn't vote to destroy the
environment by allowing corporations and industry to roll back life
saving conservation and antipollution laws - and they should actually
vote to use tax money to take care of the babies that are born to a
world where nobody cares about them and allows them to live in poverty
and abuse ...
instead of squandering our nonexistent tax payer money on debating
whether or not we should pray or cite a pledge of allegiance or call a
snack food freedom fries.

If you respect life then, you should respect all of it.

Sorry but that really bothers me.
```

- **References:** `<icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144516038.106036.49860@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>`

```
Holly wrote:
> If you are going to bring abortion into this, then be prepared.  I'm
> ready to burn my bra right now!

I'm not an abortion provider, but I think that's the wrong way to do it...

> It's not that both sides aren't presented.

They are?  Where?  Show me a site (or an organization) that presents 
*all* of the following...
  - abortion as an option
  - facts about the physical effects of abortion
  - facts about the emotional effects of abortion
  - adoption as an option
  - keeping the child as an option
  - postpartum depression resources
  - where to find community resources for abortion, adoption, and 
single/young mother support
  - the facts about fetal development (heart beat, brain waves, etc.).

I'll bet you won't find one.

(And, a side note on the physical effects - at the clinic in 
Chattanooga, TN, when patients had medical emergencies, the clinic did 
*not* call an ambulance - they called a taxi.  Honest?  I don't think so.)

> I don't like abortion and
> think it is a terrible thing.  But I don't want government telling me
> what I can and can't do to my own body.

What other "terrible thing" do you feel this way about?  And is "your" 
body the only one you care about?

> Maybe you would understand
> that it isn't just beating my chest but the fact that my best friend
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> had been through.

Did she have an abortion, or not have one?  I'm not sure which part of 
the argument you're trying to present here.

> have you ever felt the pain or the shame of something like that!  i
> don't think you have. You shouldn't judge everyone the way you do!

How do you know *I* am judging anyone?  In fact, I do not.  Sure, as you 
probably figured out, I'm pro-life.  But my biggest problem is the way 
the argument is phrased.

My statement to Mr. Tees (which is what I'm guessing you're replying to) 
simply stated that the pro-abortion crowd is scared of the facts 
regarding human reproduction.  The people who cry "science! science!" 
when you talk about evolution vs. creation cry "rights! rights!" when 
faced with the science of unborn children.

(DD - save your fingers - it's my message, my terms...)

And that's my biggest frustration with understanding some folks. 
They'll believe something as strained as evolution's answer to how we 
got here, but they don't want to know *anything* else about how children 
develop in the womb since the scientific knowledge we had in 1973.  And, 
it's what makes their allegiance to science seem weak.  We have so much 
more information on fetal development that we had back in 1973.  I 
really don't understand why more people aren't worried about what pain 
they may be causing their offspring, as well as the termination of their 
life.

It's also telling that we can't poll the aborted babies to see what 
their vote would be - and every single person who is for abortion is 
being quite ironic.

> If pro lifers really respect life - well then - they shouldn't vote in
> favor of locking up people in camp Xray with no proof and caging them
> like animals in the Bay - they shouldn't vote for government sponsored
> torture - they shouldn't vote for wars that blow up innocent families
> and rip apart civilizations

You'd rather wait for *them* to do that to *us*?

> - they shouldn't vote to allow the
> government to electrocute or inject people on death row (especially
> when some of them MAY be innocent)

YES!  I *LOVE* it when pro-abortion people say this.  Let's kill the 
innocent babies, and spare the guilty criminals.  :)  (You don't see 
that this is backwards?)

> If you respect life then, you should respect all of it.

All human life - and I do.  There are also times (war being one of them) 
that, regrettably, we have to take lives to save lives.

While fiction (and pretty long), the following link paints an 
interesting picture, based on historical documents, of what this world 
may look like if militant Islam is allowed to control the world. 
(There's also some language in here that I personally wouldn't use...)

http://www.dansimmons.com/news/message.htm

(Again, I certainly hope that none of this above is construed that I'm 
"judging *you*" - I'm simply showing some conflict that I perceive 
between the two sides.  I realize that any general statement may or may 
not apply to someone who shares that general belief.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (The "A" word - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T13:52:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144529571.003998.289290@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144516038.106036.49860@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <9fbaa$44380d27$45491d7a$8115@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
My point is pretty simple and can be boiled down to one thing.  If you
are pro-life than in my opinion you should respect all life.  If you
are anti-abortion for other reasons besides morality maybe that would
be different.  The pro life movement is realistically not just about
banning abortion, it's also about promoting evangelical religious
beliefs and legislating morality.  What about taking care of the
children that are already here first, who are dying due to warfare,
starving or dying of aids?  And if people are going to preach to me
about religious morality, then yes Jesus wouldn't have been for
abortion, but he also wouldn't have been for killing people no matter
what reason.  And I don't recall him discussing birth control being a
sin, but maybe I am wrong there.

The reality is people have to make painful choices sometimes and they
don't always choose the one that is easily defined in terms of black or
white - right or wrong.   If I was a victim of incest or rape, like my
friend who got an abortion because she couldn't tell anyone in her
family then I don't know what I would have done in her position.  She
had someone drive her 300 miles to another state at the age of 15 to
get it done.  It wasn't something she did without thinking and tearing
herself up about.  It also wasn't her choice to be repeatedly abused
and raped by her own grandfather so it's not the same as someone who
gets knocked up by their boyfriend at the prom in the backseat of a
car.  I don't think it should be freely available to whoever for
whatever reason, but there are different reasons and justifications for
what women may decide to do.

And we on the two sides are probably not going to understand this the
same way.  It's easier to make a moral decision on right or wrong when
you personally don't have to ever worry about it happening to you.
Just like it is easier to deal with the fact that killing people in a
war is either ok or not, when I don't have to see it or fight it
myself.   If we want to prevent abortions - then a better way than
banning it all together is to work on the issues that create the
problem.  To help the poor and disadvantaged, provide birth control and
free education and affordable healthcare, and when everything else
fails then find new ways to make the existing world better for
everyone.  (here comes the hippie music)   I just think it is so much
less effective to vote and campaign against something by making it a
crime than it is to actually go out and try to prevent what causes
those things in the first place.

that's all I can do today - I have to get away from the computer and go
outside while the sun is out.

ps Iraq is another topic that could be debated ad nauseum and I don't
think we would change each other's minds on it either.
    It has been interesting though to have this kind of debate.
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<1144529571.003998.289290@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144516038.106036.49860@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <9fbaa$44380d27$45491d7a$8115@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144529571.003998.289290@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Holly wrote:

[out of order quote]
 > that's all I can do today - I have to get away from the computer and go
 > outside while the sun is out.

This can get addictive and time-consuming.  I had to completely quit, as 
I was debating with 5 or 6 people at once, over nearly every topic out 
there.  (And on most, I was alone or almost alone.  I'm sure my 
detractors would like for me to infer something from that...)

I'm not sure how long you've been following this thread, but the 
religious part started when the Westboro Baptist Church (the group of 
people demonstrating at soldier's funerals) got brought up.  Just to be 
sure there was no ambiguity in my position, I took the main points from 
their terribly-named web site's front page and debunked them from the 
Bible, point by point.

For myself, personally, I was a ministerial major for a while and have 
preached several sermons.  Currently, I'm filling in on a week-to-week 
basis in a church here that is without a Minister of Music.  I'm a 
supporter of AAA Women's Services in Chattanooga, TN, a women's clinic 
that offers free ultrasounds to women in crisis pregnancies; when we 
lived there, my wife was a counselor there.  In our time there, we 
personally and financially supported a girl from California who had 
picked Chattanooga as the place to come to get an abortion.

After seeing the ultrasound, she decided to keep the baby.  She called 
her boyfriend, who did not know what she had done, and he moved to our 
city with her.  They received supplies from AAA, and we helped her (and 
her boyfriend) find a job.  My wife helped out a lot once the baby was 
born.  That child is 9 now, and the last we heard from them, they were 
still doing great.

No, that's not the choice everyone will make - but, from a "nowhere to 
go" spirit to a family of three, that was a transformation that she 
wouldn't have ever expected.  What brought it about?  Knowledge (through 
that window to her womb) and support (through my wife and other ladies 
who volunteer there).

So when I talk about being pro-life, for me anyway, it's not just a "you 
shouldn't" - I've put feet to my beliefs before, and I will again. 
You'll also not hear me condemn someone who has gotten an abortion - 
condemnation isn't my place.  I'll do my best to assist them past the 
guilt and share God's forgiveness with them.  (As a man, a lot of times 
that comes better from a woman - so my support is often financial in 
nature, more than actually talking to them.  Once they're on their way, 
I sometimes am able to talk to them about it, or answer questions or 
concerns they may have.)

Back to our irregularly scheduled message...  :)

> My point is pretty simple and can be boiled down to one thing.  If you
> are pro-life than in my opinion you should respect all life.  If you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> beliefs and legislating morality.

Which religious beliefs is the pro-life trying to legislate, apart from 
the one against abortion, and prohibiting laws allowing euthanasia?

> What about taking care of the
> children that are already here first, who are dying due to warfare,
> starving or dying of aids?

All good causes.  I see you mentioned AIDS - touchy subject, but so is a 
lot of other stuff we've discussed so far, so why not...  I have no idea 
where AIDS came from.  *But*...  I *do* know how it's spread the 
majority of the time - irresponsible behavior.  The majority of 
abortions are the same way - the result of irresponsible behavior.

Another out-of-order quote, because it works well here and doesn't 
detract...

 > If we want to prevent abortions - then a better way than
 > banning it all together is to work on the issues that create the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
 > those things in the first place.

Abortion came about as the antidote to free love.  (Yes, I know it 
existed before that, but it wasn't "mainstream.")  All that free love 
created free babies.  Since the baby is inside the mother for 9 months, 
surely it's no different than a uterine cyst - and medicine now has this 
procedure where they can go in and remove it!  Sounds like a right!

We know a whole lot more than we did back then.  But, if you try to deal 
with the irresponsible behavior, now you're "legislating morality," 
which no one thinks is a good thing.  (Funny - almost all laws reinforce 
morality of one kind or another.)

So now, we've got a contingent of society that is trying to save as many 
of these innocent lives as possible, and educate people in the process. 
  But, do school sex-ed classes deal with "You should wait, because here 
are all the problems with unwanted pregnancy, single parenthood, and 
abortion"?  Nope - it's safe sex (with a dash of abstinence, thanks to 
Bush era federal dollars).

So how, pray tell, are we going to address the "root causes" of the 
perceived *need* for abortion if we're not allowed to address sexual 
restraint?  In lieu of that, how else *could* a group of people work to 
eliminate the most odious symptom (i.e., the killing of unborn children)?

> The reality is people have to make painful choices sometimes and they
> don't always choose the one that is easily defined in terms of black or
> white - right or wrong.

I agree with you there.  That's why a set of principles are so valuable 
- they can help you if you get into a situation like that.  Feelings are 
very, very, very strong - and can lead you to do something very wrong as 
well.

> Just like it is easier to deal with the fact that killing people in a
> war is either ok or not, when I don't have to see it or fight it
> myself.

Well, I might have a different perspective on that - I'm being deployed 
next month.

>     It has been interesting though to have this kind of debate.

It's fun, as long as neither party gets defensive or is insulted.  :) 
Some might even call it "reasoned discourse"!
```

```
I know you've been having to defend yourself a lot, and are outnumbered
here, so I want you to know that even though I may not agree with all
of your views I can find some common ground.  I can tell you are a man
of pure convictions and there isn't anything wrong with trying to help
people, at least you are trying which is more than most.  I'm a bit
defensive maybe because I am a blue state girl living in a totally red
one.  So it's frustrating to be outnumbered in all our mini debates at
the watercooler (as the token liberal).  I know what it's like and the
courage it takes to defend your views even though nobody else likes
them without getting really really mad.  Many props to you for that -
you made me think about this stuff a lot more anyway.    :)

I really hope that you aren't headed into a dangerous situation over
there in Iraq.  Thank you for serving your country, I do admire that
and think it is a very courageous thing to do.  I may not agree with
Dubya or his policies but our military have my support and good will.
I hope something good can come of all that over there and that you are
able to return home soon safe to your family.
Best wishes to you and God bless!
```

- **References:** `<dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144516038.106036.49860@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <9fbaa$44380d27$45491d7a$8115@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
Ok I promise this is it - I really promise this time ...

When you said that
             The people who cry "science! science!" when you talk about
evolution vs. creation cry "rights! rights!" when
             faced with the science of unborn children

Dude that is just not true. That may be my opinion on two totally
different topics. There are plenty of people who accept evolution and
not abortion.   The world isn't just made of liberals and conservatives
- sometimes you can believe in science and religion at the same time.
At least people at my church do, anyway.  And some of them are - dare I
say it - liberals and christians simultaneously!

Why use the term pro-abortion?  I mean, come on, just because somebody
is not in favor of criminalizing something doesn't mean they think it's
an awesome idea.  I would rather let people make up their own minds on
morality rather than tell them what's right or wrong all the time. I
didn't say to spare guilty criminals.  I did say that some of those
"criminals" are now found to have been NOT GUILTY which we can prove
through DNA evidence now, which is also another gift from the wonders
of science, not religion.
```

Holly wrote:
> Ok I promise this is it - I really promise this time ...
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> not abortion.

I realize that - that's why I included the disclaimer at the bottom of 
one of my previous e-mails.  But when you add up the evolution/abortion 
is right people, the evolution/abortion is wrong people, and the people 
who are mixed - I'd bet the third group wouldn't even be close to the 
size of the first or second.

> The world isn't just made of liberals and conservatives
> - sometimes you can believe in science and religion at the same time.
> At least people at my church do, anyway.  And some of them are - dare I
> say it - liberals and christians simultaneously!

I know that exists, but I do not understand it.  My aunt and her family 
are Methodists who all voted for Bill Clinton *twice*!  I just can't 
reconcile the liberal political view with the Christian worldview.

(For the record - there are lots of things that I feel the Republicans 
are screwing up.  But, they're a lot better than what would happen with 
the other party...)

> Why use the term pro-abortion?

Because I'm tired of the "anti-choice" label.  :(

> I mean, come on, just because somebody
> is not in favor of criminalizing something doesn't mean they think it's
> an awesome idea.  I would rather let people make up their own minds on
> morality rather than tell them what's right or wrong all the time.

So you're a fan of "situational ethics"?  I'm not.  Yes, each situation 
is unique.  Truth and right, however, are not moving targets.  (And yes, 
I know there are lots of things that are accepted that aren't right - 
this just happens to be the topic at hand.)

> I
> didn't say to spare guilty criminals.  I did say that some of those
> "criminals" are now found to have been NOT GUILTY which we can prove
> through DNA evidence now, which is also another gift from the wonders
> of science, not religion.

Maybe we could work out some compromise legislation, where we'd bring 
*all* our laws up to speed with scientific advances over that last 30 
years.  :)  Isn't the Senate Majority Leader a doctor?  Maybe he could 
broker something like that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T08:34:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<leqk3216cfsgj742hitrm46p3b2rmdsoks@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144516038.106036.49860@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>`

```
Most conservative politicians allow exceptions to abortion that they
would not allow for murder.    Abortion in the case of rape or incest
are those exceptions.    Anybody who allows those exceptions either
believes that murder should be allowed for rape or incest or that the
aborted embryo (or possibly fetus) is not a person.

Either case their argument against abortion turns into "good girls
should be allowed to have abortions - bad girls should be punished by
becoming mothers".

Before the AMA (and similar organizations) decided that only a
physician could make medical decisions, it was common for abortions to
be allowed before the baby quickened.   This occurred about when an
embryo changed to a fetus and started moving.   But only the mother
knew when this happened.   This also is when brain development started
- embryos basically are not brain-alive.

The Bible says that life starts at birth, although a man could be
compensated when someone else causes his wife's unborn child to abort.
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **References:** `<icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1144425244.248111.262470@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144426541.459780.5550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d21c$44370876$45491d7a$6740@KNOLOGY.NET> <5FEZf.3644$sh3.258641@news20.bellglobal.com> <9583f$4437d465$45491d7a$16253@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

LX-i wrote:

>  but, it is the "theory of evolution".

No. You are wrong. One of the theories is 'Darwin's Theory of Natural
Selection' as an explanation of the observable effects of evoilution.
That is: evolution occurs, the theory explains how it works. Other
theories such as common decent and sexual selection alos explain part
of the mechanisms of evolution.

Creationism on the other hand is just a whole bunch of diverse myths
and legends from many different religious groups.

> There are many reasons why my children aren't in government school - and
> this is one of them.  Seems to me, though, we taught creation
> *exclusively* in our government schools for longer than the theory of
> evolution has existed.  Why exclude the old in favor of the new?

First because the churches no longer control the syllabus. Second
because we have learnt more about the world then a bunch of nomadic
goat herders did.

Do you also want taught that the world is flat and is geocentric with
seven layers of 'heaven' on which the stars are painted, that the sun
is drawn accross the sky by a chariot, and that Thor makes the
lightning with his hammer ?

> Why
> not present both sides' viewpoints, and let the children understand?

Becuae it isn't 'both sides' it is hundreds of sides, you seem to want
to exclude all the creation myths except just one. In fact you are
welcome to teach all those myths, but just don't pretend that it is
science.

> This is one of the same problems I have with the pro-abortion argument.
>   They don't want both sides presented, and let people use their free
> will to make the decision they feel is right.  All they want to do is
> beat their chests and say "IT'S MY RIGHT!"

Which happens to be exactly the same as the anti-abortionist.  They
think that it is THEIR RIGHT to control what people do.


> Notice that I didn't say evolution shouldn't be taught - I think that it
> should.  Kids (and, by extension, adults) should be knowledgeable in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> An uninformed choice is no choice at all.

And exactly how much do you know of the Hindu creation, of Bramha,
Vishnu and the pantheon. How much are you informed about the Greek
creation, the Shintu, the Canaanite, the Bantu, the Moari, the Norse ?

Without knowing all those how can you claim to be informed at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
