```
"Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>I don't know, we'll see what Pete says about NZ.

See my post to Daniel.


> If you think America
> is too liberal, then you might not make it over there!
>

We have a Labour  (that's Democrat if you're American, Labour, if you're 
British) Government. Helen Clark is the Prime Minister.  Most of us are just 
a bit Left of Centre but we have our extremists too. Of course, even our 
extremists are non-violent, the ultimate protest being throwing eggs at 
someone or mooning them. (I just realised...  having done both of those when 
I was a teenager, I must have been a protester - never knew until this 
moment... ) We are probably the only place in the world that has a female 
gay head of state, so maybe we ARE a bit more Liberal than most. Although I 
didn't personally vote for her, I have to admit she is doing a reasonable 
job.  It is likely she'll be voted out at the next election nothwithstanding 
her performance. Kiwis like to change the governement every few years; it 
keeps them fresh and on their toes and it makes sure that everyone, of 
whatever political persuasion, gets a 'fair go'...

> So we can agree that we should be targeting the employers who are
> recruiting and hiring illegals.  They are greedily taking advantage of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> businesses that hire illegals under the table.

Ah, 'under the table'... one of my favourite places...often found myself 
there on a Saturday night; never thought about recruiting there, though... 
:-)

That sounds eminently sensible to me, Holly.

>You may not like the
> Dems, but at least I think you are admitting that maybe W is making
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> defend themselves.

Maybe if fewer lawyers were created there would be less litigation all 
round... :-)
>
> One more tangent:   I thought that Republicans were supposed to be in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> book.

Just print more money... that's the joy of being President. You can even get 
your picture on it... :-) (Or maybe you have  to be dead...?)

  "Shrub's" approval rating is so low - even some of my republican
> friends are now sorry they voted for him.  I would much rather have a
> horndog like Clinton or a bore like Gore as president rather than the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
At least you get to vote... the rest of us have to live with whoever's in 
the White House and there's nothing we can do about it. Given that said 
incumbent has the power to plunge the entire world into nuclear catasrophe, 
it kind of sucks...

Even worse growing up in the Cold War and expecting to be fried any 
moment... I cried when Kennedy was killed. He gave us hope. I don't care 
what they say about him since, he was a 'great' President.  Johnson did the 
right thing but had all the charisma of an aged bloodhound. Nixon was, 
frankly, embarrassing. I think Jimmy Carter was a good man, but possibly not 
a good president. Ronald Reagan made me laugh, Clinton was a joke (but 
likeable enough), Gerald Ford couldn't walk and chew gum at the same time, 
the sooner they get a woman in the oval office the better. :-) And given 
this parade of unhappy idiots, they now think it's OK to destroy the only 
one of them who was worth anything.

(If I get pulled the next time I enter the United States, I won't be 
surprised. At least I can afford a lawyer... :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 41)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-16T03:35:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com>`

```

"Clark Morris" <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message 
news:dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 14 Apr 2006 10:58:25 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now and Zen it isn't.

LOL! (good one!)

 On a more serious note which president(s) of
> the United States in the past 100 years did you think were worth
> something?

OK - I'll ignore the ones I have no personal memory of, because my opinion 
of them would be based on history and what I read about them. That really 
isn't fair to the ones I DO remember, some of whom haven't had their history 
written yet.

So I'll not discuss all the Presidents from Teddy Rooseveldt (1901 - 1909) 
through Harry Truman (1945 - 1953), recognising that there may have been 
some 'great' Presidents in there, and not in any way dismissing the 
acccomplishments of any of them, and start with Dwight Eisenhower (1953 - 
1961)

I remember seeing movie newsreels of the campaign in America with people 
chanting "I like Ike". A war hero, and Army General, you'd think he would be 
a good man for the job. But the world was a very troubled place. There was a 
'cold War' going on with Russia. The entire planet was threatened with 
nuclear destruction. Khruschev (Leader of the Soviet Union at that time) got 
up in the United Nations and famously banged the table with his shoe on 
several occasions. He also shouted "We will bury you!" at the Western 
delegates. For young people (I was a teenager) it was a very frightening and 
worrisome time. It seemed that the Russians had a lunatic in charge and the 
US had no answer. The US appeared to be being run by old men who couldn't 
deal with or contain the energy of the dangerously erratic Russian Leader. 
The whole world could be destroyed any moment and we didn't even get to 
vote.

Please understand that to those of us who did NOT live in the USA the 
domestic policy of the President was of little consequence; foreign policy 
was everything. Dwight Eisenhower, in retrospect, was probably a good man 
but he did not show leadership, seemed to have no answer to Khruschev, and 
seemed to be totally devoid of charisma. He was unconcerned with countries 
outside the US, did not work well with allies such as the UK (this may have 
been a hangover from his wartime experiences with Churchill) and just didn't 
seem to grasp that his decisions would affect the entire Western world. I 
guess I'm saying that he was not a Statesman, in the sense that maybe FDR 
had been.

As the American elections of  1960 approached we waited anxiously.

Nixon came over in the newscasts as being insincere (we found out later that 
he certainly was :-)). This was the first election where the candidates 
debated on television  (it was also the first where Hawaii and Alaska were 
invited to vote) and Kennedy won the TV debate easily. He also won the 
election, but it was incredibly close.

There was considerable elation around the world, especially amongst young 
people. Kennedy had passion, charisma, intelligence, and was in his 40s - an 
unheard of age for a Head of State at that time.

He dealt with Khruschev over the Cuban missile crisis (Khruschev was removed 
from office in 1964 and one of the reasons was because he backed down over 
the Cuban missiles. Yet, he had a case. The West had missiles all around 
Russia targeting Russian Cities, but when the Russians tried a similar 
approach using Cuba, they were stopped.) It was seen as a major victory for 
Kennedy and the West. He had correctly realised that the Russians would only 
respect a firm stance, even though the stakes were incredibly high and  the 
risk was global annihilation.

There was a new spirit of hope. Kennedy travelled outside America and roused 
support wherever he went. A fine orator, it was difficult not to watch or 
listen to him without being moved. Even when he got it wrong (as he arguably 
did in the famous "Ich bin ein Berliner" speech), the crowd still loved him. 
(What his advisers didn't tell him is that in Germany a 'Berliner' is a kind 
of doughnut that has jam injected into it, so his phrase actually translated 
into "I am a jam doughnut".  It didn't matter; the spirit and sentiment of 
what he was trying to say was enough to bring rapturous applause.

He was badly advised and completely screwed up the Bay of Pigs (an abortive 
invasion of Cuba by US backed Cuban expats. Castro was tipped off and was 
waiting. There was carnage and no one ever tried invading Cuba again. 
(Instead, the CIA tried to remove Castro by ensuring his supply of toilet 
paper was poisoned...) Kennedy was forgiven by the world for this fiasco.

For most of us it was inspiring to see the advances he pushed for. The 
Soviets lost their superiority in the Space Race and Apollo 11 landed before 
the end of the decade, just as JFK had promised. (Lyndon Johnson deserves 
much credit for this, but it was JFK who initiated it. I think it is a 
tragedy he never got to see it.)

He showed vision, compassion, and responsibility. You felt like the world 
was in good hands.

His death was simply devastating. Most people who were over the age of, say, 
12, at the time, can remember exactly where they were and what they were 
doing when the news of his death was received. (I could even tell you what I 
was wearing...it was over 40 years ago. The only other event I could tell 
you where I was and what I was wearing was the Apollo 11 landing, 
ironically, as noted above, instigated by Kennedy.)

So, in my book, JFK was a great President.

(I think it is very sad that there seems to be a preoccupation with his 
alleged failings, now he is unable to answer or refute charges. Was he 
involved in the death of marilyn Monroe? It makes a good story. Was he a 
druken philanderer like some others of his tribe? Does it matter if he does 
his job outstandingly well? Raise all the questions you like, in my book, he 
got us through a very difficult time and his murder was a tragedy.

Since then we haven't seen anyone in the same league. There have been 
occasional memorable incidents...

Nixon's impeachment and resignation in 1975. Made me laugh. The guy was a 
villain who committed illegal and outrageous acts while holding the seal of 
President (never mind the lying and manipulation), yet, watching him on TV 
giving his farewell speech he came across as the victim :-) "They won't have 
Richard Nixon to kick around any more." I actually laughed aloud when I saw 
it; a conman to the bitter end.

Jimmy Carter always struck me as an honest, decent, man, but he didn't have 
the drive and charisma that would put hm in the Kennedy league.

The Reagan/Thatcher partnership was an interesting one and you notice that 
Gaddafi was chastised during this era. Since then we haven't heard a peep 
out of him. I wonder if Al Queda would have tried the 9/11 attacks under 
that regime. (This is not an implicit criticism of Dubya; who was 
unfortunate enough to have been in power at the time of 9/11. There are many 
criticisms of Dubya that CAN be made,  but this isn't one of them.)

George Bush Snr.  Had to deal with Desert Storm and did a passing good job 
of it. Made a mistake in not letting Stormin' Norman go into Baghdad and get 
Saddam (We're all paying for that mistake now...).

Bill Clinton? What can you say? Mention his name and I think of blue dresses 
and cigars. It is entirely his own fault if this is his legacy.

Dubya? I'm making no comment. He gets points for trying, but put him in the 
Kennedy league? I don't think so...

So where to now?  Condoleezza Rice or Hilary Clinton? Well that's a choice 
I'd be thrilled to make...NOT!

I don't envy those of you who DO get to vote... :-)

Without wishing to rub salt in the wound, there is a popular theory that in 
a democracy people may not get the government they want, but they will 
invariably get the government they deserve.

If you allow powerful hidden cartels and lobbies to manipulate the nominees, 
if you require such huge investment to run for office that only the very 
wealthy can have any hope of doing so, and even they will have to answer to 
their financial backers, if you, in effect, put democracy up for sale, just 
like everything else in your system, then you will not get outstanding 
candidates and Presidents.

The best you can hope for is to keep USA Inc ticking over. And that ticking 
may well be the time bomb of terrorism and insurrection. It seems to me that 
some of the very worrying trends we are seeing emerging and discussed here 
(like the WBC, and 1,000,000 people demonstrating to be allowed to be above 
the law) can only end in disaster for the US.

There is a dire and urgent need for tolerance and a change of attitude on 
the part of many. The widening gap between the Haves and the Have Nots MUST 
be plugged if things are not to descend into chaos.

Maybe it's time the 'silent majority' broke their silence...?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 42)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-15T18:56:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
[speaking of Kennedy]
> He was badly advised and completely screwed up the Bay of Pigs (an abortive 
> invasion of Cuba by US backed Cuban expats. Castro was tipped off and was 
> waiting. There was carnage and no one ever tried invading Cuba again. 
> (Instead, the CIA tried to remove Castro by ensuring his supply of toilet 
> paper was poisoned...) Kennedy was forgiven by the world for this fiasco.

Sure, I could see why the *world* would forgive him - they weren't the 
ones who still had a Communist dictatorship 90 miles from their 
shores...  :)

(I wasn't alive when Kennedy was - and I don't really feel that his 
bungling of that operation was something for which he needed 
forgiveness.  It was definitely a bad call.)

> (I think it is very sad that there seems to be a preoccupation with his 
> alleged failings, now he is unable to answer or refute charges. Was he 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> got us through a very difficult time and his murder was a tragedy.

He's the last Democrat with whom I actually agree (again, from studying 
history - I believe I was born when Nixon was President).  Yes, he had 
his failings, but who doesn't?  (I certainly have plenty.)

> Jimmy Carter always struck me as an honest, decent, man, but he didn't have 
> the drive and charisma that would put hm in the Kennedy league.

I thought you were writing about foreign policy.  Carter's 
administration was one of the most disastrous when it came to foreign 
affairs.  Appeasement at every turn.

> The Reagan/Thatcher partnership was an interesting one and you notice that 
> Gaddafi was chastised during this era. Since then we haven't heard a peep 
> out of him. I wonder if Al Queda would have tried the 9/11 attacks under 
> that regime.

I don't think they would have.  And, had they, I can just about 
guarantee that the resulting war would have been prosecuted much more 
harshly and quickly than the current administration has done.  (And I 
just can't hear Reagan spouting any of this "religion of peace" nonsense...)

> (This is not an implicit criticism of Dubya; who was 
> unfortunate enough to have been in power at the time of 9/11. There are many 
> criticisms of Dubya that CAN be made,  but this isn't one of them.)

I personally feel that the hot "war on terror" has gone on too long. 
That doesn't mean I think we should cut-and-run, but that we should have 
been much fiercer in our attacks.  However, balanced against how long it 
*used* to take to fight a war, this one is still quite young.

> Bill Clinton? What can you say? Mention his name and I think of blue dresses 
> and cigars. It is entirely his own fault if this is his legacy.

I wouldn't put it past some history books, in maybe 10-15 years, to 
start trying to rehabilitate his image.  He was one of the luckiest 
Presidents ever - he presided over the .com boom, then passed the .com 
bust to W.  His policies had nothing to do with the explosion of the 
Internet, but he was in office when it happened.

Our economy is actually doing better now than it was during that time - 
but, if you only watched the evening news, you'd never know it.

> Dubya? I'm making no comment. He gets points for trying, but put him in the 
> Kennedy league? I don't think so...

Well, with Clinton and W, we don't have the years of viewing results 
that we have with some of the other Presidents.  (Like you said, history 
has yet to be written.)  I'm looking forward to seeing the long-term 
results of the W presidency.  He's got a chance to make his legacy a 
great one; I don't know if he has the will to, though.

> So where to now?  Condoleezza Rice or Hilary Clinton? Well that's a choice 
> I'd be thrilled to make...NOT!

Condi all the way, man...  I'd take Condi over McCain or Giuliani any 
day.  I think she'd be better than W - same mindset, more... how do our 
Hispanic friend say it...  cajones.  (Who would have thought that 
"compassionate conservatism" meant "I never veto anything"?)

> If you allow powerful hidden cartels and lobbies to manipulate the nominees, 
> if you require such huge investment to run for office that only the very 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> candidates and Presidents.

With our system, though, there's no way to keep money out of politics - 
very few outlets are available at no cost.  With our government 
structured into its three branches, that's supposed to kind of make up 
for it.  However, what we've got now is people working not for their 
constituents, but for special interest groups and lobbyists.  (And I'll 
fault Republicans for this as much as Democrats.  Washington corrupts.)

There's one thing we could do that, I think, would go a long way to 
fixing some of our problems - and, they have to do with two of the 
Constitution's amendments.

The first is repealing the 17th amendment.  This is the amendment that 
changed Senators from being appointed by the state legislatures to being 
elected by popular vote, the way Representatives are.  This, in effect, 
took away the participation of the states' governments in the 
legislature.  The Senate was supposed to be the voice of the states (you 
know, that whole "United STATES of America" thing), and the House of 
Representatives was the voice of the people.  These two bodies had to 
agree on Federal law before it was enacted.  Allowing the states to 
appoint Senators would give us people holden to the state, not special 
interests - and would provide balance to the legislative process. 
(Also, this is important when it comes to judicial appointments - at the 
Federal level, they must be approved by the Senate.  Rather than having 
them approved by the "people", they would be approved now by the 
representatives of the states.)

The second is our 10th amendment, the last one in the Bill of Rights. 
It says that any powers not specifically delegated to the Federal 
government are the responsibility of the states.  This should be 
enforced - with every bill that comes before Congress, they should ask 
"What section in the Constitution authorizes us to do what this bill 
sets forth to do?"  The founders of our country envisioned a small, 
small Federal government, with most of the power and responsibility 
delegated to the states.  Getting that power back decentralized is 
essential to getting good laws passed and bad laws repealed, and would 
also get money out of the Federal system - why bribe someone who has 
little power?  (This would also offset corruption - if the groups poured 
their money into a certain state, it wouldn't affect the whole country.)

This last principle is a big part of the conservative agenda in this 
country.  The Federal government should do very few things...
  - Defend our nation
  - Ensure each state plays nice with the others
  - Have enough infrastructure to maintain our capital
  - Maintain diplomatic relations with other nations
  - Collect the minor taxes to pay for the above

Past that, local management is generally much more efficient (and where 
the power should be).  The disbursement of education money in Idaho, for 
example, probably needs to be done differently than it does in Alabama, 
New York, or California.  Yet with every budget, Congress tells the 
states how they must divvy up part of the funds they receive from the 
Federal government.  This brings up a good question - why are they 
receiving funds from the Federal government for education at all?  Take 
Washington, D. C. out of the loop!  It could very well be that different 
states need different levels of funding for their various programs.  Why 
should *my* tax money, paid in Alabama, support some program in 
Wisconsin?  (I've got nothing against Wisconsin - but they have people 
there paying their own taxes.)

I'm sure there are lots of folks who'll love to poke holes in this - but 
I've never heard anyone make the argument that it wouldn't help with 
some of the problems we're having today.  Bureaucracy is expensive - 
eliminate it, and you've saved money.  If that money is tax money, 
you've just let every person in this country keep a little more than 
they've earned.

(Not that any of that has anything to do with foreign policy...)

> There is a dire and urgent need for tolerance and a change of attitude on 
> the part of many. The widening gap between the Haves and the Have Nots MUST 
> be plugged if things are not to descend into chaos.

Those are interesting terms - "haves" and "have nots".  People who are 
wealthy, generally speaking, are doing things that make them wealthy. 
Ditto for the poor.  This country isn't the land of equal outcomes, it's 
the land of equal opportunity.  Think of it this way - take 10 random 
people and give them $10 apiece.  That's equal opportunity.  Do you 
think the outcome would be equal?  Not a chance...

There are lots of assistance programs in this country for those who want 
to make a change.  Contrary to some, the lottery is *not* one of those 
programs.  :)
```

```
LX-i wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
...
> 
>> Bill Clinton? What can you say? Mention his name and I think of blue 
>> dresses and cigars. It is entirely his own fault if this is his legacy.

And yet he was the last President to actually appoint people to major 
government posts based on competence to do the jobs required by law 
(e.g., James Witt at FEMA).  Under the current President, with 
appointments based only on ability to "suck up" to the President or as 
pay offs to major contributors who want to maximize profit at the 
expense of the public by having deliberately-incompetent federal 
regulatory agencies,  we find that when disaster strikes there is no one 
left at the Federal level that has the slightest idea how to do anything 
other than photo ops.

...
> 
>> So where to now?  Condoleezza Rice or Hilary Clinton? Well that's a 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> "compassionate conservatism" meant "I never veto anything"?)

Another apparent failure of the Peter Principle.  As National Security 
advisor she has memos passing over her desk warning of terrorists 
planning to use airplanes as attack weapons, apparently fails to 
consider it significant, and then has the arrogance to say after 9/11 
"no one would have expected terrorists to fly planes into the World 
Trade Center".  The CIA Director had the same information, and when he 
heard news of the WTC immediately understood what had happened.  Either 
Candi was incompetent at her job or deliberately lying to obscure 
Dubya's incompetence in failing to take the warnings seriously.  So what 
does Dubya do? Promote her to Secretary of State.  I guess we are 
supposed to assume she was lying to protect Dubya and he was rewarding 
her.  Just the sort of competent, credible leader we need to follow 
Dubya. NOT!

...
  > The second is our 10th amendment, the last one in the Bill of 
Rights. It
> says that any powers not specifically delegated to the Federal 
> government are the responsibility of the states.  This should be 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> their money into a certain state, it wouldn't affect the whole country.)
...
	I strongly agree that the system is in need of an overhaul.  Those who 
count up the billions that are now siphoned from the Federal Treasury 
every year for special benefits, exceptions, subsidies, tax breaks, etc. 
that are slipped into law to "pay off" campaign contributors have 
calculated that if we could avoid this excess by having all Federal 
campaigns completely financed directly by the tax payers, it would cost 
us much less in the long run.
	I would propose a simpler first step than justifying each bill - simply 
require that every congressman or Senator have first read a bill in its 
entirety in its final form before they can vote in favor of it.  A 
significant part of major legislation these days (e.g., the original 
Patriot back, the annual appropriation bills) is either too large to be 
read in the allotted time, or is amended behind closed doors with 
minimal notice. Votes are cast in ignorance based on recommendation of 
committee chairmen or other party leaders, and then we wonder why so 
much garbage and waste becomes law!

> (Not that any of that has anything to do with foreign policy...)
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

To have equal opportunity one must start with equal resources.  Totally 
eliminating Estate Taxes as the current President and Republican leaders 
advocate would guarantee even greater long-term concentration of wealth 
and power in the hands of those who through the lucky fortune of birth 
and through no merit of their own inherit large wealth, and make for 
considerably less "equal opportunity".  Our founding fathers were 
concerned about this possibility, because they were well acquainted with 
the injustices resulting from lesser men inheriting titles, power, and 
wealth in England at the time.  What job do you think Dubya would have 
today if he hadn't had his father's connections and money to provide a 
"party experience" at an elite college, and get him another executive 
position every time he proved incompetent in the business world? - 
perhaps he would have made a good used car salesman, where likability 
and a certain lack of candor and lack of honesty are traditionally expected.

If you look at the history in this country (USA) of those that have 
accumulated great wealth without inheriting it, you will occasionally 
find one who has managed to come up with a brilliant idea and 
successfully exploit it commercially; but more often than not you will 
find someone who just happened to be in the right place at the right 
time with the right resources to successfully exploit the ideas of 
others, exploit natural resources they didn't create, or exploit the 
work of others.  I am always amazed when such consider themselves "self 
made men", worthy of special tax breaks and other consideration, when 
without a strong element of luck and the resources of others they could 
just as easily have been a "have not".  And more often than not they 
create problems in their wake that others have to clean up, often at 
public expense - a good argument why such should be subject to 
progressive tax rates.
```

```
I don't know that this is the Peter Principle quite.  The Peter
Principle has people being promoted because of their competency until
they get promoted once too often.

What we have in this country is something that has plagued most
countries throughout time - and is worse of a problem in Communist
countries.    That is promotion by cronyism.    It is a sad fact that
"who you know" is more important than "what you know".   Joseph
Nacchio has publicly stated that's how he got Qwest.    It certainly
is the way to start your own company (that needs some seed money). And
it's the only way our president understands.

>I am always amazed when such consider themselves "self

The skill that these "self made men" excel in is in making friends
with people with money or power.   They note that anybody could do
this, but most of us don't and are thus not deserving of that kind of
success.
```

```
Fuedalism in Europe was one of the things that they were most adamantly
opposed to, according to what I was taught in school. Mind you, I am
Canadian, so was not taught near as much as you about USA history.

Now, if you were also taught about what happened in Britain and Europe,
you will know that feudalism was caused mainly by the concentration of
wealth in the great families, that is, the nobility. The democracies of
the rest of the world evolved by destroying that, mainly by death
duties, though the French took a more direct route.

Once great wealth could be diluted by a couple generations, fuedalism
became a thing of the past.

Until Corporations were invented.  Once you have an entity with an
infinite lifespan, it all becomes possible again.  You simply do the
exact same thing ... concentrate all the economic power into a few
corporations, and give control of the corporations to the new nobility.
 Voila, history repeats. If Bush gets his way, the United States will
eventually also have a revolution. And there is 2000 thousand years of
history to explain how and why, and maybe even when.

Donald
```

```
On Mon, 17 Apr 2006 17:34:26 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Sounds like socialism to me.  If you feel that socialism is so great, 
>why not become a citizen of one of the many countries that are already 
>socialistic?  Why try to turn our republic into one - because you are 
>smarter than our founding fathers, I suppose?

Every country has socialistic aspects, and every country has
capitalistic aspects.    Most people don't want to move every aspect
of their country in one direction, but have some things they believe
are better handled by society and others which society already runs
too much.

Oh and insurance is socialistic.    Society can share expenses without
state ownership.    State mandated insurance is a bit more
socialistic.   Company subsidized insurance is more socialistic than
non-subsidized.    Church subsidized hospitals are socialistic.

Heck, when everybody in a church gets together to help a parishioner -
that's socialistic.   

Lots of gray in that word.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1145375307.510724.58500@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<OU61g.1202$DR6.92490@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <TNE0g.8086$i41.5881@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <48df8$444417ea$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <fqp942hrh68qiulii88q7hc44vnuaiog98@4ax.com> <OU61g.1202$DR6.92490@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Good posts!  I never understood why "socialism" became a dirty word,
kind of like "liberal" has become.  There are many movements within
socialism, spanning from a more communist ideal to a democratic
socialism to a libertarian (anarchic) socialism.  I think that people
should really understand the term before using it like a catch phrase
talking point.  It's one of those Talk Radio Angry White Man lingo
terms that is too easy to use, one can dismiss anything involving
responsibility to the community as "socialist".  Any kind of regulation
or government sponsored program becomes "socialist".   I don't think
that unconstrained capitalism, deregulation, and private control of our
country's infrastructure is a great idea.  Look at what happened to the
airlines with deregulation.  You can thank the neo-cons for that (at
least my dad, a retired pilot from a bankrupt airline who lost all of
his pension money does).

Some might even say that Jesus' Sermon on the Mount was socialist.  And
we wouldn't be enjoying weekends or limited work hours without
socialism (which those are being eroded again, unfortunately).   There
are good and bad things that arise from any movement.  The problem is
when people are unwilling to compromise their ideals, whether they be
capitalist or socialist, that not everything should be treated the same
way.
```

- **References:** `<4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <TNE0g.8086$i41.5881@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <48df8$444417ea$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <fqp942hrh68qiulii88q7hc44vnuaiog98@4ax.com> <OU61g.1202$DR6.92490@news20.bellglobal.com> <1145375307.510724.58500@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com>`

```
Holly wrote:
> Good posts!  I never understood why "socialism" became a dirty word,
> kind of like "liberal" has become.  There are many movements within
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yes, there are lots of good things about socialism, and lots of bad
things about unfettered capitalism.  As soon as people start talking in
terms of capital letters, though, it beomes simple jingoism.

We have state paid medicare here, and it is a more humane as well as a
cheaper system than capitalism.

I recently went in for my 60 year checkup.  Full cat scan, ultrasound on
my lungs/heart(I am a smoker) a dozen blood tests, and an hour with a
specialist.  My bill is $27 as a few of the blood tests my doctor
ordered were not paid for.

I had to wait a week for the cat scan, and my USA neighbours are
screaming that the capitalist system in the states is so much better
because there is no wait time. If it had been an emergency(as was the
case when my roomy had a heart attack last year), the cat scan would be
close to immediate.

I find it hard to believe that it is *better* stateside, though the
stateside medical corporations are screaming that we *must* allow them
to compete in Canada because they can give *immediate* treatment and to
not let them institute a faster service for the privileged rich is an
unfair trade practice. They can do the same thing for only $13,000 per
cat scan.  The rest is extra.

Unfettered capitalism is really the same as "everyone must have their
price" and not to be that way is immoral. What bullshit. I know of few
American citizens that feel *anything* is ok as long as you can pay for
it, but the American government has been treating it's neighbours that
way for years.  All in the name of capitalism, and with tones of moral
outrage.  Then they wonder why people do not trust them.

Donald
```

```
Holly wrote:
> Good posts!  I never understood why "socialism" became a dirty word,
> kind of like "liberal" has become.  There are many movements within
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> his pension money does).

1.  Have you ever stopped to think that talk radio brings in the 
audience it does because they are saying things that people *already* 
agree with?  Or are you convinced that only mind-numbed robots listen to 
talk radio?

2.  Socialism is *not* a catchphrase - it has a specific definition. 
There are times and places for things to be done in a socialistic 
manner, but government is *not* one of those places.  See my post to Mr. 
Tees about "motivation."

> Some might even say that Jesus' Sermon on the Mount was socialist.

So *now* you want this country run the way the Bible says?  heh...  The 
church *should* come together to meet the needs of its members, and as 
many of its non-members as possible.  That's what Jesus was talking 
about, in the Sermon on the Mount, when He said that we should be like 
salt and light.  Salt is a preservative, and light eliminates darkness.

The *government* has no business taking your (tax) money and giving it 
to me, just because I have children.  (I could write a few paragraphs 
about how a married E-5 with 3 kids still makes little enough to qualify 
for EITC, but I won't.)  The *government* has no business taking your 
money to pay for some old person's medicine.  The *government* has no 
business in the wealth redistribution business at all!
```

_(thread depth: 49)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-19T01:07:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e242gg$691$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <OU61g.1202$DR6.92490@news20.bellglobal.com> <1145375307.510724.58500@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <7fe46$444577fa$45491d7a$30029@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
In article <7fe46$444577fa$45491d7a$30029@KNOLOGY.NET>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

[snip]

>The *government* has no business taking your 
>money to pay for some old person's medicine.  The *government* has no 
>business in the wealth redistribution business at all!

Not according to at least one Founder, Mr Summers... and rather 
explicitly, at that.  From 
http://odur.let.rug.nl/~usa/P/tj3/writings/brf/jefl41.htm :

--begin quoted text:

Another means of silently lessening the inequality of property is to 
exempt all from taxation below a certain point, and to tax the higher 
portions or property in geometrical progression as they rise. Whenever 
there are in any country uncultivated lands and unemployed poor, it is 
clear that the laws of property have been so far extended as to violate 
natural right. The earth is given as a common stock for man to labor and 
live on. If for the encouragement of industry we allow it to be 
appropriated, we must take care that other employment be provided to those 
excluded from the appropriation. If we do not, the fundamental right to 
labor the earth returns to the unemployed.

--end quoted text

Redistribution of wealth via confiscatory taxation, Mr Summers... and 
rather clearly stated as 'the government's business'.

DD
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lg2f42p4ikoipandunegoei192l93uhlsv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <TNE0g.8086$i41.5881@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <48df8$444417ea$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <fqp942hrh68qiulii88q7hc44vnuaiog98@4ax.com> <OU61g.1202$DR6.92490@news20.bellglobal.com> <1145375307.510724.58500@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <1145482856.231768.203050@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 19 Apr 2006 14:40:56 -0700, "Alistair"
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Socialism has not really been tried properly, but
>then neither has Communism (human greed seems to corrupt the
>principles).

Trying is one thing, succeeding is another.

A system that attempts to enforce equality has account for when
someone gains power.   If his competition has been made weaker, he is
free to become a despot.    Communism without despotism needs to come
up with a solution for this.

A free enterprise system has lots of big powerful entities that most
of us don't care for.   We bash Microsoft, we bash the insurance
industry, we bash the oil companies and the drug companies.    But we
are better off with a bunch of powerful entities fighting each other
than with one powerful entity that doesn't need to listen to anybody.

(Drug companies and insurance companies have lobbyists fighting each
other).
```

```
On Tue, 18 Apr 2006 18:20:12 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Right - but government-sponsored socialism is the big problem.  If 
>people want to get together and be social, that's fine with me.  History 
>has shown that, as a governmental philosophy, it is a poor one.

All the time?    

There are some people who believe that all roads should be private, as
should all armies.

Virtually all people believe that some public works should be
supported by the state, either partially or fully.   The argument is
which works, and I haven't seen any easy criteria to answer that
argument.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-18T14:28:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e22t1j$g51$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <TNE0g.8086$i41.5881@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <48df8$444417ea$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
In article <48df8$444417ea$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>Joel C. Ewing wrote:

[snip]

>> If you look at the history in this country (USA) of those that have 
>> accumulated great wealth without inheriting it, you will occasionally 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sounds like socialism to me.

What something 'sounds like to me', Mr Summers, might, possibly, show more 
about one's ability to hear than anything else.  Nowhere above can I see 
advocated 'the collective or governmental ownership and administration of 
the means of production and distribution of goods'.

>If you feel that socialism is so great, 
>why not become a citizen of one of the many countries that are already 
>socialistic?

Were Mr Ewing to have posted something about the greatness of socialism 
that might be a germane question, Mr Summers; in the context of the 
discussion it seems to be a non-sequitur.

>Why try to turn our republic into one - because you are 
>smarter than our founding fathers, I suppose?

In case your education neglected it, Mr Summers, you might be surprised to 
learn that the Founders were, along with their education, foresight and 
compassion for others, human beings... with all the associated virtues and 
shortcomings thereof.  To accuse Mr Ewing of advocating socialism, based 
on what he has posted above, would seem to be as reasonable as to accuse 
you of a sort of Deification of the Elders... which is, I believe, the 
*last* thing that the Founders wanted, based on their writings.

'I am not an advocate for frequent changes in laws and constitutions, but 
laws and institutions must go hand in hand with the progress of the human 
mind. As that becomes more developed, more enlightened, as new discoveries 
are made, new truths discovered and manners and opinions change, with the 
change of circumstances, institutions must advance also to keep pace with 
the times. We might as well require a man to wear still the coat which 
fitted him when a boy as civilized society to remain ever under the 
regimen of their barbarous ancestors.'

These words of Jefferson are written in the stones of his Memorial, Mr 
Summers.  'Laws and instutituions' must keep pace with 'manners and 
opinions'; to ask for otherwise is to request a kind of stagnance which 
appears, to the above-stated sentiments, to be utterly repugnant.

DD
```

- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> There is a dire and urgent need for tolerance and a change of attitude on 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the outcome would be equal?  Not a chance...

    I'm not sure everyone gets "equal opportunity" in America though (and 
now I'm about to justify this claim by describing my experience in Canada 
and extrapolating it to all of North America). Some people are born rich, go 
to private schools with a 25-students-to-teacher ratio, receive additional 
private tutoring, form networking friendships with people at high paying 
jobs, and then acquire high paying jobs themselves. I have a friend like 
this. He's very generous: he paid for my limousine rental at highschool 
graduation, gave me his collection of XBox games, etc. But he's also very 
rich: I see him maybe once every two to four months, and every time I see 
him, he's driving a new luxury car.

>
> There are lots of assistance programs in this country for those who want 
> to make a change.  Contrary to some, the lottery is *not* one of those 
> programs.  :)

    I like to think I'm managing my money fairly wisely. I've been making 
the maximum RRSP contributions I'm allowed, got a small stock portfolio 
which has been beating the average market returns, and I'm looking at real 
estate. However, unless I win the lottery, I don't think I'll ever be as 
wealthy as my above described friend.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-18T05:30:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ai1kvFt5gjfU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <bXM0g.8327$Vg.7386@edtnps90>`

```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:bXM0g.8327$Vg.7386@edtnps90...
>
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And even if you did manage to get into his league, there are many people who 
are richer still. You are unlikely to become the richest man on earth or 
even get close, even if you DID win the lottery. Get over it. Be glad for 
your friend and be thankful he has enough generosity of spirit to help you 
out occasionally.

During the course of my life I have generated considerable wealth (I worked 
it out once; it was millions...) Yet I am not a really wealthy man. OK, I am 
debt free, I own all of my property, I always have enough cash to do the 
things I want to do, go to the places I want to go, and buy the things I 
want to buy, but I still can't quite afford to retire. (I had a very 
expensive divorce that means I have less of an asset base than I expected, 
but, like most things, there is an upside to this too.)  Had I set myself 
the goal of becoming a wealthy man, there is no doubt I could have achieved 
it, but I didn't. And I'm not sorry. The quality of my life has been full 
and excellent. I don't regret one unsaved penny.

Wealth in itself is a poor goal. You need enough to do the things you want 
to do. You don't need to compete financially with friends or anyone else; it 
is an unwinnable game.

When I was quite young I realised that some of the kids at school had things 
that I didn't, and I knew we couldn't afford expensive toys. It didn't 
matter. We all shared our toys (rich kids and poor ones) and it really 
wasn't a problem. However, I did ask my father whether we were rich or poor. 
(Actually, we were quite poor, but not on the poverty line. My father had 
suffered a severe heart attack that left him unable to work and welfare in 
those days was almost non -existent. My mother took in washing, cleaned 
houses, and did ironing so we could get by, and we did.). My Dad said: "Son, 
to a poor man, we are very rich. To a rich man we are very poor."  We were 
actually fabulously wealthy in the things that money can't buy, but it was a 
number of years before I really understood what he meant.

By all means plan for your financial independence, but don't ever make the 
getting of money a goal in itself.

Pete.
```

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:4ai1kvFt5gjfU1@individual.net...
>
> "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> and extrapolating it to all of North America).

[snip description of my rich friend]

> And even if you did manage to get into his league, there are many people 
> who are richer still. You are unlikely to become the richest man on earth 
> or even get close, even if you DID win the lottery. Get over it. Be glad 
> for your friend and be thankful he has enough generosity of spirit to help 
> you out occasionally.

    Right. I didn't mean to give off the impression that I'm money-crazed or 
envious of my friend. I was just offering a evidence of lack of equal 
opportunity. As far as I can tell, I made "all the right choices", so there 
must be some other reason why I'm not as rich as he is. I claim that one of 
the reasons for this is the wealth of the families we were born into, which 
directly leads to unequal opportunities.

    - Oliver
```

```
LX-i wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> [speaking of Kennedy]
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> shores...  :)

Pardon? You had a communist dictatorship West of Alaska already. That's
what? 18 miles?


>

> I thought you were writing about foreign policy.  Carter's
> administration was one of the most disastrous when it came to foreign
> affairs.  Appeasement at every turn.

I thought that the worst thing he ever did was to cancel the neutron
bomb project.

>
>
> Our economy is actually doing better now than it was during that time -
> but, if you only watched the evening news, you'd never know it.

Is your economy still bankrupt?

>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> interests - and would provide balance to the legislative process.

Wouldn't that just push the money vote down to Governor level?

>
> Past that, local management is generally much more efficient (and where
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> they've earned.

That presumes that by empowering states you really do make savings.
However, instead of a national bureaucracy you will have 50 state
bureaucracies so I suspect this will be a more expensive option. Still,
it is your money.

>
> Those are interesting terms - "haves" and "have nots".  People who are
> wealthy, generally speaking, are doing things that make them wealthy.
> Ditto for the poor.  This country isn't the land of equal outcomes, it's
> the land of equal opportunity.

If you start with less then it will be so much harder to acquire the
wealthy status of those who start with a few inherited millions in
their back pockets.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-17T08:53:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9oa742lsosv4hslj286pbpk9ugfo9g3867@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 17 Apr 2006 07:41:04 -0700, "Alistair"
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>That presumes that by empowering states you really do make savings.
>However, instead of a national bureaucracy you will have 50 state
>bureaucracies so I suspect this will be a more expensive option. Still,
>it is your money.

One advantage to distributed processing is distributed screw-ups.   We
can observe, compare, and correct.   When everything is under one
system, we don't really have anything to compare with - and a disaster
is universal.

As we see with computers though - we're not as isolated as we think.
Our objects, hardware, and infrastructure are more dependent upon each
other than we pretend.
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com...
> LX-i wrote:
>> Alistair wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not if there are a large number of such entities, Donald. Or even a 
reasonable number. I like your analogy of corporations with robber Barons 
but it only goes so far. Feudalism was far worse for the peasants than 
anything we have to bear today. And autocratic dictatorship (or the "Divine 
Right of Kings") was even worse than that. John didn't happily hand over 
power at Runnymede in 1215. The Magna Carta was not just so the Barons could 
acquire the King's power; they weren't all villains. Many of them were God 
fearing men who realised that their wealth and power depended on a viable 
life for the peasants. Misrule by the monarch was not conducive to good for 
anyone. (OK, they weren't about to give away their wealth or power, but they 
would certainly see that people could live reasonably, and they provided the 
protection for them, not just in times of war but also in normal times, 
settling disputes and providing justice.)

(The Dashwood family motto is "Pro Magna Carta" - we've been liberals for at 
least 1000 years...:-))

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 47)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
> news:ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com...
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
And what is so different today?  I rather expect there are a few
corporations that have morals too. There was also more than one noble.
The fact remains that without death, any entity that can be managed for
several hundred years will do a lot better than mere humans. The wealth
becomes very concentrated in fewer and fewer hands.  That has already,
to a large extent, happened.

Doing away with death duties will solidify that trend. Note that Habeus
Corpus, about the only right as old as the Magna Carta, has been
repealed in the states.

The United States has become a monarchy.  The only difference is their
monarchy is for limited term, and a new monarch has to be chosen every
couple years. LX-i reminds me of one of her majestie's loyal officers,
willing to die for the crown, regardless of what the crown does or says.
He and such policies belong in the dark ages.  Unfortunately, we have
not progressed much beyond them.

Donald
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> The United States has become a monarchy.  The only difference is their
> monarchy is for limited term, and a new monarch has to be chosen every
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> not progressed much beyond them.

This is laughable.  And, you're missing one very important part of what 
makes capitalism work every time it's tried - *motivation*.  If you know 
that you can leave none or very little of your accumulated wealth to 
your children, then where's the motivation to build your business? 
Don't *you* want *your* children to have it easier than you did?
```

```
Donald
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <IFo1g.1596$DR6.127554@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Wed, 19 Apr 2006 06:54:31 -0400, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>LX-i wrote:
>> Donald Tees wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>had little else to work with.

If you want to talk concentration of power, the Canadian Prime
Minister in a majority government situation has far more power than
the US president.  The Republican congress can shove something down
the Republican president's throat (or Democratic congress and
Democratic president or mixture of parties).  The Canadian Prime
Minister can determine who is allowed to run for the party in a given
riding while in most states it is determined solely by the voters who
decide to show up and register for a given party on primary day.  The
Canadian Prime Minister can force all members of his/her party to vote
in a given way on a confidence motion where lining up votes on some
critical issues in the US Congress is like herding cats.  The things
that can be done with Orders in Council still astound me.  I'm a US
citizen and landed immigrant who quickly caught the relevance of the
various jibes of the Royal Canadian Air Farce.  The Canadian Senate is
a sometimes check on Parliament and the Supreme Court is a
surprisingly independent body given that the members are appointed by
the prime minister and I'm not clear as to whether Parliament even
gets to rubber stamp the appointment.  In the US, the Senate must
concur for legislation to pass.  In the US, the Senate can block any
presidential appointment that requires advice and consent.  This
includes cabinet ministers.  The US government is more powerful than
the Canadian government and thus within the institutional constraints
the US president has more power to wield than does the Canadian Prime
Minister.  Within the government itself, the Canadian Prime Minister
has fewer checks on his/her power than the US president does.


>
>As to motivation, I have never wanted, nor do I want, to put my children
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-19T14:06:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET...
>
> you're missing one very important part of what makes capitalism work every 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> have it easier than you did?

    Granted, I haven't thought about this very much, but it seems to me that 
people are "financially at-ease" not when they have a large sum of money in 
the absolute sense, but when they have more money than the people around 
them. If I have a 10^6 dollars, it doesn't mean much if everyone else around 
me has 10^26 dollars. So to ensure a financially easy life for my children, 
I have not only accumulate a lot of money, but I have to make sure no one 
else accumulates more money than I do (or at least, not at a greater rate).

    Unfortunately, the rich have various opportunities that aren't available 
to the poor. My bank account has a fee of about $5 per month just for 
keeping it open. However, if I maintain a balance of $1000 or higher 
throughout the month, the fee is waived. Someone who doesn't have $1000 to 
just have lie around would be suffering an extra expense. When I trade 
stocks, I have a flat $29 fee per transaction. Let's say I spend $100 to buy 
stocks. $29 is taken away, so my book value is actually only $71. Let's say 
I then make an amazing return of 81% so that my book value is now $128.51. I 
sell my stocks, and pay another $29 fee, ending up with $99.51, so I've 
actually lost money. The richer you are, the more you can put aside for the 
investment, and the less the flat rate fee hurts. If I had place $5000 
instead of $100, I'd have ended up with $8968.51. So it's easier for the 
rich to get richer than for the poor to get less poor.

    That means that as I'm accumulating money for my children, richer people 
are presumably accumulating money even faster than I am. The ratio to which 
their children are richer than my children will be greater than the ratio to 
which my rich peers were richer than me, thus actually making life *harder* 
for my children, rather than easier.

    Perhaps if we were not allowed to leave any of our wealth to our 
children, we could eliminate this problem. Every child becomes wealthy via 
their own actions, rather than the actions of their ancestors plus compound 
interest. And since every child starts off in the same financial state, they 
would all have equal opportunity (I've made some simplifying assumptions 
here, such as eliminating racism, sexism, and other forms of prejudice that 
might be barriers to these children).

    - Oliver
```

- **Message-ID:** `<59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> at least, not at a greater rate).

That's not necessarily true, though.  We're in the minority among our 
friends of being a single-income family.  And, from what I've seen, 
we're actually better off than a lot of them.  Sure, we don't have as 
many nice things, but we have enough - and, rather than spend our 
"disposable" income on new gizmos, we'll take off for a $299 week at a 
condo somewhere, taking our food with us and cooking, so that the condo 
rental and the gas there and back are the only added expenses.

It's called "living within your means", and it's not fun at times. 
People think that if they're successful at their job, then they 
shouldn't want for anything.  That's just not the way the world works, 
though.

>    Unfortunately, the rich have various opportunities that aren't 
> available to the poor. My bank account has a fee of about $5 per month 
> just for keeping it open. However, if I maintain a balance of $1000 or 
> higher throughout the month, the fee is waived. Someone who doesn't have 
> $1000 to just have lie around would be suffering an extra expense.

Banks have been screwy like that forever, though - what happens if you 
write a check for more than you have in your account?  They charge more 
of the money you obviously don't have!  :)

> When 
> I trade stocks, I have a flat $29 fee per transaction. Let's say I spend 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> get less poor.

No, it's more important that you leave your money in the market where it 
can grow.  30% of your investment taken up in fees seems high to me as 
well, and I think that there are firms whose fees aren't so high.

>    That means that as I'm accumulating money for my children, richer 
> people are presumably accumulating money even faster than I am. The 
> ratio to which their children are richer than my children will be 
> greater than the ratio to which my rich peers were richer than me, thus 
> actually making life *harder* for my children, rather than easier.

I disagree with the premise in your first paragraph, so I disagree with 
this conclusion here.  If you manage to leave your children $100,000, 
but someone else leaves their children $10,000,000, are you telling me 
that you think you've done your children a disservice?

>    Perhaps if we were not allowed to leave any of our wealth to our 
> children, we could eliminate this problem.

Then who gets it?  It's *your* money, not the government's.  The 
government only has money as it takes it from its citizens.  (Ditto for 
corporations - but we won't bring "corporate taxes" into this...)

> (I've made some 
> simplifying assumptions here, such as eliminating racism, sexism, and 
> other forms of prejudice that might be barriers to these children).

The only people for whom racism and sexism are barriers are people who 
have a vested financial interest in the problem not being solved.  The 
bigotry of the 60s is long gone, and the people of this country have 
grown.  Now, the "civil rights" movement has moved on to frivolous 
things.  As Kate O'Bierne has said, "There's good money in being an 
aggrieved feminist."

My point is that these barriers (simplifying assumptions, just as you 
did - there are exceptions to every rule) are only used as excuses for 
failure by people who will not take the responsibility themselves, to do 
whatever they need to do to "make it happen".

(And if you think I want the government disbursing my life savings to a 
group of folks like that...)  :)
```

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EEM1g.769$Fd6.514@edtnps82>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90> <59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> for anything.  That's just not the way the world works, though.

    I don't think your example contradicts (or even addresses) my claim. 
What I was trying to say is that if the average family around me has 10^26 
dollars (that is the digit '1', followed by 26 '0's), then you can expect 
the average meal to cost around 2*10^20 dollars.

    With 10^6 (or a million) dollars, I wouldn't be able to afford that 
meal.

    Basically, I was saying when accumulating money to give to your child, 
you have to be beating inflation. You can't be beating inflation if the rate 
at which you're accumulating money is the same (or less) as the rate at 
which everyone else is accumulating money. So you have to somehow ensure not 
only that you are are accumulating money, but that no one else is (to keep 
inflation down).


>
>>    Unfortunately, the rich have various opportunities that aren't 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the money you obviously don't have!  :)

    Right. Banks give preferential treatment to their rich clients.

>
>> When I trade stocks, I have a flat $29 fee per transaction. Let's say I 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> well, and I think that there are firms whose fees aren't so high.

    I'm trading with TD Canada Trust, and they're charging a $29 flat fee 
(as opposed to a 30% fee) for stock trading. If you know of a cheaper firm, 
I'd be interested to know about it (so I can slowly move my accounts over). 
Note that I do want the service of buying individual stocks. When I buy 
mutual funds through TD, I pay a 1% fee instead of the $29 flat rate.

>
>>    That means that as I'm accumulating money for my children, richer 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> you think you've done your children a disservice?

    Let's everyone in my generation received $100 (including me) from 
inheritance. So we've all got an "equal" opportunity. From there, we all try 
to grow out money. Years later, as we're nearing death, if *everyone else* 
in my generation leaves their children $10'000'000, and I'm the only one who 
left my children $100'000, then I think my children will have a HARDER time 
than I did, rather than an easier one via the inflation argument mentioned 
earlier.

    I think money is a zero sum game. If someone is "winning", someone has 
to be "losing". And as mentioned earlier, it's easier for people who are 
already winning, to win even more. So unless you're in first place (i.e. the 
richest person in the world), you're going to be falling further and further 
behind at an ever increasing rate, with each successive generation of 
children being worse and worse off, unless we allow for an external referee 
to end the game, saying "okay, you won, now let's start the next match with 
everyone at a score of zero again".

>
>>    Perhaps if we were not allowed to leave any of our wealth to our 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> corporations - but we won't bring "corporate taxes" into this...)

    I don't know. I'm not claiming to have "a better way" than what we've 
got right now. I was just pointing out that in the long run, if you want 
life to be as easy for your descendants as possible, then disallowing 
inheritance is probably in your best interest (unless you happen to be the 
single richest person in the world, in which case you would favor 
inheritance).

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 52)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-20T08:40:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90> <59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET> <EEM1g.769$Fd6.514@edtnps82>`

```
On Thu, 20 Apr 2006 14:11:48 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>    I think money is a zero sum game. If someone is "winning", someone has 
>to be "losing". 

I don't think so.   It is so for players on an NFL team, but not in
the real world.    There is some merit in the Trickle Down theory,
despite the hay the Democrats made from it.   Move a factory into a
town where people are starving and the rich don't start missing meals
in order to feed the newly hired workers.   Everybody is better off.

Times change.   I know a guy who bought an expensive mansion built a
hundred years ago, and he had to figure out how to put a kitchen in
the same floor as the dining area - he can afford to spend millions on
a house, but can't afford servants.   In Agatha Christie's
autobiography, she mentions that as a child she didn't expect ever to
be rich enough to afford an automobile - or so poor that she couldn't
afford live-in servants.

But we have more stuff.    If "winning" is having more stuff than your
neighbor, then you're right - you either win or lose.    If "winning"
is having enough so you would be happy if your richer neighbor was
poor, then it's not a zero sum game.

Even when you compare with your neighbor, you find that it isn't
linear.   Your neighbor wins when they go on a vacation in New
Zealand.    But you win when you buy a new car.   Your other neighbor
wins when he builds a new porch.    We make different choices.

Even if we were all billionaires, we couldn't do even a tiny
percentage of the fun stuff we want to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-20T15:24:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XIN1g.782$Fd6.205@edtnps82>`
- **References:** `<4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90> <59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET> <EEM1g.769$Fd6.514@edtnps82> <rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 20 Apr 2006 14:11:48 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> in order to feed the newly hired workers.   Everybody is better off.

    When the rich build a factory, they are "losing" money. When they pay 
the workers, they are "losing" money. When they make money from the factory, 
someone else is paying that money, and that someone else is "losing" money. 
The idea is that money doesn't appear out of thin air. When person A is 
"winning" money, there must exist some person B who is losing the exact same 
amount of money.

    Even when the government decides to print new money, this causes all the 
other money of the same currency in the world to depreciate in value via 
inflation. So the government is "creating" money for itself, by decreasing 
the worth of all the other money, and putting that worth in the newly 
printed money.

    The "game of money" would be an exactly zero sum game if only products 
were sold (i.e. no services). There's a finite amount of resources on Earth 
(and even in the subset of the universe that we can actually physically 
reach), and that resource is worth something. We can't create something out 
of nothing, so we can never "create" more worth (again, assuming the 
inexistence of services for now). So whenever I give something to you in 
exchange for something else, the total amount of "worth" in the universe 
remains constant.

    When you add services into the equation, it gets a bit trickier. A 
deliciously cooked meal is more valuable to me than the individual 
ingredients that made up that meal. So I'm willing to pay more at a 
restaurant than I would for the same materials found in a grocery store.

    The part I'm unsure about (which is why I said I *think* money is a zero 
sum game), is whether or not we can create "worth" via these services, or if 
we're really just exchanging fixed resources now. For example, when the chef 
"creates" worth by cooking the meal, he's losing the resource known as 
"time". That's 20 minutes of his life that he'll never get back. In exchange 
for those 20 minutes, I've given him some money. But to get that money, I 
had to work at a job, which took some time which I'll never get back in *my* 
life. So I *think* money is a zero-sum game, but I'm not sure of it yet.

[...]
>
> But we have more stuff.    If "winning" is having more stuff than your
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> percentage of the fun stuff we want to do.

    Right, but I was measuring money (or more generally, financial worth), 
not happiness or emotional value.

    Obviously, money isn't everything, and the amount of happiness you have 
and the amount of money you have could be two completely unrelated values. I 
was just trying to show that if your goal is to maximize the finacial-ease 
of your descendants, you should probably be opposed to inheritance for the 
above reasons.

    - Oliver
```

```
>    The "game of money" would be an exactly zero sum game if only products 
>were sold (i.e. no services). 

When more new products are built than old products thrown out, it is
not a zero sum game.
```
