- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T10:55:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1008$4437dccf$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Holly wrote:
> Thank you for helping out a hopeless cause-
> We discussed Microfocus and that was what we were going to do until our
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> they surprise us by changing the platform.

This is the first time I've ever heard of someone converting VB to 
COBOL.  :)  I've personally done it the other way around.  In fact, we 
have a process that we use for ad-hoc updates to the database, where 
they key changes daily (and is specific to the database partition 
they're updating).  It used to not be specific to the database - and, 
the algorithm was widely known.

What the customer wanted me to do was change it up to be 
database-specific, and set up a process to e-mail the control keys for 
"today" and "tomorrow" to the Major Command database managers; then, 
they would be in charge of allowing these ad-hoc updates.  (This keeps 
people from continually "fixing" the database instead of reporting the 
broken programs so we can fix them.)  The web server that sends e-mails 
ran ASP (which is VBScript).  So, I had to mimic the COBOL algorithm in 
VB - that was a challenge.  :)

> So now we have two options, option 1 is to use the VB apps that are
> currently running in production, that we know work, or option 2 to use
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't be able to get it to work by May 1st.

Personally, I'd stick with the known-good app over the unknown app.  :) 
  (especially with the time crunch you're working under)

> It's sort of out of my control, I feel more comfortable with our
> existing VB apps, using .NET and VBScripting.  But my team lead, I
> think, wants to go with Microfocus cobol., which is fine too but
> wouldn't be my choice.

If they end up targeting a .NET environment, then the language becomes 
somewhat less important.  Microsoft has tools that will convert a VB 6 
app to VB.NET.  And, if there are going to be more apps built, I'd start 
getting smart on C#.  C# and VB.NET interoperability is very good, and 
the C# is enough like C++ that you'll end up being able to write and 
maintain it as well.

> Eventually they will decide and then let me know a couple days before
> the deadline what it is.  But as a programmer, not a systems analyst or
> network analyst, I have a bit of a learning curve, especially when I'm
> asking all of these questions and my team lead and manager aren't.  It
> scares me a bit.

That's natural.  But, if you know the basic programming concepts (data 
manipulation, decision structures, etc.), given a manual, you could 
probably pick up whatever language they throw at you.

Also, if you're interested in doing some stuff on your own, Mono 
(http://www.mono-project.com) is an open-source implementation of C#, 
that runs in the Microsoft .NET environment.
```

- **References:** `<1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <b1008$4437dccf$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
Thank you Daniel!  I know I got a bit sidetracked on the religion and
the politics and all, but I really do respect your beliefs.  And
especially when you have been so helpful to me on this.  I'm a bit
sleep deprived and stressed out.  I appreciate the tips and
encouragement, that is very nice of you.  I wish I could convert my
team lead to VB right now 'cos that's what a couple of us agree would
be the easiest.  But I think he is one of the anti-microsoft
contingent, for some reason he refuses to believe that VB is how we
should go.  For our apps, we are simply receiving data feeds,
reformatting some of them, adding them to an Oracle database (currently
DB2 which is going away) and extracting data based on criteria.  It's
very simple really.  So to him, I think, Cobol was what he knew.  And
thinking we were going to aix, he probably thought it would be quickest
to redo the programs in cobol and use microfocus (which we do have but
only for aix not windows).  Now, we have this Windows server agreed on,
so we are trying to campaign to keep the existing VB code for the
implementation and then we wouldn't be so bugged out.

Maybe I could use some prayers - I guess I gotta go to church tomorrow.
 Thank you again - you are a good guy!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T10:13:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144689212.732350.66220@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <b1008$4437dccf$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144517273.147549.143130@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <enrk325dm81j3p8ogjkgffgvmfkc9rhslh@4ax.com>`

```
Hey Howard.  I respect anyone's beliefs and their right to have them. I
want them also to respect mine, which I know is hard for some people to
do on both sides.  I know a lot of very good people who don't believe
the same things I do, they are very conservative around here and I
don't fault people for that.  We were raised in a semi-liberal
household (liberal for Tennessee anyway) where we could think for
ourselves.  And maybe some of my ideas aren't for everyone.  We
travelled a lot as a family, my dad was a pilot, and I got to see the
world and meet all kinds of people.  Not everybody has had that same
kind of experience so they are going by what they know.  Helping people
to see that there are other valid beliefs and that it all really boils
down to being a good person, doing no harm to others, and loving your
neighbor should be what it is all about.  I think that is what Jesus
did which is why I think Christianity is a beautiful religion.  It's
only when people use it to control other people or as law that it
starts to become something else.  So if they have the right motivation
to help their fellow human beings I respect that.
I may not agree with how they go about doing it, but I can still
understand the motivation why.
```

```
In article <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET>, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> writes:
> anderschwan@hotmail.com wrote:
> > What generally is easier, from COBOL, would it be fujitsu cobol, cobol
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL.

We do.  I don't know about what Fujitsu has, but no doubt there is
some information about that on their website.  I do know that our
emulation of mainframe environments like CICS is less intrusive
than theirs, which requires source code changes.

> (I've never used them, but I've heard others discuss them here, 
> and I've had them advertised to me - I think it was Micro Focus and 
> their .NET Express product.)

Actually, confusingly, it's "Micro Focus Net Express", which was
eventually joined by "Micro Focus Net Express with .NET".  We were
using the "Net Express" name before Microsoft introduced .NET (which
is, IMO, as dumb a moniker as they've coined yet, but inexplicably
Microsoft has yet to put me in charge of their product branding).

In May, Net Express officially is replaced by Micro Focus Studio,
which (on Windows) contains Net Express 5 and other things.
```

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sure, we as people feel that way - but, then again, we aren't God.

    By this argument, it doesn't matter what answer anyone gives to the 
question coming up, as whoever answers it is (probably) not God, and so 
their answer is "wrong".

> If you are the absolute, one and only Creator of the Universe, aren't you 
> entitled to demand respect if your uppity creations don't give it on their 
> own?

    I have no idea how one determines whether God, or anyone else to that 
matter, is "entitled" to something or not. I raised a child from birth. Am I 
"entitled" to undying servitude from that child as they reach adulthood? I 
gave my friend a $1000 gift. Am I "entitled" to receiving a similarly valued 
gift in return? My pizza arrived later than 30 minutes. Am I "entitled" to 
receiving it for free? etc.

    Ignoring omnipotence for now, one cannot "demand" respect anyway. One 
could demand an act which is intended to be symbolic of respect (e.g. 
demanding that I stand up), but short of mind control, one cannot demand 
respect itself (e.g. I could stand up, but resent the person who forced me 
to do so).

    God, being omnipotent, certainly has the *power* to demand respect. I 
don't know if He is entitled to respect. And if he actually does demand 
respect itself (as opposed to merely demanding an act symbolic of respect), 
does that invalidate free-will?

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T02:52:49+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49ovolFplfm9U6@individual.net>`

```
Bugger! Sorry about the blank post... I haven't done that for a while... 
Anyway, here's the real response...

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> own?

Well, that entirely depends on how important respect is to you. You might 
take the view that if your creations are getting uppity they have probably 
grown considerably, and this might be a Good Thing... Personally, I would 
expect that God requiring respect from Man is a bit like me requiring 
respect from a cockroach...and I didn't even create cockroaches :-)
>
>>> Back to Jesus - He was conceived by the Holy Spirit inside a virgin - 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK. I give you that one.

>>> I definitely agree on your last statement above.  Let me ask you this - 
>>> how is evangelical Christianity treated in New Zealand?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's the COBOL market like over there?  ;)

Almost dead.

I don't know of anybody here who is still writing COBOL, apart from Richard, 
and myself (occasionally, and purely for my own purposes now).

J2EE is red hot. Ironically enough, the place where I'm working is currently 
a Unisys (A series) site, that uses LINC to generate COBOL. It is likely 
that this will be moved to a server platform and the LINC will become ABS(?) 
and generate C#... There are a number of things under consideration at the 
moment, including implementing a major package written in J2EE and Web 
Based.

I am managing a team of developers and BAs who are using J2EE.
>
>>>  Are cities allowed to put up Christmas trees without the threat of 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> summer!)

Well, not for us. We often go to the beach on Xmas day, have a picnic at the 
beach, and eat Xmas dinner in the evening when things are a bit cooler. (Not 
everybody does this, of course, but there are quite a few who do.)
>
>>> Are Bible clubs allowed in public high schools, and afforded the same 
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sadly, reason does not often play a part in getting legislation changed... 
It comes down to the emotional responses of the electorate and what will get 
the most votes. I guess that's the downside of Democracy. It's still a 
better system than the alternatives. :-)

>> Believe it or not, but in a 'humane and civilized' society, these crimes 
>> simply would not occur.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't believe human nature is naturally depraved. Or even 'sinful'. I 
think we start with a blank sheet when we're born, and our life experiences 
determine to a large extent what we write on it. If you have loving parents 
and a loving family it is likely you will be a loving person. If you see 
your parents respecting each other, and their friends, you will learn to 
behave similarly.  (There is an expression in Liverpool which always amused 
me: "They don't lick it off the stones..." It's true; they don't.) Sadly, 
the converse is also true. The pressures and influences of modern living 
seduce people and screw their values up so they think that working overtime 
to have this year's model car is more important than spending time helping 
the kids with their homework. We get damaged by life. We learn the wrong 
lessons. People we trusted let us down. We find that others don't share our 
ideals and aspirations. All of this contributes towards aberrating our good 
sense, optimism, and decency. We start to behave badly.

And yet, there is a part of us that can rise above all of it. Even at rock 
bottom, people have turned their lives around. The human spirit transcends 
the bad stuff. I see people in every day life behaving with courage and 
gentility. People going the extra mile to see someone else gets a better 
deal,  people sacrificing their time and energy gladly to assist others, 
raising money for the many worthy causes that we can never hope to saturate, 
or just being a friendly shoulder for someone whose load has become too 
heavy.

People are basically good. Give them half a chance and they'll do a good act 
in preference to a bad one.

I'm not ashamed of being human, and if God actually exists, and chooses to 
judge me for using the intelligence He (presumably) gave me, to arrive at a 
world view that does not include Him, well, I'll cross that bridge when I 
come to it :-)

Pete.

<snipped remainder as we agree on it :-)>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T19:59:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49rt7cFq3k2vU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <49ovolFplfm9U6@individual.net> <1e67a$4437e0ad$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:1e67a$4437e0ad$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
<snip>

>> J2EE is red hot. Ironically enough, the place where I'm working is 
>> currently a Unisys (A series) site, that uses LINC to generate COBOL. It 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I had to smile when I read this...

I have spent the last couple of weeks calming down some management who have 
a bee in their bonnet about J2EE performance. Just when I think I can move 
on, Daniel asks the same question... :-)

You have to understand that the question was prompted in the first place 
purely by politics. The people asking have no idea what J2EE even stands for 
and have never programmed a computer in their lives. However, it is good for 
one's image to be seen as asking the 'hard' questions and it is especially 
good when you are trying to stop something new anyway...

J2EE, provided you wite it properly (just like any other language) will 
perform well.  Our system is using entity beans to store things on a 
database. There is no easily accessible data model, and looking at the 
physical database really tells you nothing. You give the data to a bean and 
it puts it away for you. The contract is that it will retrieve it for you 
whenever you want it. Because these beans contain certain generalised code, 
there may be some initialization that takes longer than writing directly to 
the DB would. The idea is a separation of the data layer from the business 
layer, but it comes at a price. However, this price is not outrageous (or 
even really dear, when you consider the advantages of this approach) and the 
system is still being developed.

I see no point in optimizing data access while things are still under 
development, but, due to political pressure that was becoming tiresome, I 
finally relented and did some optimization. It far exceeds the existing 
system in efficiency and that is using a DMS database which I understand is 
hierarchical (anyone from Unisys please correct me if my understanding here 
is wrong). The beans are accessing a DB2 relational DB.

I reported the results to "Concerned" who noted this was just one process. 
Yes, it was. But a major one. Not persuaded. His sources told him J2EE data 
access was poor. I asked which processes he was referring to. Oh, not in our 
system, in another system developed by someone else 4000 miles away. But it 
was J2EE so it must be the same, right?

I finally bought my freedom by promising to have the IBM laboratories do 
some testing and optimization (argument from Authority...works every time 
with people who are uninformed :-)) I also agreed that we will make 
performance review part of our RAD iterations of processes. (For all the 
good it will do... it is running on a local development server that bears no 
relationship whatsoever to the deployment environment. Just by doing some DB 
buffer tuning we improved the local performance by around 30%...) What bugs 
me about all this is that I have to comply with people who have no idea what 
the Hell they are talking  about and are being fed bullets as part of a 
political agenda. Never mind, it comes with the territory.

Bottom line: There is no more reason to have poor performance from Java than 
there is to have poor performance from anything else. Yes, in our particular 
case there are some overheads in data storage, but these are acceptable and 
can easily be compensated for by other measures.

Can I go now...? :-)

Pete
```

```
Thanks for digging through that yet again.  I know when we were looking 
at Oracle 9i and 9iAS (Application Server), they said that their JVM did 
JIT, but then remembered what it had done.  The only time the .class 
needed to be relinked what when the server was rebooting.  (Which would 
make it the only server I know of that may run its slowest right after 
its rebooted!)

since you've been so helpful.
```

```
A friend and former colleague has a requirement for a pc desktop
"dashboard" to monitor the progress of a large batch system. His users
do not wish to bother signing on to the mainframe and he has considered
using email but has rejected that option (for reason of maintenance,
unread messages and other reasons). Q: Is there a pc dashboard package
available which does not require logging on to a mainframe and does not
use CICS?

Ta.

Honest, it is not my homework!
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<LISTSERV%200603311027003712.03F1@BAMA.UA.EDU> <2RhXf.336804$vi2.1027@fe03.news.easynews.com> <FQQXf.22337$dy4.13649@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <44326B0F.63E4C7A5@alum.mit.edu> <e0tufh$acm2$1@masala.cc.uh.edu> <udv432l4g9ttt6srmqoerl685rlg5s5ep6@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 04 Apr 2006 16:08:54 +0200, Tim C. <tim.challenger@aon.at>
enlightened us:

>Following up to "N. Shamsundar" <shamsundar_at_@uh.edu> :
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Then I count myself lucky I don't live in the USA.  ;-)

There is no such thing as "Daylight Savings Time".  Where's the
savings?  You can't save daylight.  What are you going to do?  Put it
in a jar and use it later?  I don't think so.  Rather, DST really
means Daylight Shifting Time, because that is all one is really doing
with the bouncing of the clock 1 hour forward in the spring and 1 hour
back in the fall.

As for the poster who is glad he doesn't live in the USA because of
DST, it is also observed in 70 foreign countries -- see list at
http://webexhibits.org/daylightsaving/g.html

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


If blind people wear dark glasses, 
why don't deaf people wear earmuffs?
--George Carlin
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Daylight Savings Time [was: IBM Mainframes]

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Bob Lidral <l1dralspamba1t@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T15:29:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<4432C904.1040401@comcast.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<LISTSERV%200603311027003712.03F1@BAMA.UA.EDU> <2RhXf.336804$vi2.1027@fe03.news.easynews.com> <FQQXf.22337$dy4.13649@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <44326B0F.63E4C7A5@alum.mit.edu> <e0tufh$acm2$1@masala.cc.uh.edu> <udv432l4g9ttt6srmqoerl685rlg5s5ep6@4ax.com> <g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> [. . .]
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
In the 1970s, Richard Nixon tried some things to reduce energy
consumption in the US.  One was the 55 mph speed limit which has since
been repealed.

Another was the imposition of year-round Daylight Savings Time.  The
theory possibly being that we'd save on business lighting costs if a
larger percentage of each workday occurred during daylight hours.  This
failed, among other reasons, because school children would have had to
travel to school before sunrise during some parts of the winter and
there was a lot of backlash from parents.  Also, many of the energy
consumption concerns were related to gasoline and heating oil,
consumption of which would not be affected much by such a policy.

One of my favorite cartoons of that time had three panels, each showing
Nixon with a blanket and a pair of scissors.  The captions read "Today 
I'm going to show how to make a blanket one foot longer", "by cutting a 
foot off of one end", "and sewing it onto the other end".

Bob Lidral
lidral  at  alum  dot  mit  dot  edu
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Daylight Savings Time [was: IBM Mainframes]

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T13:40:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<8ni532t8ha8qlvql16ahclics64v230u7t@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<LISTSERV%200603311027003712.03F1@BAMA.UA.EDU> <2RhXf.336804$vi2.1027@fe03.news.easynews.com> <FQQXf.22337$dy4.13649@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <44326B0F.63E4C7A5@alum.mit.edu> <e0tufh$acm2$1@masala.cc.uh.edu> <udv432l4g9ttt6srmqoerl685rlg5s5ep6@4ax.com> <g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com> <4432C904.1040401@comcast.net>`

```
On Tue, 04 Apr 2006 15:29:08 -0400, Bob Lidral
<l1dralspamba1t@comcast.net> wrote:

>Another was the imposition of year-round Daylight Savings Time.  The
>theory possibly being that we'd save on business lighting costs if a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>consumption of which would not be affected much by such a policy.

The only way Daylight Savings Time works is because people don't
adjust to it in 6 months.   If it was all year round, then people and
organizations would simply change their schedules to what is most
comfortable to them - which would be the same as if there was no clock
adjustment.    Schools would have kids arriving after dawn.   Golfers
would arrange to finish work before dusk.

>One of my favorite cartoons of that time had three panels, each showing
>Nixon with a blanket and a pair of scissors.  The captions read "Today 
>I'm going to show how to make a blanket one foot longer", "by cutting a 
>foot off of one end", "and sewing it onto the other end".

That describes Daylight Savings Time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Daylight Savings Time [was: IBM Mainframes]

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T22:50:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<1144216251.262663.269310@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<8ni532t8ha8qlvql16ahclics64v230u7t@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<LISTSERV%200603311027003712.03F1@BAMA.UA.EDU> <2RhXf.336804$vi2.1027@fe03.news.easynews.com> <FQQXf.22337$dy4.13649@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <44326B0F.63E4C7A5@alum.mit.edu> <e0tufh$acm2$1@masala.cc.uh.edu> <udv432l4g9ttt6srmqoerl685rlg5s5ep6@4ax.com> <g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com> <4432C904.1040401@comcast.net> <8ni532t8ha8qlvql16ahclics64v230u7t@4ax.com>`

```
> The only way Daylight Savings Time works ...
>  Schools would have kids arriving after dawn.   Golfers
> would arrange to finish work before dusk.

Just revert to using sun dials. Noon is when the sun is overhead,
school and work starts 15degrees after sunrise and home time is 15
degrees before sunset.

Of course it makes it impossible to have a railway timetable which is
why time zones were established in the first place, and you'll be paid
the same for a long day in summer as for a short one in winter, but if
you want to revert to the 17th century then why not.

I am quite happy with being GMT+1300 in summer even if some programs
don't believe it is possible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Daylight Savings Time [was: IBM Mainframes]

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T08:41:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<mgl7321tsnkdmvpek2p9l7p1poku3f9ums@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<LISTSERV%200603311027003712.03F1@BAMA.UA.EDU> <2RhXf.336804$vi2.1027@fe03.news.easynews.com> <FQQXf.22337$dy4.13649@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <44326B0F.63E4C7A5@alum.mit.edu> <e0tufh$acm2$1@masala.cc.uh.edu> <udv432l4g9ttt6srmqoerl685rlg5s5ep6@4ax.com> <g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com> <4432C904.1040401@comcast.net> <8ni532t8ha8qlvql16ahclics64v230u7t@4ax.com> <1144216251.262663.269310@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 4 Apr 2006 22:50:51 -0700, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Of course it makes it impossible to have a railway timetable which is
>why time zones were established in the first place, and you'll be paid
>the same for a long day in summer as for a short one in winter, but if
>you want to revert to the 17th century then why not.

If you want to be practical, do what they do in China.   Have one time
zone for the whole country and stick to it.

In these days of ESPN, many people are converting to this anyway - the
game they want to watch is listed in Eastern Time.    I'm at work
before 6:30 someone else arrives at 9:30 in my work place.   This was
real useful when I worked in a place with customers in different parts
of the country.    But it illustrates - we don't need to all agree in
what time is "natural" to do things.

I've seen people miss meetings after Outlook translated their time to
local before flying into their boss' time zone.   A single time zone
would eliminate these mistakes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Daylight Savings Time [was: IBM Mainframes]

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T10:40:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<nds732pebm7bc5dsbb0bvufnkv1b3o4t3j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<44326B0F.63E4C7A5@alum.mit.edu> <e0tufh$acm2$1@masala.cc.uh.edu> <udv432l4g9ttt6srmqoerl685rlg5s5ep6@4ax.com> <g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com> <4432C904.1040401@comcast.net> <8ni532t8ha8qlvql16ahclics64v230u7t@4ax.com> <1144216251.262663.269310@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <mgl7321tsnkdmvpek2p9l7p1poku3f9ums@4ax.com> <r1n732tdcaas21v8tq1qeemfsmdb0bp6r2@4ax.com> <50q7329h1q44m3k7no2dms70g3imd9ci3k@4ax.com> <nvq732lanl552h6c2inflspie5bons1nbe@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 05 Apr 2006 18:21:50 +0200, Tim C. <tim.challenger@aon.at>
wrote:

>>>>   I've seen people miss meetings after Outlook translated their time to
>>>> local before flying into their boss' time zone. 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>What were you thinking? Legislation? 

Time zones are legislated, getting rid of some of the problems that
result in people having clocks set according to personal whim, or by
exact longitude.    The transportation age made time zones important.
In fact, the universal time concept became important to help ocean
navigation, even before railroad schedules.

A single time zone for the country does a better job of getting rid of
these problems.

A single time zone for the planet does an even better job.

The information age is making a universal time zone important.
```

```
The military solves this problem by using Z time (UTC, nee Greenwich). 
Thus all (U.S.) military units throughout the world are on the same page.
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** mojaveg@mojaveg.iwvisp.com (Everett M. Greene)
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T08:24:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<20060405.7A76518.7EF9@mojaveg.iwvisp.com>`
- **References:** `<LISTSERV%200603311027003712.03F1@BAMA.UA.EDU> <2RhXf.336804$vi2.1027@fe03.news.easynews.com> <FQQXf.22337$dy4.13649@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <44326B0F.63E4C7A5@alum.mit.edu> <e0tufh$acm2$1@masala.cc.uh.edu> <udv432l4g9ttt6srmqoerl685rlg5s5ep6@4ax.com> <g6353294kam0hfnncmjg1n8srnv7h44bld@4ax.com> <4432C904.1040401@comcast.net> <8ni532t8ha8qlvql16ahclics64v230u7t@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> Bob Lidral <l1dralspamba1t@comcast.net> wrote:
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> That describes Daylight Savings Time.

In the desert areas, DST puts one at work during the cooler
parts of the day and gets you home at the hottest time of
day.  You have to wait until after sundown to do anything
outside in the evening by which time it's getting to be
late on funny time.

I see that the Feds in their vast wisdom forced Arizona
to go on DST this year.
```

```
In article <4960bgFmtancU1@individual.net>, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> writes:
> 
> It looks like Ruby has, as well as unnamed blocks, some sort of "closure"
> functionality.  I'm not sure I have a good grasp of it, but I just found an
> interesting article that discusses it.  You might like to take a look.
> http://www.artima.com/intv/closures.html 

Thanks for the link.  In fact, that piece suggests that Ruby has full-
blown continuations (which let you capture the whole state of a
computation and then resume it at a later time, possibly in a
different location in the process), not just closures, which would be
nice.
```

```
In article <AwoYf.391$Es3.354@newsread3.news.atl.earthlink.net>, Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> writes:
> Michael Wojcik wrote:
> > 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I've never heard of!  :-)

That's OK.  I studied closures and continuations and all that stuff
in college, but I run across computing concepts I've never heard of
before pretty much every day on the OCaml newsgroup.

There's a short introduction to continuations at:

   http://www.madore.org/~david/computers/callcc.html

Basically, a continuation lets you save the state of what you're
doing at the moment, and then resume it later on.
```

```
inqusite wrote:

>

> anybody please inform me the difference between Unisys cobol and IBM
> cobol.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Cobol and Unisys Cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T14:47:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KFvYf.64310$Jd.9026@newssvr25.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<1144142452.933543.203420@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7e2dc$44325f12$45491d7a$13760@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144161653.878930.43200@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
There may be some minor variations in the SELECTs and possibly other parts
of the ENVIRONMENT DIVISION;  and maybe in the the FILE SECTION of the DATA
DIVISION, but the PROCEDURE DIVISION code should be a cut-and-paste job.

But when you think about it, that's why standard languages such as COBOL
exist, no?

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Cobol and Unisys Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T08:37:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<580532pq7jeqci7ab4l5cns39jhdp2nsq3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1144142452.933543.203420@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 4 Apr 2006 02:20:52 -0700, "inqusite" <indypoli@gmail.com> wrote:

>I am new to this industry. I have started working on Unisys, can
>anybody please inform me the difference between Unisys cobol and IBM
>cobol.

JCL is a bigger issue, and file types.

I've used CoBOL with IBM mainframes since 1969, Burroughs, Univac,
DEC, Honeywell and PCs.    The Select/Assign statements change a bit,
VSAM file status checking is different.   If you use SQL, that's not
really CoBOL but it can be different.

At one point, my resume got stuck in personnel because I didn't have
CoBOL II experience.   Well my VAX CoBOL that I had been working with
was the same ANSI version of CoBOL.
```

```
I might get flamed for this...I have been a COBOL Programmer for 20
years.  I would not recommend getting into this field.  Choose a more
popular language.  The systems I worked on for the most part are being
replaced by SAP R/3.  See if you can learn ABAP language.  My manager
asked me if I know Perl.  I'm trying to learn it...
best wishes,
Chris "flame bait"
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting started

- **From:** anderschwan@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T08:24:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144423464.874296.195540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1144276096.670093.277350@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
There are entry level jobs in COBOL, but you will have to look at the
bigger companies.  I got into mainframe technology in the Y2K good ole
days, when they would sit a warm body in a chair and call you a
programmer.  We went to a cobol "boot camp" and 3 months later I was a
consultant doing exciting stuff like changing the mm/dd/yy to
mm/dd/ccyy.  It was so glamourous!
If you really want to work Old Skool, check industries like Healthcare,
Insurance, Banking, etc.  Call their HR departments or email and ask if
they have a trainee program (we have one here at our company if you
like to work for peanuts) but once you get some actual work experience
you can move on from there.  You could talk to consulting companies if
you can stand it - but usually they aren't going to talk to you unless
you already have a job, and don't want another, from my experience.
Cobol programming is not at all "hot" or "glamourous" but it is stable
and in my humble opinion is not going away any time soon.  I've worked
at companies where they tried to get off the mainframe, spent millions
of $ and found out it doesn't work when they are overbudget and way
past their deadline.  For large amounts of data processing, it is hard
to beat the processing power of a mainframe (maybe a unix system but
probably not Windows)
You also want to make sure you have some other skills besides mainframe
apps.  To be able to use existing legacy code and either convert it to
languages that can be used on a windows or unix server is a plus.  Then
you can market yourself as a "legacy migration consultant" and make big
bucks promising to get off the mainframe, which probably won't work
anyway.  In Healthcare knowing stuff like Hipaa transactions, edi, aix,
scripting helps too.
People can use cobol in combination with client server technology.
They just don't want to use it, or like it, because Cobol isn't cool.
But, if you have experience in it, and are pretty young, then you may
find that it is useful after all the older dudes start to retire, which
is starting now, and the new college grads don't know it because
everyone thinks it will be obsolete.  Guess what - in corporate
america, especially in the backwaters outside of the west coast like in
the south, they are VERY slow to adopting new technologies because it
takes money, skills, and some understanding on management's part (which
is a rarity).
```

- **References:** `<1144276096.670093.277350@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <e16131$1q7$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144424814.144458.304550@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1144425485.077187.281490@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <e1637o$hbr$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
Spooky!

So I'm not the only Liberal Arts Cobol programmer - what a coinkydink!
Are you my long lost brother?

Until one of my get rich quick schemes pays off, I'll be doing this.  I
am getting used to cubicle beige and corporate dog tags, but it would
be so great to do something else.  I almost considered teaching -
that's how bad it's gotten!  I tried hypnosis like in office space but
it just didn't work.  And antidepressants are too expensive.

My long term vision is to escape this office someday with my team lead
from Bangalore, once he starts his own company he could hire me.  When
he's started the biz I'll move to India where I can afford to live
after our country is bankrupt and where all the jobs will be anyway
unless Lou Dobbs has his way, and eventually learn to like curry.
It sounds I'm being flippant but I've actually thought it would be a
fun possibility.  There are 3 guys from India who work on our 5 person
team (I represent the Southern redneckian quotient) and we've thought
it was a good idea.  They already have friends who have started their
own companies back home.

Holly
```

#### ↳ Re: conflicting variables names on differents records.

- **From:** "Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T12:58:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144353508.544473.273660@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<44355b11$0$31480$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<44355b11$0$31480$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
Hello

It is always helpful to specify the compiler version (including the
supplier's name) and operating system that you are using.  If nothing
else, it may enable us to post links to relevant manuals when we don't
know the answers ourselves.  Bill Klein's FAQ, which was posted just
before your message has a good set of links, which probably include the
supplier of your compiler.

It would also help to post the contents of the Environment Division so
we can see the SELECT statements for the files.

I have never seen the VALUE OF clause of the FD statement in use (can't
even remember if there is one in the standard), it looks as though it
is there to do what a SELECT statement should do, but I haven't got and
don't know the relevant manual for your compiler.

You should check whether your compiler supports free-format coding
lines or requires fixed format.  If fixed format only, then the
starting column of each statement must be within the appropriate
margin, e.g. the 01 of an 01 level must start in columns 8-11 and the
02 of an 02 level must start in column 12 or greater up to 70/71.  Your
manual will explain which statements must start where.

For condition names i.e. 88 level items, the PIC clause is not allowed
and is not relevant,  since the reference is to the data name to which
it belongs in this case the condition names OK and KO belong to the
data name FLG.

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: conflicting variables names on differents records.

- **From:** "gianluigi beuzard" <gbeuzard@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T13:04:47+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4436474f$0$30253$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<44355b11$0$31480$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
Voila, I forgot to specify the source field in the select statement ... Now 
it works.
-----------------------------------------------
           SELECT FLIVRE ASSIGN TO DISK
                  ORGANIZATION INDEXED;
                  ACCESS MODE DYNAMIC;
                  RECORD COD OF ENR-LIV; <<
                  ALTERNATE RECORD TITRE OF ENR-LIV; <<
                  ALTERNATE RECORD AUTHEUR OF ENR-LIV DUPLICATES; <<
                  ALTERNATE RECORD THEME OF ENR-LIV DUPLICATES; <<
                  STATUS SLIV.
```

```
PIC's descriptions...
I use an old microsoft COBOL v2.20 compiler under XP, the  but I do not have 
the manual. The margin rules are followed so the error is else.

Here's the environment...

      ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
         SOURCE-COMPUTER. CPU1.
         OBJECT-COMPUTER. CPU1.
         SPECIAL-NAMES.
      *    DECIMAL-POINTS IS COMA.
           CURRENCY SIGN IS 'E'.

        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
         FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT FLIVRE ASSIGN TO DISK
                  ORGANIZATION INDEXED;
                  ACCESS MODE DYNAMIC;
                  RECORD CODE;
                  ALTERNATE RECORD TITRE;
                  ALTERNATE RECORD AUTHEUR DUPLICATES;
                  ALTERNATE RECORD THEME DUPLICATES;
                  STATUS SLIV.

           SELECT FTHEME ASSIGN TO DISK
                  ORGANIZATION RELATIVE;
                  ACCESS MODE DYNAMIC;
                  RELATIVE CLE-THE;
                  STATUS STHE.
```

```
Hi, I use Fujitsu v7 with Embedded SQL but when I write the command "DROP
TABLE" and "CREATE TABLE" return a error in compilation like this:
SQL STATEMENT IS INVALID - ODBC 7600E NOT EXECUTABLE SQL STATEMENT

Someone can helpme?

Thanks
Roberto
```

#### ↳ Re: SQL Commands

- **From:** "void * clvrmnky()" <clvrmnky.invalid@hotmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T12:19:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<siwZf.17427$43.324@nnrp.ca.mci.com!nnrp1.uunet.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4436367c$0$36928$4fafbaef@reader3.news.tin.it>`
- **References:** `<4436367c$0$36928$4fafbaef@reader3.news.tin.it>`

```
Servizio Tecnico wrote:
> Hi, I use Fujitsu v7 with Embedded SQL but when I write the command "DROP
> TABLE" and "CREATE TABLE" return a error in compilation like this:
> SQL STATEMENT IS INVALID - ODBC 7600E NOT EXECUTABLE SQL STATEMENT
> 
"CREATE TABLE" is not a valid SQL statement.  Are you creating a proper 
statement as defined by the SQL language?  You need a table name, at 
least.  Embedded SQL may have its own constraints and requirements for 
the CREATE keyword, as well.
```

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the CREATE keyword, as well.
"Created table ... " IS a valid SQL statement.

Problem with ESQL from Fujitsu is that THEY have restricted the SQL
statements allowed.

Solutions, as previously stated within this group (and that would show
up if the OP have bothered to search the group postings), can pass
through the use of

1- Dynamic SQL
2- Use of ADO CODE (not the ADO control supplied by Fujitsu (DO NOT
USE IT!!)) (or eventually DAO/RDO)




Frederico Fonseca
```

#### ↳ Re: SQL Commands

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T19:23:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0bf0$44370268$45491d7a$6510@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4436367c$0$36928$4fafbaef@reader3.news.tin.it>`
- **References:** `<4436367c$0$36928$4fafbaef@reader3.news.tin.it>`

```
Servizio Tecnico wrote:
> Hi, I use Fujitsu v7 with Embedded SQL but when I write the command "DROP
> TABLE" and "CREATE TABLE" return a error in compilation like this:
> SQL STATEMENT IS INVALID - ODBC 7600E NOT EXECUTABLE SQL STATEMENT

Notice the word "executable" statement.  There are two types of SQL 
statements - CREATE and DROP are both considered Data Definition 
Language (DDL) statements.  My guess would be that FJ7 doesn't like DDL 
through ESQL.  You may be able to get around this by using dynamic SQL - 
check your manual for that term if you're not familiar with it.  :)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SQL Commands

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T14:43:04+00:00

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:d0bf0$44370268$45491d7a$6510@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Servizio Tecnico wrote:
> > Hi, I use Fujitsu v7 with Embedded SQL but when I write the command
"DROP
> > TABLE" and "CREATE TABLE" return a error in compilation like this:
> > SQL STATEMENT IS INVALID - ODBC 7600E NOT EXECUTABLE SQL STATEMENT
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> check your manual for that term if you're not familiar with it.  :)

I've used CREATE *TEMPORARY* TABLE via ESQL... not with FJ but with IBM M/F
and some 'nix COBOL (MF?) I worked on.. maybe that's a solution ...

Then again, I still use CREATE TABLE thru the Windows ODBC API and that
works just fine.

Then again, maybe all this speculation is a waste of time until we see what
happens using a syntactically correct CREATE TABLE statement.... after all,
the ESQL compiler has to call the DBMS's PREPARE statement during
compilation, and that will surely fail if the statement is syntactically
incorrect.

MCM
```

- **Message-ID:** `<49r7vrFpgg5dU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4436367c$0$36928$4fafbaef@reader3.news.tin.it> <d0bf0$44370268$45491d7a$6510@KNOLOGY.NET> <YZPZf.501$Lm5.30@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <jclf32h58f7qn008k25c28kh1f7cis53qv@4ax.com>`

```

"Frederico Fonseca" <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote in message 
news:jclf32h58f7qn008k25c28kh1f7cis53qv@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 08 Apr 2006 14:43:04 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
> <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 I really HATE it when someone says "It's the ONLY way..." :-)

My experience (and it is extensive) is that when IT people say this, what 
they really mean, is: "It's the ONLY way I can think of or know of, and if 
there are other alternatives I can't be bothered exploring them..."

I have seen it many times throughout my career and  almost invariably, a 
little imagination or "thinking outside the square" provides a (usually 
better...) alternative.

I remember one famous case in Spain where I wanted to refresh multiple 3270 
screens when certain events occurred. IBM (Spain's) position on it was that 
it could not be done because conversational mode required the user to press 
ENTER. End of story. It can't be done. Three weeks later I demonstrated a 
number of 3270 screens all being simultaneously refreshed without user 
intervention. IBM Spain came along and saw it. "You could not have done this 
using standard code; you must have modified our system routines and we won't 
support it."  I assured them I had done no such thing and that everything 
was standard. It just required some lateral thinking. When I finally 
revealed how it was done (and I teased them for a while just for the fun of 
it :-)), the memorable comment was: "Oh Well, if you do it like THAT, of 
course it is possible..."

Frederico is absolutely correct when he says that the preferred way to 
manipulate DDL in Fujitsu COBOL is by dynamic SQL, and Fujitsu do not 
support ESQL CREATE.

However, dynamic SQL  is NOT "the ONLY way".

You can get an even richer experience if you simply create an ADOX object in 
your COBOL, and utilise the methods, properties, and events provided by 
MicroSoft. This will allow full acces to ALL DDL statements (NOT just CREATE 
TABLE) and it also allows cube access as well as flat tables.

And it is not limited to Fujitsu COBOL;  it will work with any COBOL that 
supports ActiveX or COM objects.

Pete.
```

```
pre-compiler supplied by FJ, as other venders will do things
differently.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL Commands

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-11T00:39:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49v1vsFpgqmdU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4436367c$0$36928$4fafbaef@reader3.news.tin.it> <d0bf0$44370268$45491d7a$6510@KNOLOGY.NET> <YZPZf.501$Lm5.30@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <jclf32h58f7qn008k25c28kh1f7cis53qv@4ax.com> <49r7vrFpgg5dU1@individual.net> <jllh32dusg0g8pnfoa59m2st6ckpsfe4l8@4ax.com>`

```
 

"Frederico Fonseca" <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote in message 
news:jllh32dusg0g8pnfoa59m2st6ckpsfe4l8@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 9 Apr 2006 13:57:09 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sure... no problem :-)

Here's some code that meets the requirements you stated above:

...
EXEC SQL
    BEGIN DECLARE SECTION
END-EXEC
01 TableName PIC x(30)
EXEC SQL
    END DECLARE SECTION
END-EXEC

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

EXEC SQL
  CONNECT TO DEFAULT
END-EXEC

MOVE "Whatever" to TableName
EXEC SQL
   CALL CreateTable (:TableName)
END-EXEC

EXEC
    SQL COMMIT
END-EXEC

EXEC SQL
   DISCONNECT DEFAULT
END-EXEC
...

The above will work for any RDBMS that supports stored procedures, and where 
you have stored a general procedure that creates any table known to the 
database, given the table name. (A series of selectable CREATE TABLE 
statements. They might just as well be DROP or ALTER; it really doesn't 
matter...)

It is ESQL. And it works on Fujitsu V7.

The point I am trying to make is that just because the manual says you can't 
use ESQL to write DDL statements, doesn't mean that you 'can't'. It simply 
means the syntax for those statements is not directly supported. I think 
Michael was expressing the same idea in his mail to this thread.

And, BTW, my observations were not intended to be personal and I wouldn't 
suggest that you personally didn't bother to explore solutions. I have seen 
some of your posts in the Tek forum and I know you are giving good advice. 
(In fact I refrained from posting there because I saw you had it covered on 
a couple of occasions :-))

But all of us should be careful to qualify the statement "It's the ONLY 
way..." [I can think of...] or, better still, just don't use it... :-)

Pete.
```

```
read anything that is not related to the forum's stated purpose. (Yet,
somehow, I have a feeling you still would... right? :-))

There are a number of bright minds who come here and over the years I have
found the off topic postings much more interesting than the on topic ones.
I've learned heaps of stuff here and been stimulated to investigate things
further that may or may not be COBOL related.  Above all, it is FUN! How is
it bad?

Pete.
```

```
Hey Pete.  I thought this in google groups may help if you haven't
tried it already -

http://groups.google.com/group/alt.usage.english/browse_frm/thread/1e72eb80d02e8ed2/1a24794869916641?lnk=st&q=outlook+header+too+long+server+rejects+post&rnum=1&hl=en#1a24794869916641

one of their 'fixes' to edit the header -

Drag the message to which you want to reply onto the desktop,
right-click it
there and open with Notepad.  Edit the headers _ad libitum_ and
save/close.
Double-click it, and it's open again for you to make your response in
the
normal way.  Delete the file left on the desktop whenever convenient.

Why it happened I don't think it is clear, I found some posts
mentioning that
Outlook has some problem trimming the reference that are a certain # of
chars long because it retains them all -
or there is a wrapping issue with certain message id's.
That is a real weird one and I certainly am no winders expert (that's
how we say it in Tennessee).
```

## Thread: END-IF — 3 this month / 85 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2005-06](2005-06.md#t-c74a1b7822)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Future of programming was Re: END-IF

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T21:07:02-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n18j329v8hbqlbbp8vnknt9kmhkol6m1ub@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<42a6ad66$1_1@news.bluewin.ch> <1118261822.381824.286780@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d87kn8$7kt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3gpg4nFda52oU1@individual.net> <d886ue$hln$1@panix5.panix.com> <42A858FB.3437AB71@mb.sympatico.ca> <3gs5u8Fe58f3U1@individual.net>`

```
The following is top posted with history left below for anyone
interested.  

Along with Peter Dashwood, I am skeptical about the future of
procedural programming.  However, I am not that convinced that OO is
the main replacement.  I believe that institutions will either farm
out applications to service providers or go to packages such as SAP
and other enterprise packages.  Outside forces like stricter data
handling requirements and audit trail as exemplified by the
Sarbanes-Oxley law in the United States and various privacy laws are
going to drive this.  The expertise needed will not be so much coding
as it will be understanding and resolving conflicting business and
legal requirements.  Human factors specialists and documentors finally
will become important.  How many of us have sworn at major company
web-sites that may be technically good in terms of correctly
processing the data once we could figure out how to use them?
Companies will finally have to acknowledge that people have to be able
to figure out how the system works without reading the code.  My memos
were probably unintentional bafflement and frustration to at least
some of my users and they were lucky to get even some documentation.
This is because I hate to write and the reason my postings may be
somewhat clear to this audience is that you are fellow programmers.
Companies swung between having documentation the last and often
skipped step or saying users were responsible for documenting the
system.  Users also can't be expected to do good documentation because
they also were not hired for their ability to write technical
documents, let alone really validate systems.  

While I can't speak about the latest generations of packages, the ones
from the 1980's in all too many cases were drastically modified by
inline code and I think many companies don't want to repeat that
mistake.  The trap is that component integration in any field is a
tricky business.  Anyone who thinks that putting in a package even
with the latest OO techniques will lessen the amount of testing is a
candidate for the Nigerian million dollar account scams.  The basic
question that must be answered is does the system as configured handle
the business correctly?  That means that the data controlling the
function must be correct (tax tables, sales rules tables, etc.) and
all of the interactions are correct.  Scripts (this includes mainframe
JCL/OCL, utility control statements, startup parameters, as well as
perl, shell scripts, REXX and others) are code every bit in need of
testing as COBOL/PL1/C++/language du jour or du vendor code.  It must
be audited to make sure it passes government requirements as
implemented.  These include accessibility for blind or motion impaired
users (section 508 in the US and ISO regulations elsewhere which thank
heaven at least started out the same).  

Is OO major?  My answer is probably although I suspect that much of
the power has nothing to do with OO concept.  My guess is that it is
the repository and the better documentation of entities that gives the
biggest bang for the buck (yen, loonie, pound, etc.). The idea of a
repository which contains information about all entities (program
code, record descriptions, data base entities, business rules, etc.)
goes back to the early 1980's or before.  I know that I was discussing
the implications and looking at the idea in the SHARE Guide Language
Futures Task Force in the early 1980's and we were not the only ones
looking at this.  The idea of defined interfaces and guaranteeing of
expectation (is the called module's expectation that parameter two is
a 4 byte signed binary field the same as the caller's expectation) and
handling of differences which could be fail the bind or do a
transformation (numeric string to binary, etc.) is what makes the
difference.  

I write this as someone who should not be given the job of designing
the original system but rather be the one brought in to upgrade, fix
or tune the system.  I will truly understand OO when I have to work
with it to modify it be it at the script level, the module level or
where ever needed (this could be data tables in a relational data
base).      

On Fri, 10 Jun 2005 12:41:44 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[123 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> place, but it won't be what we think it is.

And all of that depends on the problem space.  For example, embedded 
systems folks writing all that code that runs in your fancy set-top box 
will obviously use different tools.  There is a good chance these people 
will never even know what SAP is, nor will they care.  They don't much 
care about OO, either.
```

<a id="t-5c91a9765d"></a>
## Thread: Book of Judas (was Re: Any comments ...) — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Book of Judas (was Re: Any comments ...)

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T08:56:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1dv8b$1rfo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
*New post to avoid the same trap Pete Dashwood ran into.

Holly wrote:

<<Didn't they just find the book of Judas was valid  ...>>

I've done a fair amount of study on old and new testament apocrypha and 
noncanonical writings.

Valid, as being genuinely from a very early period?  Yes, although it's a 
translation into Coptic (Egyptian using basically the Greek alphabet) from 
the original (most probably) Greek.   The copy, poorly preserved, has been 
radiocarbon-dated to 220-340AD.

Genuine, as in consistent with what we know about the contents of the 
previously-lost Gospel of Judas?  Well, hard to say.  What's preserved 
doesn't seem to contain the information Irenaeus, Bishop of Lyons, 
complained about in around 180AD, though it does include information 
Epiphanus of Salamis, Bishop of Cyprus, cited some two centuries later.  .

<<and should have been included so everyone's idea of Judas might have been 
misinterpreted for
centuries?>>

Aye, there's the rub.  I believe during the first few centuries of the 
Christian era the New Testament was evolving from various collections of 
writings into a single approved list.  I don't think it started out as an 
approved list.  The Gospel of Judas didn't make it.   It's not a "given" 
that it should have been.

There are a number others that were also excluded, and I think we could all 
be enlightened by some of them (in particular, for me, the epistle of 
Barnabas and the epistle of Clement of Rome to the Corinthians, which I 
believe are acknowledged by most scholars as genuine in the form we have 
them).  I'm not convinced about the Gospel of Judas.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Vince Coen" <VBCoenDespawn@btconnect.com> wrote in message 
news:1144711106@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn...
> Hello Chuck!
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The Gospel of  Aloysius the Cobbler  was translated from the original JOVIAL 
(Jules own version of the international algorithmic language) , through 
Pascal, into COBOL.  Unfortunately, scholars disagree on whether three of 
the full stops in the original translation were later changed to scope 
delimiters by COBOL coders who wanted to put their own stamp on it during 
the mid to latter 20th Century.

The preponderance of the evidence indicates that the purity of Aloysius' 
Algorithm (which was believed to involve merging any number of 80 column 
card decks onto magnetic tape) may have been compromised by the 
adulteration.

Apocryphal copies of the original have been offered on e-bay for 6 figure 
sums, but the figures were poorly formed and written in crayon, so no sales 
are known to have been transacted.

The details of the life of Aloysius the Cobbler are obscure (and subject to 
much conjecture in Assembler and PL/1 by various whisky Priests of 
Procedure) but he is believed to have decided that there was no money in 
making shoes by hand, as everyone was making sandals out of old car tyres, 
and far too stoned to be doing any serious walking anyway.

He therefore picked up an Assembler manual and decided there was more money 
in making computers do stuff. The only problem was that this Assembler 
programming was completely unintelligible to anyone with a  mere human 
brain, and that was what he was equipped with.

After a torrid love affair with a punch girl named Julie, he smoked a few 
joints, dropped some acid, and received the divine revelation that 
programming should be removed from the machine. JOVIAL was invented on the 
spot.  The subsequent refinements led to COBOL which was named after 
Aloysius' original calling  and the rest is history.

Any more questions?

Pete.
```

```
news:1akn32t8aapu1ooldse5lrn275f8hts1gf@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 12 Apr 2006 00:12:32 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> enlightened us:
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That would be a red axled parrot, surely? :-)

There is apochyrphal literature (maily epistles from Aloysius to the coders 
in Crete and the testers in Thebes) that does indicate he had a parrot...

On one occasion when looking for his painkillers he shouted in disgust: "The 
parrot's eat 'em all!"

A nearby Business Analyst reorded it from force of habit (apparently it is 
their wont to record things without understanding what they mean, and then 
convert this into a totally unintelligible spec.), and it was worked into an 
Executive Overview (the only bit that anyone ever reads), for  a new 
labelling system that was to be used by the famous drug company, Bayer of 
Byzantium. (After the Huns sacked Rome the Board of Bayer decided the safest 
place in Europe would be Germany, so they moved to Leverkusen.)

To this day we refer to painkillers as "paracetamol" an obvious derivation 
from  Aloysius' heartfelt cry...

Pete
```

## Thread: The "A" word (was RE: Any comments (The "A" Word ...)...) — 51 msgs

### The "A" word (was RE: Any comments (The "A" Word ...)...)

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T10:55:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1e67f$1vm7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
New post to avoid the "overflow" problem Peter Dashwood first mentioned.

Holly wrote:

<< ... I don't like abortion and think it is a terrible thing.  But I don't
want government telling me
what I can and can't do to my own body ... >>

and then, further down,

>> - they shouldn't vote to allow the
>> government to electrocute or inject people on death row (especially
>> when some of them MAY be innocent)

Daniel responded:

> YES!  I *LOVE* it when pro-abortion people say this.  Let's kill the
> innocent babies, and spare the guilty criminals.  :)  (You don't see that
> this is backwards?)

This looks a whole lot like tarring Holly with the "pro-abortion" label 
after her specific and explicit statements to the contrary.   Whether a 
person feels something is a good idea is completely orthogonal to whether 
they feel it should be legal or illegal.

Because a person does not agree that anyone who either performs or allow
themselves to be subjected to an abortion deserves to be charged with
capital murder -- or for that matter charged with any *crime* at all -- does
not make them "pro-abortion".  I think it's a moral question that lies
squarely with the participants and their Creator.

And in the particular specific case that Holly cited, I am not of the 
opinion that forcing a
fifteen-year-old incest victim to go through with the pregnancy to term is 
obviously and unconditionally the best possible answer for all parties.   I 
am not of the opinion that
counseling her on the advantages of raising the child herself in comparison
to giving it up for adoption is obviously appropriate, as alluded to in your
list of alternatives (apparently to be considered as having equal merit in
all instances) that should be presented to *anyone* seeking an abortion.

Given that in this instance (reading between the lines) it appears to me 
that the
*perpetrator* of this incest got off scot-free, making demands of what the
consequences need to be for the *victim* of that crime strikes me as a
"pro-incest" stance.  Is that a fair characterization?  If not, why is it
any less fair than "pro-abortion" as it has here apparently been applied to
Holly?

I think the *victim* of a violent crime, which I believe includes 
*statutory* rape (whether incest or otherwise) should *not* be legally 
*forced* to undergo any more suffering or physical affects of the crime than 
is possible.  In this case a minor child was victimized by a relative.  To 
force that minor child to carry the child to term is to *sentence* that 
minor child to nine months, if not a lifetime, of suffering.

When the "pro-life" stance includes the victims of rape (statutory or 
otherwise) and incest, it is indistinguishable to me from the attitude of 
those who think the world needs to know what a fundamentally horrible and 
despicable person Matthew Shepard was, and who defend the actions of those 
who murdered him as an understandable lapse in judgment, nothing more.

Similarly, I don't buy the premise that doctors who provide abortions 
deserve to be treated as murderers while those who have shot them down in 
cold blood have maybe overstepped the bounds of civility a bit, and ought to 
be told "Naughty! Naughty!".

It may be *possible* to counsel a victim of (statutory or otherwise) rape or 
incest to the point that that victim *might* be able to carry that pregnancy 
to term without horrendous long-lasting effects on her.  It might even be 
*possible* to counsel her to the point that she would be able to raise the 
child as a single mom.  But to presume that evey case in which such 
counseling does not reach the desired goal is an utter failure --  
particularly an utter failure *on the victim's part* -- is, in my opinion, a 
decidedly arrogant and self-righteous stance.

     -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: The "A" word (was RE: Any comments (The "A" Word ...)...)

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> New post to avoid the "overflow" problem Peter Dashwood first
> mentioned.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No one is executed for killing another human. Retribution is specifically 
not included in the rationale for codes of criminal procedure.

People are executed to deter others from killing.

Given that deterrence is the reason, it then makes no difference whether the 
person executed is the actual perpetrator of the original offense.
```

#### ↳ Re: The "A" word (was RE: Any comments (The "A" Word ...)...)

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T21:05:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5eb2c$443b0ecd$45491d7a$29040@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e1e67f$1vm7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<e1e67f$1vm7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> New post to avoid the "overflow" problem Peter Dashwood first mentioned.
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> they feel it should be legal or illegal.

But, as Mr. Brazee mentioned (even today) - if it's okay in *certain 
cases*, then all that's left it deciding what those cases are.  And, if 
you can murder an innocent child because it resulted from a crime, 
where's the moral high ground in not allowing its murder because it 
would degrade a teenager's high school experience?  Or because it would 
be inconvenient for the rising executive who just got a promotion?

I understand your experience with this issue, and I understand Holly's. 
  I have personally been close with someone who has gone through similar 
things, though a child did not result.  I don't believe the way I do 
without having thought them through.  Did you read the Kathleen Parker 
article I linked?  "Sometimes an accident is just an accident."

And, to sort of buttress my point above - last year in New York City, 
there were 74 abortions for every 100 live births.  That's 42.6% - and 
there's *no way* I'm believing that rape and incest are that bad in NYC. 
  (I thought the junior Senator from New York was the "safe, legal, and 
rare" gal.  Two out of three ain't bad, some would say...)  This is what 
we get with the laws as they currently stand.

Estimates I've read put the abortions performed because of R&I at less 
than 1%.  Even at 1%, in NYC that would be 1 R&I victim forced to carry 
a child they may not want to 135 innocent children murdered before they 
draw their first breath.  I may be the only contributor here that thinks 
that's a better deal than 74 babies dead for every 100 births - but it 
all comes down to the value placed on human life, and at what point.  If 
someone doesn't believe that those 74 are babies being murdered, I'm 
sure my position seems... oh, how did you put that...

[huge snip]

> a decidedly arrogant and self-righteous stance.

Yeah, that.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The "A" word (was RE: Any comments (The "A" Word ...)...)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-11T02:20:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RnE_f.52316$ot5.31839@fe09.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<e1e67f$1vm7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5eb2c$443b0ecd$45491d7a$29040@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:5eb2c$443b0ecd$45491d7a$29040@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>> New post to avoid the "overflow" problem Peter Dashwood first mentioned.
>>
<much snippage>
> But, as Mr. Brazee mentioned (even today) - if it's okay in *certain cases*, 
> then all that's left it deciding what those cases are.  And, if you can murder 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Daniel,
   Am I correct that you understand the question is "what is a human?"

I don't (personally) know of anyone who is for or against "capital punishment" 
who questions that criminals are "human".
(FYI, I am against capital punishment).

There are, however, LOTS of questions about when a "fetuts" becomes a "human 
being".  I understand your view (or think I do - that "human life" begins at 
conception).  I also understand the position that human life begins at birth - 
and the harder to define (but closer to my view) that "human life" begins when a 
fetus reaches a stage that it COULD survive outside the womb.

It is my strong belief that all those on the "pro-life" side believe that "human 
life" begins sometime before birth
    and
that those who are on the "pro-choice" side beleive that fetuses at stages 
allowed to be aborted are not "human beings".

I have never understood the purpose of discussions about abortion among those 
with different views of when human life begins.  I just don't see how they can 
ever progress productively.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The "A" word (was RE: Any comments (The "A" Word ...)...)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-11T07:26:31-06:00

- **References:** `<e1e67f$1vm7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5eb2c$443b0ecd$45491d7a$29040@KNOLOGY.NET> <RnE_f.52316$ot5.31839@fe09.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Tue, 11 Apr 2006 02:20:33 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>There are, however, LOTS of questions about when a "fetuts" becomes a "human 
>being".  I understand your view (or think I do - that "human life" begins at 
>conception).  I also understand the position that human life begins at birth - 
>and the harder to define (but closer to my view) that "human life" begins when a 
>fetus reaches a stage that it COULD survive outside the womb.

There is an argument with backing that says a fetus is a human being
but an embryo is not.   This matches the tradition of quickening, and
it is also when some human-like brain activity starts.

But I don't see this in the abortion debates.
```

```
LX-i wrote:
>
> But, as Mr. Brazee mentioned (even today) - if it's okay in *certain
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> would be inconvenient for the rising executive who just got a promotion?

There were about 74,000 abortions in Florida in 1981. These 74,000 were 
unable to vote in 2000. Presumably, most would have voted for Al Gore, 
descended, as they were, from a more "liberal" family environment. Gore lost 
Florida by 520 votes.

The pro-abortion crew is evidently "eating their seed corn" and this 
phenomena is so apparant, it has a name: "The Roe Effect."

But that's neither here nor there in the debate. The debate will not end and 
there will be no substantial changes to the abortion laws. Both sides are 
too heavily invested in the struggle (i.e., the "struggle" provides 
employment and life-goals to literally thousands, including politicians).
```
